---
title: "HOWTO: Latest Nvidia Drivers- on Dapper Drake"
date: 2006-03-03
forum: Multimedia &amp; Video
---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-03
Hi, I've ported my guide about the Nvidia Drivers to Dapper Drake on the USDF.
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper]("http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper")

Or on the Official Wiki:
[Latest Nvidia Dapper]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper")

The guide works [COLOR="Red"]ONLY[/COLOR] for driver 8756 and 8762 (Method 2)

I need your help in order to:

1) test if the methods work for you (I'm particularly keen to know if Method 2 works for everybody)

2) test which of the 2 Methods (Draugen's or Stormbringer's) to install 32 bit compatibility libraries works better for you 
(this is for Ubuntu 64bit users ONLY)

3) find typos or bad errors

4) gather suggestions to improve my guide

5) Test the script below (for automating Method 2) **NEW!**

Thanks in advance for your help (which I promise to give you back ;)  )

--------------------------------------------------------------------
[COLOR="Blue"]**Script to make Method 2 faster**[/COLOR]
[B]
NOTE:  this can be dangerous and buggy[/B]

This script will download the Nvidia installer (and all the files the installer needs) and set the driver for you.

Get to the following website:
[http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html](http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html)

And follow the instructions you will find there.

The scripts will install the Nvidia driver [COLOR="Red"]8774 and 7184[/COLOR]
[B][COLOR="Red"]
NOTE: the script currently REMOVES your RESTRICTED MODULES.[/COLOR][/B]
------------------------------------------------------------------

---

### Post by joffe on 2006-03-03

The procedure was identical to that in Slackware, only Ubuntu puts the options in another file.

Attached my options and xorg.conf files.

### Post by r4ik on 2006-03-04
Method 1 got me back my Xserver all it took was a,

sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx linux restricted-modules-2.6.15-16-k7

If you use a different kernel trace it just do a `uname -r` in a terminal and use youre own after "modules" 

sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg

And follow the steps.
To finish,

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart

Badly stolen from Tseliot (thanks again !)
Now i can test all i want without the "break X fear"

My specs.
AMD Duron 900
512 RAM
MSI MX4000-T128 (Nvidia chipset)

2) Try this:
cd path_to_the_installer
sudo sh Name_of_the_installer --uninstall

Then reinstall it again.

### Post by hamil on 2006-03-04
Followed your howto, and bumped into some troubles... :)

This is my specs:
An AMD Athlon64 machine, running Ubuntu Dapper i386
With MSI K8Neo motherboardcard
Graphic-card identified as "NV36 GeForce 5700 FX"
Uname -r gives this output: 2.6.15-16-386

I followed your Method 2
Downloaded 1.0-8178/NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run from the Nvidia page.

I am a huge fan of copy and paste, so the first is just a typo, in the explanation in paragraph 5 it should be this text: 
> sudo apt-get --purge remove linux-restricted-modules-386 :D

Followed the rest of your HowTo, but could not get it to work.

The installer gave errors:
> Error message nr 1
ERROR: UNable to create '/lib/modules/2.6.15.16-386/volatile/nvidia.ko' for copying (No such file or directory)"

Error/warning message nr 2
WARNING: Unable to restore file '/lib/modules/2.6.15.16-386/volatile/nvidia.ko'


The X-server would not start up againafter this, and I recieved these error messages from it:
> EE Failed to load module "glx" (a required submodule could not be loaded, 0)
EE Failed to load module "nvidia" (module does not exist, 0)
EE No drivers available


Attached is the xorg.conf file, if it is necessary.
Fatal server error
no screens found

Brgds
Lasse

Section "Module"

Section "Device"

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-05
[QUOTE=lithorus]No offense, but what's the point with this? Dapper already have latest Nvidia driver...[/QUOTE]
Dapper has the latest driver at least for now. When Dapper is stable and a new Nvidia driver is released it won't be in Dapper's repos. 

Method 2 will enable you to install the latest driver.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-05
[QUOTE=lnxconvrt]No luck.  With method 2, I can't even get x to start.

Messages about modules not loading when I look at the xserver output, the first seems to be something like "Couldn't open module GLCore".

I'm back at my original xorg.conf again.[/QUOTE]
Uninstall the driver you installed with the Nvidia installer:
```
sudo sh Name_of_the_installer --uninstall
```

It shouldn't need GLCore.

Use Method 1 and add manually the options in red:

```
Section "Module"
	[COLOR="Red"]Load	"GLcore"[/COLOR]
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	[COLOR="Red"]Load	"ddc"[/COLOR]
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection
```

Let me know if it works

---

### Post by qwer on 2006-03-05
I installed the drivers using method #1.  3d acceleration works, but text in firefox (also sometimes in other applications, but mainly here) often looks like this:


[[img]http://img158.imageshack.us/img158/7345/namenlos5vb.th.jpg[/img]](http://img158.imageshack.us/my.php?image=namenlos5vb.jpg)
[[img]http://img240.imageshack.us/img240/8471/namenlos6ay.th.jpg[/img]](http://img240.imageshack.us/my.php?image=namenlos6ay.jpg)
(the menu bar looks ok)

by selecting the text with the mouse, it looks as it should again ...

xorg.conf
```

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"
	# path to defoma fonts
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "i2c"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "de"
    Option         "XkbVariant" "nodeadkeys"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "Buttons" "6"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Standardbildschirm"
    DisplaySize     338    270
    HorizSync       28.0 - 64.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 60.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV35 [GeForce FX 5900XT]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV35 [GeForce FX 5900XT]"
    Monitor        "Standardbildschirm"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "RenderAccel" "true"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection


```

1) a firefox bug?
can you try:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade

2) Does firefox work fine if you go back to your old xorg.conf (it's all in my guide)?

3) Are you using Composite? If so can you disable it

### Post by hamil on 2006-03-05
[QUOTE=cantona]Method 2 same here and method 1 is most worst, it freezes my pc!
Geforce4 MX 460

kernel 2.6.15-17-amd64-k8[/QUOTE]

Further down on this HowTo, I can find this text:
> 4) If you have an AGP graphic card and your system freezes but you can still move the mouse pointer you will have to do this:

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Add the lines which begin with the word "Option" in this section of the file:

Section "Device"
Identifier "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6200 TurboCache]"
Driver "nvidia"
BusID "PCI:1:0:0"
Option "NvAGP" "0"
Option "RenderAccel" "Off"
Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off"
Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" “Off”
EndSection

Then save the file and exit. Press CTRL+ALT+Backspace in order to restart the xserver.

IF it doesn't work yet then you can add also the following line which will disable 3d acceleration:

Option "Accel" "Off"

If this doesn't work for you try asking on the Unofficial Nvidia Forum and you might be talking to some of the developers of the NVIDIA drivers (there's a Linux section) (it's very useful) " [http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/forumdisplay.php?f=14](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/forumdisplay.php?f=14) 

Have you tried this?

### Post by lnxconvrt on 2006-03-05
[QUOTE=tseliot]Uninstall the driver you installed with the Nvidia installer:
```
sudo sh Name_of_the_installer --uninstall
```

It shouldn't need GLCore.

Use Method 1 and add manually the options in red:

```
Section "Module"
	[COLOR="Red"]Load	"GLcore"[/COLOR]
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	[COLOR="Red"]Load	"ddc"[/COLOR]
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection
```

Let me know if it works[/QUOTE]

Uninstalled and went back to method 1.  The xorg.conf file generated by nvidia-xconfig already has 'Load "ddc"', and I added 'Load "GLcore"'.

Same result.  Went through the /var/log/Xorg.0.log file and found that it couldn't load GLcore.

However, with or without that line in the config file, I get this in the log:

(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to read EDID for display device CRT-0
(II) NVIDIA(0): Frequency information for CRT-0:
(II) NVIDIA(0):   HorizSync   : 28.000-33.000 kHz
(II) NVIDIA(0):   VertRefresh : 43.000-72.000 Hz
(II) NVIDIA(0):      (HorizSync from Conservative Defaults)
(II) NVIDIA(0):      (VertRefresh from Conservative Defaults)
(II) NVIDIA(0): PF790: Using hsync range of 28.00-33.00 kHz
(II) NVIDIA(0): PF790: Using vrefresh range of 43.00-72.00 Hz

Then many messages about various modes that it won't use that look like this:
(II) NVIDIA(0): Not using default mode "1024x768" (hsync out of range)

The VESA driver seems happy with them, and it's a Viewsonic PF790, which when I looked it up before supports 30-97 kHz for horizontal sync and 50-180 Hertz vertical.

The monitor section of my xorg.conf is very simple:
Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "PF790"
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Does it need to be changed so that the nvidia driver will accept the modes in my config?

---

### Post by lnxconvrt on 2006-03-06
Well, modifying xorg.conf as follows does allow me to get high resolution from the nvidia driver:

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "PF790"
[COLOR="Red"]    HorizSync      30-97
    VertRefresh    50-180[/COLOR]
[COLOR="Red"]#[/COLOR]    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Changes in red.

However, it actually put me in 1200 x 1600, I think, and put the gnome-panel back in the middle of the screen.  Worse, the mouse pointer entirely disappeared.

Too late to play with this any more tonight.  Maybe tomorrow I'll find further answers.

---

### Post by lnxconvrt on 2006-03-07
[QUOTE=tseliot]Follow this guide to set the exact resolution. Then try the driver again.
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=83973&highlight=resolution]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=83973&highlight=resolution")[/QUOTE]

Good guide, thanks for the pointer.  With some trial and error, I did manage to find a couple of modelines that let me use 1024 x 768 and 1280 x 1024, though the latter seems "shimmery" compared to 1280 x 1024 with the vesa driver.  I think that only the 1024 x 768 would be usable for very long, though I prefer the higher resolution.  I used the second of the 2 modeline generators with HZ settings agreeing to some preset timing modes listed in the Viewsonic PF790 manual.

I still have no mouse pointer when I use the nvidia driver.  I have my Gnome set to highlight the mouse location when I press Ctrl, but that doesn't work when I start x with the nvidia driver.  The mouse seems to be functional if I can just guess where the heck the pointer is.

I looked through the logs and the messages about the mouse seem the same either way, I just can't see the pointer.

So, perhaps some progress, but still not usable for me.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-07
[QUOTE=lnxconvrt]Good guide, thanks for the pointer.  With some trial and error, I did manage to find a couple of modelines that let me use 1024 x 768 and 1280 x 1024, though the latter seems "shimmery" compared to 1280 x 1024 with the vesa driver.  I think that only the 1024 x 768 would be usable for very long, though I prefer the higher resolution.  I used the second of the 2 modeline generators with HZ settings agreeing to some preset timing modes listed in the Viewsonic PF790 manual.

I still have no mouse pointer when I use the nvidia driver.  I have my Gnome set to highlight the mouse location when I press Ctrl, but that doesn't work when I start x with the nvidia driver.  The mouse seems to be functional if I can just guess where the heck the pointer is.

I looked through the logs and the messages about the mouse seem the same either way, I just can't see the pointer.

So, perhaps some progress, but still not usable for me.[/QUOTE]
About the mouse cursor:
add the following options to the Section Device of your xorg.conf:
```
Option "HWcursor" "Disabled"
Option "SWcursor" "Enabled"
```

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

I hope it helps

---

### Post by lnxconvrt on 2006-03-08
[QUOTE=tseliot]About the mouse cursor:
add the following options to the Section Device of your xorg.conf:
```
Option "HWcursor" "Disabled"
Option "SWcursor" "Enabled"
```

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

I hope it helps[/QUOTE]

That did it!  Thanks very much.

Curiously now things seem to work without the special modelines also.  A bit more shimmer than before at 1280 x 1024, but not intolerable, and this monitor at times has had that behavior.

Possibly some of the problems were from my mobo being fairly new and having limited Linux support (Asus with nforce 430 and onboard Nvidia Geforce 6150).  Another factor maybe my older (4-5 years?...bought it reconditioned) Viewsonic PF790 whose parameters weren't auto-detected.  It may be some of both.

In case it helps anyone I have compared the original nvidia-xconfig generated xorg.conf file with what is working for me now, and will attempt to summarize what I have currently working.

1)  Used method 1 from tseliot's how-to linked from page one of this thread.
2)  Made the following changes to the nvidia-xconfig generated xorg.conf file:
Changed the InputDevice section by adding the lines in red below - 

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
 [COLOR="Red"]   Option         "HWcursor" "Disabled"
    Option         "SWcursor" "Enabled"[/COLOR]
EndSection

In the Screen section I took out most of the subsections, and added just one Modes line with the two resolutions that I wanted, making the section as shown below -

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "Generic Video Card"
    Monitor        "PF790"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Finally, I changed the Monitor section from this:

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "PF790"
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

to this:

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "PF790"
    HorizSync      30-97
    VertRefresh    50-180
EndSection

Anyway, that's it for what it's worth to anyone else.  Thanks again, tseliot.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-09
[QUOTE=tseliot]About the mouse cursor:
add the following options to the Section Device of your xorg.conf:
```
Option "HWcursor" "Disabled"
Option "SWcursor" "Enabled"
```

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

I hope it helps[/QUOTE]
I've added this to the Problems section of the guide.

### Post by Mustard on 2006-03-09

It worked perfectly and solved the Direct Rendering issue (not enabled in the nvidia-glx version) I just tested Americas Army @ 1600x1200 UltraHigh and it ran smooth.  I do have a side question, how can you load the nvidia-settings settings on load, every time I log in I have to go through there and re-set everything.  Great Howto.[/QUOTE]

I'm on breezy so I don't know where to find the Sessions menu in Dapper, but normally you would add this to your session preferences under the 'Startup programs' tab.

```
nvidia-settings --load-config-only 
```

On Breezy, the sessions menu is in System>>Preferences>>Sessions. (Hopefully that give some indication of where it is on Dapper) :)

### Post by leech on 2006-03-09
That's probably because of the framebuffer issue.  I have it on my laptop as well.  Unless I pass vga=0x317 to the kernel options, upon shutting down the screen goes black, but it doesn't lock up, you just need to wait for it to shutdown/restart properly.  Just add a similar line to your /boot/grub/menu.lst after 'splash' on your kernel line and it should work on next boot up.

Leech

---

linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-17-**686-nvidia-legacy**.

( I have all dapper repos including universe and multiverse), and it doesn't think
there is such a thing.  Nor does it think there is a
linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-17-**386-nvidia-legacy**.

Is this a typo, should it really be **linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`**
in the command, or is there some other magic I need?

---

### Post by leech on 2006-03-09
Because there isn't a restricted modules specific for nvidia legacy modules.  Restricted modules contains ALL restricted modules (including the fglrx, nvidia, nvidia-legacy, some wireless ones, etc.)  You still need the glx packages because of the libraries that are in them.  So really all you need is the nvidia-glx-legacy package.

Leech

### Post by tonyr on 2006-03-10
I tried 7667 and Dapper Method 2.  kdm startup failed: Xorg.0.log said it could not load
GLcore as a sub module for glx, so glx wasn't installed.  Remembered comments
in this or other forums about libglx location and duplication, about GLcore shouldn't 
be needed, about moving drivers files and libglx.so.  Decided that the wrong libglx.so
was being used.

I think this road has been travelled before, by diebels maybe?

I copied the nvidia driver files (nvidia.o, nvidia.so) from /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/drivers
to /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers.

I copied libglx.so.1.0.7667 from /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/extensions to /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions, and linked it to libglx.so (with proper precautions).

I think maybe both of these could have been accomplished with the use of command line
options to the installer.

kdm started right up, 3D acceleration was there, Restart and Shutdown produced
exactly the same effect as the 8178 driver: black screen, system freeze.

Oh well...

- tony

### Post by klahjn on 2006-03-15
[QUOTE=eantoranz]Is there a solution yet for: Failed to load module "glx"
I'm using method 2. The installer first kind of complained because I had another installation of the nvidia driver (back from breezy), but It installed the new one without a hickup.
I can even load the module nvidia, but then X doesn't want to start.
In case you need more information (from the logs, maybe), tell me. :-)[/QUOTE]

Hopefully this helps.  I'm currently using an nvidia card, but one thing i would personally check just to make sure (i remember having problems with it) is that your /etc/X11/xorg.conf is configured properly.  Sometimes it does not install it properly for some reason, especially with the new X.org release 7.0 that is there.  Let me post what i have here, and hopefully it will help you.
This is my module section:
Section "Module"
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
#	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

And this section also.
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV31 [GeForce FX 5600XT]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
	Option		"RenderAccel"			"true"
	Option		"AllowGLXWithComposite"	"true"
EndSection
I believe i had to add the "RenderAccel" option to get it to work properly

```

An update to my problem.

I can confirm the Method 2 now **does** work for me.  The problem was that the NVIDIA installer was in a folder with an escaped space (~/My Downloads/NVIDIA).  See the bolded error.

After copying the installer to a new folder (~/NVIDIA) the installer is able to compile the drivers, without a hitch.

I'm almost 100% sure this wasn't a problem with Breezy, so perhaps it was to do with the patch.   Anyway don't put the NVIDIA installer in a folder with escaped spaces.

### Post by joffe on 2006-03-16
I used to have the not so funny green stuff show up on my VTs when I used slackware. It appears that the nvidia driver is incompatible with the normal vesafb frame buffer in higher resolutions. Patching the kernel with the [vesafb-tng]("http://dev.gentoo.org/~spock/projects/vesafb-tng/") did the trick for me (along with changing the boot line in grub/lilo).

I havent yet gotten used to roll my own kernel in Ubuntu so unfortunately I cannot give you any help how to fix it in dapper.

### Post by funkenstein on 2006-03-17
Yea, the final thing that ended up actually working, was to purge *everything*, dpkg-reconfigure the lot, clean up all the downloaded debs etc, and
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-kernel-common nvidia-glx
``` This was followed by editing my xorg.conf to change the driver it used from nv to nvidia. The restricted modules wree already there. Could it be that the nvidia-kernel-common are useful for something?

Now I got Xgl/Compiz working using [_THIS_]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=131267"), I get  glxgears score of over 8000, a slight improvement from before Xgl (5000fps)... But I think that's just because there's an overlay  :p

### Post by claas on 2006-03-20
Hello!

I was trying to install the NVIDIA drivers manually. But it did not work the way it's described in the howto. The problem was that the installer installed the files to /usr/X11R6/... and not to /usr/lib/xorg/...

This is easy to solve:

1) wget NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run
2) wget nvidia-dapper-patch.txt
3) sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run -x
4) cd NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1 
5) patch -p0 < ../nvidia-dapper-patch.txt
6) **ln -s /usr/lib/xorg/ /usr/lib/xorg/lib**
7) ./nvidia-installer -n **--x-prefix=/usr/lib/xorg/**
8 ) adopt your /etc/X11/xorg.conf to fit your needs

I wonder why the nvidia installer does not find the right destination for the drivers...

### Post by theturner on 2006-03-20
Method 1 is outdated regarding the legacy drivers. The 7174 kernel modules are now included in linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r` .
So you just have to install nvidia-glx-legacy and its dependencies (nvidia-kernel-common and linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`), and you're set. I think it does some symlink magic to load nvidia_legacy instead of nvidia.

---

### Post by trorion on 2006-03-20
Tried method 2 and borked.  Uninstall went fair.

To be honest the instructions are rather complicated for a poor dog like me.  I've installed on breezy before and got them running but this has too many jumps in it.

I'll give method 1 a shot; worked but no real love (not running opengl).

For instruction set 2 I think there are some problems.  I'm using dapper kernel and 8178 installer.  I'll walk through what I did.
```
METHOD 2

1) Download the installer from this page according to your architecture (**32bit** or 64bit) http://www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html

2) Download the patch for the installer (REQUIRED for kernels 2.6.15-x, i.e. fundamental to the kernels that come with Ubuntu by dafault):

http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/attachment.php?attachmentid=15794&d=1137942929

3) Open Terminal or Konsole and type:

sudo apt-get install build-essential gcc linux-headers-`uname -r`


4)

NOTE: if APT complains because it can't find any package to remove in steps 5-6 it's not a problem at all and you can go ahead with the next steps. If there were other errors you should report to me.

5) Then open Terminal or Konsole and type:

sudo apt-get --purge remove linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r` nvidia-glx nvidia-settings nvidia-kernel-common

It will ask you to remove also several files (among which linux-386, etc.): answer Yes.

6) remove the file manually:

sudo rm /etc/init.d/nvidia-*

7) Press CTRL-ALT-F1 (so as to get to the command line, not a windowed terminal, but out of the graphical interface GUI)

login with your username and password (if required)

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (OR "kdm stop" if you use KDE)

cd &#8220;directory_where_you_have_the_nvidia_installer&#8221;

NOTE: If and ONLY IF you use a Breezy kernel or a recompiled kernel lower than 2.6.15x have a look at point 14) in the PROBLEMS SECTION and skip step 11 right here below.

8 ) Now you need to patch the Nvidia installer.

Extract the content of the installer:

sh /path_to_the_installer/NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run --extract-only

A new folder will be created

cd NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1

Patch the file:

patch -p0 < /path_to_the_patch/NVIDIA_kernel-1.0-8178-U012206.diff.txt

Install the driver:

sudo ./nvidia-installer -n
```

This installer asks several steps.  One of them is 'do you want to run the auto config file?' to which I answered 'yes' then I moved on...

```
9) Make a backup of your xorg.conf

sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup

[In this way if anything goes wrong and you have problems with the xserver (i.e. if it doesn't start) you can type:

sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup /etc/X11/xorg.conf ]
```
I'm an old dog but it seems to me since I ran the configure tool in the installer i've just replaced my old xorg.conf_backup with the newly created xorg.conf file right?  So maybe you should put this step BEFORE you do the ./nvidia-installer -n OR you should tell people to NOT use the nvidia configuration tool.

```
NOTE: If you use the nvidia installer version 8174 or higher you can go straight to point 13.
```
So I go straight to point 13!
```
13) NOTE: If you use a Breezy kernel or a kernel lower than 2.6.15x have a look at point 14) in the Problems Section

sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-7667-pkg2.run

NOTE: the name of the installer may vary: e.g. it could be NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-7667-pkg1.run. Therefore just put the name of the installer you've downloaded from Nvidia website

At a certain point the installer will attempt to find a precompiled module for your kernel on the NVIDIA website. It won't find anything therefore it will ask you whether it should try to compile a module for your kernel: answer "Yes" to its question.

NOTE: If you have Ubuntu 64bit you can't install OpenGL32bit compatibility libraries, so when the installer asks whether to install it just answer no OR you may want to try 2 workarounds which Draugen and Stormbringer found but which I haven't tried myself (look at the PROBLEMS SECTION at the end of the guide: point 5) 
```

Since i'm currently in /home/name/Desktop/NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1/ this command will do nothing so I backed OUT of that file and ran this command.  Also, why am I doing this at all?  Didn't I already install this driver when I did ./nvidia-installer -n?

Anyway, startx gives me:
If I just run the "nvidia-installer -n"
- could not load 'nvidia' driver not found

if I run the NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-7667-pkg1.run
- No screens found.

### Post by codejunkie on 2006-03-21
i was finally able manually install the nvidia driver with the 2.6.16 kernel, and i figured id post the steps here incase they can help someone.

First download the nvidia installer, and follow the steps [**HERE**]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=62021") to patch the nvidia installer and repackage it into a custom .run package.

now make sure to remove all nvidia related packages installed by default in ubuntu by running this in a terminal.
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia*
```

kill the xserver by pressing ctrl+alt+F1 login and enter
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
```
cd to the directory containing the custom NVIDIA .run package you created and run
```

sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg0-custom.run --x-prefix=/usr/lib/xorg/

```
then 
```
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules
```
now load the nvidia module with
```
sudo modprobe nvidia
```
and run ```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
change the "Driver" line in the "Device" to **"nvidia"**
and restart the xserver with 
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart
```
hope this helps someone.

NOTE####
this will install the driver and it runs fine on the 2.6.16 kernel, but when trying to use Xgl, when compiz is run you'll get this error message if someone finds a fix please post it.
compiz.real: GLX_EXT_texture_from_pixmap is missing
compiz.real: Failed to manage screen: 0
compiz.real: No managable screens found on display :0.0

### Post by tonyr on 2006-03-21
As long as you are looking at edits for the guide, the package(s)
**linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy** do not
seem to exist in any of the dapper repositories **main**, **restricted**.
**universe**, **multiverse**.  Are they somewhere else,  have they
been assimilated into something, or are they just missing?

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-22
[QUOTE=tonyr]As long as you are looking at edits for the guide, the package(s)
**linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy** do not
seem to exist in any of the dapper repositories **main**, **restricted**.
**universe**, **multiverse**.  Are they somewhere else,  have they
been assimilated into something, or are they just missing?[/QUOTE]
As theturner pointed out, they have included the nvidia-legacy modules in the restricted modules. This means that there won't be a "linux-restricted-modules-nvidia-legacy" any more.

Here's the evidence of what theturner said (BTW thanks):
```
/lib/linux-restricted-modules/2.6.15-19-k7$ ls
ath_hal  fcdslsl     fcdslusb2  fcpcmcia     fglrx    ltserial
fcdsl    fcdslslusb  fcdslusba  fcpcmcia_cs  fxusb    **nvidia**
fcdsl2   fcdslusb    fcpci      fcusb        ltmodem  **nvidia_legacy**
```


In other words doing "linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`" is enough.

I'll modify the guide.

EDIT: fixed!

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-24
For all the users who had problems with Method 2:

First of all I have edited the guide as I was having some problems with the drivers in Method 2 (such as no nvidia module found, etc.).

Uninstall the driver:
sudo sh Name_of_the_installer --uninstall

and follow EVERY STEP of Method 2 again (also the ones which can look superfluous to you, such as "sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r` **linux-restricted-modules-common**")


Please let me know if you have further problems.


Alberto

Meathod 2 seems to be working well! Thankyou so very much!!!

Only one error in step 8:

----------------------------
Copy the following files:

sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/lib/ 
>>>>>Target: '/usr/lib/xorg/modules/lib/' not valid directory

[No problem with next 2 copy commands however :)]
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/drivers/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/

----------------------------

I did not install the OpenGL32bit compatibility libraries as recommended. (amd64)
Perhaps that is the cause? [of cp target error]

When i rebooted the nvidia logo came up and everythings smooth at last!!
Thanks again!

*edits in [  ]

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-26
[QUOTE=shambling]Meathod 2 seems to be working well! Thankyou so very much!!!

Only one error in step 8:

----------------------------
Copy the following files:

sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/lib/ 
>>>>>Target: '/usr/lib/xorg/modules/lib/' not valid directory

[No problem with next 2 copy commands however :)]
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/drivers/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/

----------------------------

I did not install the OpenGL32bit compatibility libraries as recommended. (amd64)
Perhaps that is the cause? [of cp target error]

When i rebooted the nvidia logo came up and everythings smooth at last!!
Thanks again!

*edits in [  ][/QUOTE]
Very interesting... it could be the OpenGL32bit compatibility libraries. I hope someone can test it.

BTW if you want you can do this:

sudo mkdir /usr/lib/xorg/modules/lib
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/lib/ 

Please, tell me if it works

### Post by davmor2 on 2006-03-27
Nothing I have done now has actually installed the nvidia-glx drivers and have them work completely.  I have reported the bug that I have to both Ubuntu and now to Nvidia themselves.  This is what Nvidia sent me.

0) For the first issue, are you reporting that the system freezes before
fully halting, or just that it fails to power off or actually rebooting?
 Is this problem only present when using the nvidia X driver?
1) Does either problem also reproduce with an official Ubuntu kernel?
2) If you disable the console framebuffer graphics does the 2nd problem
persist?
3) If you boot with the noapic and/or acpi=off kernel parameters does
that have any impact on either problem?
4) What kind of motherboard and BIOS version are you using?

The only question I could not answer was 2) I don't know how to turn off the framebuffer, the second problem reffered to was the fact that it crashed out when logging out or restarting.  Can someone either let me know how to do this or do this and let me know the result so I can get back to Nvidia with it.  I must commend them they got back to me straight away and where quite helpful.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-28
[QUOTE=davmor2]Nothing I have done now has actually installed the nvidia-glx drivers and have them work completely.  I have reported the bug that I have to both Ubuntu and now to Nvidia themselves.  This is what Nvidia sent me.

0) For the first issue, are you reporting that the system freezes before
fully halting, or just that it fails to power off or actually rebooting?
 Is this problem only present when using the nvidia X driver?
1) Does either problem also reproduce with an official Ubuntu kernel?
2) If you disable the console framebuffer graphics does the 2nd problem
persist?
3) If you boot with the noapic and/or acpi=off kernel parameters does
that have any impact on either problem?
4) What kind of motherboard and BIOS version are you using?

The only question I could not answer was 2) I don't know how to turn off the framebuffer, the second problem reffered to was the fact that it crashed out when logging out or restarting.  Can someone either let me know how to do this or do this and let me know the result so I can get back to Nvidia with it.  I must commend them they got back to me straight away and where quite helpful.[/QUOTE]
I doubt framebuffer is enabled by default in Ubuntu.

This is the line in my /boot/grub/menu.lst

kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.15-19-386 root=/dev/hdb1 ro quiet splash

And there's no sign of framebuffer.

If framebuffer were activated there should be something like "video=vasafb" in that line.

I've never used framebuffer anyway.

---

### Post by tonyr on 2006-03-28
[QUOTE=tseliot]I doubt framebuffer is enabled by default in Ubuntu.

This is the line in my /boot/grub/menu.lst

kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.15-19-386 root=/dev/hdb1 ro quiet splash

And there's no sign of framebuffer.

If framebuffer were activated there should be something like "video=vasafb" in that line.

I've never used framebuffer anyway.[/QUOTE]

My /var/log/messages includes the lines
```
Mar 28 11:29:11 playroom kernel: [4294669.588000] vga16fb: mapped to 0xc00a0000
Mar 28 11:29:11 playroom kernel: [4294669.819000] Console: switching to colour frame buffer device 80x25
Mar 28 11:29:11 playroom kernel: [4294669.819000] fb0: VGA16 VGA frame buffer device
```

Doesn't that indicate console is using framebuffer?  I'm running Kubuntu
Flight-5, freshly installed last night, dist-updated this morning, lilo bootloader,
on my Dell Inspiron 2650 (P4M, GeForce2 Go 100, 2.6.15-19-686).  
I added your boot options to the kubuntu boot image section:
```
image=/mnt/hda5/vmlinuz
	label="linux-kubuntu"
	root=/dev/hda5
	initrd=/mnt/hda5/initrd.img
	append=" ro quiet splash"
```
and there are no other "video=" specs in there anywhere.  I'm going to try
"video=vga16fb off" to see if that makes any difference.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-28
UPDATE: there was a typo in my Method 2:
this line:
```
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules[COLOR="Red"]/lib[/COLOR]
```

has to be like this:
```
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/
```

I'll update the scripts as well.

EDIT: the scripts have been fixed.

Sorry for the typo, guys.

---

### Post by tonyr on 2006-03-28
[QUOTE=tseliot]I don't know Lilo, I use only Grub. And as I've said before I don't use framebuffer or I use it and I'm not aware of it ;) .[/QUOTE]

On my system (described in my previous post), inclusion of the
**splash** boot command option causes **vga16fb** to be loaded.
When I remove that option, the entries in /var/log/messages about **vga16fb**
and **framebuffer** are gone, and boot trace reverts to text only.

### Post by WelterPelter on 2006-03-29
Just got a new motherboard and cpu (details in signature) and decided to do a fresh install of 64-bit Dapper. I used method 2 to get the nVidia drivers going, and it worked fine. The only slight tweak was that Dapper didn't have Make installed by default, so the kernel wouldn't recompile without that. There was also minor weirdness getting the right version of gcc running. Other than that, all went well. The graphics look great!
Thanks, tseliot. :KS

### Post by dc2447 on 2006-03-30
When I upgrade my system after installing the drivers I lose the driver.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-30
[QUOTE=dc2447]When I upgrade my system after installing the drivers I lose the driver.[/QUOTE]
I guess that's because also your kernel is updated.

Each kernel need its modules, i.e. you will have to reinstall the drivers every time your kernel is updated or you decide to change it.

That's true for Method 2, 3 and for the script.

---

### Post by dc2447 on 2006-03-30
[QUOTE=tseliot]I guess that's because also your kernel is updated.

Each kernel need its modules, i.e. you will have to reinstall the drivers every time your kernel is updated or you decide to change it.

That's true for Method 2, 3 and for the script.[/QUOTE]

sure - I understand that but I kind of assumed that the script was working out which kernel is installed using uname -r and getting the correct headers.  It appears (and I would need to check) that the script doesn't work with anything other than the stock Dapper kernel.

Cheers

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-03-30
[QUOTE=dc2447]sure - I understand that but I kind of assumed that the script was working out which kernel is installed using uname -r and getting the correct headers.  It appears (and I would need to check) that the script doesn't work with anything other than the stock Dapper kernel.

Cheers[/QUOTE]
Of course if you use a kernel you have compiled and forget to install the headers the script could never find the headers for your kernel because only you can.

If I did not understand what your problem is, please fill me in.

---

### Post by joffe on 2006-03-30
Does anyone here have the problem with nvidia proprietary driver and a garbled console? The console only gets corrupted when I have upped the resolution to e.g. 1024x768-16 (vga=791) in grub's menu.lst. Sometimes the screen just gets black instead of displaying green goo, either way my consoles are useless in this mode.

Now it is relatively easy to remedy by patching the kernel with vesafb-tng, but in my opinion there should be an option to just enable vesafb-tng over regular vesafb. I have been told by nv reps over at nvnews.net that this will be fixed in a future release. This was over a year ago and I have lost all hope having this fixed in my graphics card's lifetime. What is your stance on this?

---

# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder3)  Wed Dec 14 16:39:22 PST 2005

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"

	# path to defoma fonts
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"

EndSection

    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"

EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "ca"
    Option         "XkbVariant" "fr"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Écran générique"
    HorizSync       31.5 - 80.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 75.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV18 [GeForce4 MX 4000 AGP 8x]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV18 [GeForce4 MX 4000 AGP 8x]"
    Monitor        "Écran générique"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"

EndSection

### Post by plush on 2006-04-02
hey TSEliot, I accidentally downloaded your script and ran it on my  PPC version of Dapper without realizing it was intended for the x86 based version.  Is there any harm done?  It seemed to download the package and whatnot, though at one point it said "cp:cannot stat 'usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/*" and then after I put "y" to automatically configure I got "sudo: /usr/bin/nvida-xconfig: command not found, FATAL: Module nvidia not found."  Finally after it asked to start xserver and i put yes it said "sudo: /etc/init.d/kdm command not found".  Please excuse my n00bness.  So far everything SEEMS fine after a bootup too.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-03
[QUOTE=plush]hey TSEliot, I accidentally downloaded your script and ran it on my  PPC version of Dapper without realizing it was intended for the x86 based version.  Is there any harm done?  It seemed to download the package and whatnot, though at one point it said "cp:cannot stat 'usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/*" and then after I put "y" to automatically configure I got "sudo: /usr/bin/nvida-xconfig: command not found, FATAL: Module nvidia not found."  Finally after it asked to start xserver and i put yes it said "sudo: /etc/init.d/kdm command not found".  Please excuse my n00bness.  So far everything SEEMS fine after a bootup too.[/QUOTE]
I think everything's ok because the installer didn't do anything. I don't think the nvidia installer works on PPC.


The installer which is used by my script is thought for x86 (and x86_64) processors and operative systems.

I do not own a PPC and an nvidia driver for PPC doesn't seem to exist at the moment.

---

### Post by WelterPelter on 2006-04-05
Today's update in Dapper (which included an xserver update) killed my nVidia. (Probably due to the new kernel, or maybe due to the new X... or both). I just went through the nVidia 64 driver install again (not sure if this was necessary) and it got the thing working again. 

Just a note.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-05
[QUOTE=WelterPelter]Today's update in Dapper (which included an xserver update) killed my nVidia. (Probably due to the new kernel, or maybe due to the new X... or both). I just went through the nVidia 64 driver install again (not sure if this was necessary) and it got the thing working again. 

Just a note.[/QUOTE]
Every time you install a new kernel you have to reinstall the nvidia driver.

That's why my script might be useful and save you some time.

---

### Post by WelterPelter on 2006-04-05
Is there an option in the script to NOT download the drivers, since I already have them?

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-05
[QUOTE=WelterPelter]Is there an option in the script to NOT download the drivers, since I already have them?[/QUOTE]
Edit the script with a text editor and remove the line I put in red in the example below:

```
#!/bin/bash
# Licence GPL
# Written by Tseliot
# Alpha release
# Version 0.01a
# For 64 bit ONLY!!!
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop

[COLOR="Red"]wget ftp://download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/1.0-8178/NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2.run[/COLOR]
```

Then remember to do "cd path_to_the_folder_of_the_nvidia_installer" before running the script (so that you can use the installer you had downloaded previously)

---

### Post by WelterPelter on 2006-04-05

BTW, I don't see a 64-bit script at your download site, just the nvidia_script1, which says it's only for 32-bit.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-06
[QUOTE=Mythin]Sorry, new to this forum system, here they are.[/QUOTE]
Please, follow these steps:

1)Comment out (i.e. put a # before the line) the line in red as in the example below:

```
Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    [COLOR="Red"]#UseModes       "16:10"[/COLOR]
    HorizSync       30.0 - 83.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 75.0
EndSection
```

2) And set the driver to "nvidia" (instead of "nv")

3) Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

Tell me if you can see anything on your screen

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-06
[QUOTE=Mythin]I'm not getting a mouse cursor showing, but I'll try that tonight also.

I'll update you tonight on my progress, and thanks![/QUOTE]
About the mouse cursor:
add these options in the Section Device of your xorg.conf

```
Option "SWCursor" "on"
Option "HWCursor" "off"
```

### Post by Mythin on 2006-04-08
I've made some progress!

My system still isn't working with the latest NVidia drivers, but I believe the new errors are much more useful.

It was trying to use CRT-0 instead of DFP-0.  Forcing it to use DFP-0 caused different problems.

xorg.conf:

```
Section "Device"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Option	   "ConnectedMonitor" "DFP"
    Option	   "UseEDID" "False"
EndSection

Section "Modes"
   Identifier "16:10"
   ModeLine "1680x1050" 146.2 1680 1960 2136 2240 1050 1080 1086 1089
   -hsync +vsync
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
   Identifier "Generic Monitor"
   UseModes "16:10"
   HorizSync 30-83
   VertRefresh 56-75
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1680x1050" "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600"
	ViewPort   0 0
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

```
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to use mode "1680x1050" for DFP-0; cannot compute
```

Happens for all modes.

```
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1680x1050"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1280x1024"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1024x768"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "800x600"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): 
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to validate any modes; falling back to the default mode
(WW) NVIDIA(0):     "nvidia-auto-select".
(WW) NVIDIA(0): 
(II) NVIDIA(0): Validated modes:
(II) NVIDIA(0):     "nvidia-auto-select"
(II) NVIDIA(0): Virtual screen size determined to be 800 x 600
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to get display device DFP-0's EDID; cannot compute DPI
(WW) NVIDIA(0):     from EDID.
(==) NVIDIA(0): DPI set to (75, 75); computed from built-in default
```

Occurs near the end of the video section of Xorg.0.log.

[QUOTE=Mythin]I've made some progress!

My system still isn't working with the latest NVidia drivers, but I believe the new errors are much more useful.

It was trying to use CRT-0 instead of DFP-0.  Forcing it to use DFP-0 caused different problems.

xorg.conf:

```
Section "Device"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Option	   "ConnectedMonitor" "DFP"
    Option	   "UseEDID" "False"
EndSection

Section "Modes"
   Identifier "16:10"
   ModeLine "1680x1050" 146.2 1680 1960 2136 2240 1050 1080 1086 1089
   -hsync +vsync
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
   Identifier "Generic Monitor"
   UseModes "16:10"
   HorizSync 30-83
   VertRefresh 56-75
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1680x1050" "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600"
	ViewPort   0 0
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

```
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to use mode "1680x1050" for DFP-0; cannot compute
```

Happens for all modes.

```
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1680x1050"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1280x1024"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1024x768"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "800x600"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): 
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to validate any modes; falling back to the default mode
(WW) NVIDIA(0):     "nvidia-auto-select".
(WW) NVIDIA(0): 
(II) NVIDIA(0): Validated modes:
(II) NVIDIA(0):     "nvidia-auto-select"
(II) NVIDIA(0): Virtual screen size determined to be 800 x 600
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to get display device DFP-0's EDID; cannot compute DPI
(WW) NVIDIA(0):     from EDID.
(==) NVIDIA(0): DPI set to (75, 75); computed from built-in default
```

Occurs near the end of the video section of Xorg.0.log.[/QUOTE]
Replace Option   "UseEDID" "False" with the following option:

```
Option "ModeValidation" "NoEdidModes"
```

Tell me if it works, please.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-08
[QUOTE=Mythin]Same problem.  Same lines in the Xorg.0.log also, but it added lines about not being able to find any EDID info or something like that.[/QUOTE]
These options are very new to me.

Try this on instead of the previous option I suggested:

```
Option "UseEDIDFreqs" "FALSE"
```

Then restart the xserver

### Post by Mythin on 2006-04-08
I fixed my problem!

I don't know why, but X was trying to use a CRT that doesn't exist.  within the video card device section, I added:

```
Option   "UseDisplayDevice" "DFP"
```

Everything started beautifully.

Thank you for all the help!

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-09
[QUOTE=darknightuk]i believe the directories below in method 2 is incorrect at least it is for me?
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/drivers/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/

here's what i do
/usr/X11R6/lib/modules/drivers/nvidia_drv.* to /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
and to get 3D
copy /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/extensions/libglx.so* to /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/
(libglx.so is a link to libglx.libglx.so.1.0.xxxx(Version Installed)[/QUOTE]

The directories in Method 2 should be correct because I use this command:
sudo ./nvidia-installer -n --x-prefix=/usr/lib/xorg/

which tells the installer to put the files into /usr/lib/xorg/ instead of /usr/X11R6/.

In /usr/X11R6/lib/ I have only the folder X11.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-09
[QUOTE=gibbylinks]Hi,

I've been struggling trying to get this working for what seems like months. I have tried so many different things that my head is swimming and I've lost track. So......

[LIST]
[*]I have just done a complete fresh install of Dapper Flight 6.
[*]I then installed all the latest upgrades using synaptic.
[*]I rebooted to the latest kernel 2.6.15-20-386
[*]I used method 1 and rebooted
[/LIST]

And I get the same thing I have been getting all the time.
My monitor goes black...then I get a white box that alternates between "Analogue" and "Digital" and then I loose the signal, and I have to reboot in recovery mode and change back to using the "nv" driver to be able to login.

I have a inno3d 6600GT AGP graphics card with a Samsung 930BF TFT monitor

I'm hoping some fountain of knowledge can assist me as I've been itching to try xgl & compiz

I've attached my Xorg.0.log file

Hellllllp[/QUOTE]
Boot in Recovery Mode

Then type this
```
nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

add this option to the Section Device of the file:
```
Option   "UseDisplayDevice" "DFP"
```

CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

type:
reboot

I hope it works

```
```
sudo ./nvidia-installer --uninstall
```

### Post by meatbites on 2006-04-10
I'm having issues keeping the new driver running on my Pentium 840 system (dual core, running on 2.6.12-10-686-smp). It compiles and installs fine and when I head back into gdm it works beautifully. The problem is when I reboot -- the drivers no longer work and X-server refuses to start. I've poked around the forums a bit and from what I can tell, this is the result of the linux-restricted-modules nuking the folder that the drivers have been installed to (I haven't confirmed this).

As a result, I need to install the drivers every time I reboot. I would rather not. :p

Looking through the guide and the unofficial NVIDIA script, I see they both require the removal of nvidia-kernel-common. The script also removes linux-restricted-modules. This wouldn't ordinarily be a problem, however I suspect I can't remove either without a dead install:

```
apt-get -s remove nvidia-kernel-common
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  linux-386 linux-686-smp linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-10-386 linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-10-686-smp
  linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-9-386 linux-restricted-modules-386 linux-restricted-modules-686-smp nvidia-kernel-common

apt-get -s remove linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  linux-686-smp linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-10-686-smp linux-restricted-modules-686-smp
```

Unless I'm very much mistaken, both of these actions will remove both kernels thus invalidating my install. Any suggestions greatly appreciated!

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-10
[QUOTE=meatbites]I'm having issues keeping the new driver running on my Pentium 840 system (dual core, running on 2.6.12-10-686-smp). It compiles and installs fine and when I head back into gdm it works beautifully. The problem is when I reboot -- the drivers no longer work and X-server refuses to start. I've poked around the forums a bit and from what I can tell, this is the result of the linux-restricted-modules nuking the folder that the drivers have been installed to (I haven't confirmed this).

As a result, I need to install the drivers every time I reboot. I would rather not. :p

Looking through the guide and the unofficial NVIDIA script, I see they both require the removal of nvidia-kernel-common. The script also removes linux-restricted-modules. This wouldn't ordinarily be a problem, however I suspect I can't remove either without a dead install:

```
apt-get -s remove nvidia-kernel-common
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  linux-386 linux-686-smp linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-10-386 linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-10-686-smp
  linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-9-386 linux-restricted-modules-386 linux-restricted-modules-686-smp nvidia-kernel-common

apt-get -s remove linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  linux-686-smp linux-restricted-modules-2.6.12-10-686-smp linux-restricted-modules-686-smp
```

Unless I'm very much mistaken, both of these actions will remove both kernels thus invalidating my install. Any suggestions greatly appreciated![/QUOTE]
Trust me, I would never write a script which remove your kernel.

Packages such as linux-386 or linux-686-smp are dummy packages: they make you install linux-image-2.6.x.x linux-restricted-modules-2.6.x.x and linux-headers-2.6.x.x

If you remove linux-386 or linux-k7, etc. you remove only the restricted modules for that kernel. You will keep linux-image-2.6.x.x (which is the tru kernel)

### Post by meatbites on 2006-04-10
Thanks for the reassurance, tseliot.

It's probably because I'm running Breezy, but the script failed dismally. That said, I have the drivers fully working and I can now restart without any issues.

I compiled and installed the drivers the usual way. With the troublesome packages gone, I simply created a symlink for 'cc' to 'gcc-3.4' in /usr/bin/. I also had to apt-get remove the gcc and build-essentials packages that the script installed, as the NVIDIA installer complained about a compiler/kernel version difference (I assume this doesn't occur in Dapper?).

Just in case someone else finds this useful, aside from gcc-3.4, the other build-type packages I have installed are:

	libc6
	libstdc++6
	make
	autoconf
	automake1.9
	libstdc++2.10-glibc2.2
	linux-headers-`uname -r` (2.6.12-10-686-smp)

That's the lot.

Thanks again for your help -- you're providing a wonderful service here.

---

### Post by tonyr on 2006-04-10
About **Method 2 Uninstall**, a few minor observations from my
own doomed attempts to make this work on my Dell Inspiron 2650 laptop
(P4M, 1.5GHz, GeForce2 Go 100):

1. The uninstall process removes the relevent files from
    **/usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/lib/{drivers,extensions}**, but not from the
    copy targets **/usr/lib/xorg/modules/libX*** and
    **/usr/lib/xorg/modules/{drivers.extensions}**.  A complete
    removal should remove the copied files, also.
    

2. Some of the files that are copied in step 6 from
    **/usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/<whatever>** are actually symbolic links,
    but do not get copied as symbolic links on my system.  The result
    is that the original **/usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/libglx.so** is 
    overwritten (destroyed).  Some applications, like xscreensaver,
    suffer because the GLX support disappears.  I have to make sure to have
    a copy of the original libglx.so around to restore after uninstall.
    I have been manually copying the real files and recreating the
    symbolic links in the target directories.  **cp** should be able to do this,
    but I haven't discovered how to convince it in exactly the right way.

### Post by tonyr on 2006-04-10
[QUOTE=tonyr]About **Method 2 Uninstall**, a few minor observations from my
own doomed attempts to make this work on my Dell Inspiron 2650 laptop
(P4M, 1.5GHz, GeForce2 Go 100):
...[snip]
 **cp** should be able to do this,
    but I haven't discovered how to convince it in exactly the right way.[/QUOTE]

The **cp** option I was looking for is **--no-dereference.**

I suggest that the Dapper guide, Method 2, be modified from
> 
Copy the following files:

sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/drivers/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
sudo cp /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/

to
> 
Copy the following files:

sudo mv /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/libglx.so   /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/libglx.so.backup
sudo cp --no-dereference /usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/
sudo cp --no-dereference /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/drivers/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
sudo cp --no-dereference /usr/lib/xorg/lib/modules/extensions/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/


EDIT: that didn't come out quit the way I envisioned it, but you get the idea - tr

and that the Method 2 Uninstall section have lines something like
> 
sudo rm /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/libglx.so
sudo mv /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/libglx.so.backup  /usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/libglx.so
****

It seems that some people have the same problem with resolution.

In KDE-Settings->Devices->Display (shoot me if this is correct)
I noticed the nvidia card was set to Nvidia 6800 although I have a 6200 and the Monitorsettings weren't correct either.
Initially I tried to find a corresponding monitor, but it didn't do the trick. But nevertheless these KDE settings changed the xorg.conf file.
 
By the way my monitor is a TFT connected via DVI, after testing with a VGA-cable it did work??? Changing it back, again lower resolution.

I thought than it is the driver for the graphic card. Corresponding to KDE changes after playing around with the drivers some lines of the xorg.conf looked like the following:
boardname "NVIDIA GPU GeForce 6200"
busid "PCI:1:0:0"

### Post by ubernoob on 2006-04-11
one minor correction:

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start & sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start

should be

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start **&&** sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start

### Post by mannheim on 2006-04-11
> one minor correction:

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start & sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start

should be

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start && sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start

I don't think that's right. I *think* the second syntax means that kdm would be executed only if the "sudo ... gdm" command exits with 0 (i.e. succesful completion). If gdm is not installed (e.g. if its kubuntu, not ubuntu), then the "sudo ... gdm" will fail, and we then want to try kdm. The original version, with "&", will try both.

So:
```
foo &  bar 
# execute foo and bar

foo && bar 
# execute foo; and if foo exits succesfully then execute bar

foo || bar 
# execute foo; and if it fails then execute bar

```

The "||" seems most logical in this case. But probably "&" has the same effect, because perhaps kdm just won't launch if gdm is already running on the same display (I guess).

```
Option "SWCursor" "on"
```

### Post by mannheim on 2006-04-11
[QUOTE=tseliot]I have the same issue (and I'm not using XGL):

Add this option to the Section Device of your xorg.conf

```
Option "SWCursor" "on"
```[/QUOTE]

I had flickering cursor problems with the *previous* version of the nvidia driver (8169 or whatever it was): the animated cursors would always flicker (but only the animated cursors). The above workaround (SWCursor) fixed it; but the SWCursor did not work well over windows showing video or gl stuff -- the cursor disappeared when over a video window, or caused corruption, as others mention in this thread. I have a 6200-based card, and I am not using xgl.

For me, one big plus for the recent driver update is that my own flickering cursor problems have gone with version 8756! I don't need to use SWCursor any more: the default HW cursor is working fine for me.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-12
> **ghostzapper]I took the installer from NVidia's Website and installed it before even installing the nvidia-glx driver from the repositories. As i said, i _wanted_ to be smart :)[/QUOTE]
To be even smarter, you should uninstall it from the nvidia installer before trying the driver from the repos  said:**
> It's a 6700 XL but the xorg.conf sais 6600 with ? behind it. It  is a card for a Medion PC Model 8800.
It's ok, it doesn't matter which model the xorg.conf detects.

> **ghostzapper]I installe dthe 8756 first but couldn't get x to start. The error message was about glx missing though the line with glx was in xorg.conf. I guess this is because i didn't install the nvidia-glx driver first (which must be th 7174).[/QUOTE]
There are 2 drivers in the repos: 8756 and 7174 (which is the legacy driver said:**
> So to be exact, i used first method 2 and when x didn't start i used method 1.
Never ever do that again.


Now, if you want to solve the problem, follow these steps:

Press CTRL-ALT-F1 (so as to get to the command line, not a windowed terminal, but out of the graphical interface GUI) 

Login with your username and password (if required) 

Stop the xserver: 

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (OR "kdm stop" if you use KDE)
```

```
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer
```

```
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer --uninstall
```

Now the driver (from the installer) has been uninstalled.

Uninstall the driver 7174 and clean the little mess you made ;) :
```
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-glx linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r` linux-restricted-modules-common nvidia-kernel-common
```

Answer "yes" no matter what it asks you to remove (e.g. linux-386 and other stuff) (trust me, I know what I'm doing :)  ).

Then reinstall the driver from the repos:
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
```

NOTE: make sure you use this apostrophe ` instead of this '

Restart the xserver and everything should work:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (or "kdm start" if you use KDE)
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-14
[QUOTE=apollo2011]Hmm, I tried running the script, and when I restarted KDM and X, I got a black screen and was unable to get back to the console. I had to reboot in Recovery mode before I could see a command line again and revert the xorg.conf.[/QUOTE]
I need to diagnose the error.
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

Since then, xorg insists that it cannot find the nvidia module, neither does modprobe. I use a custom kernel with the latest stable sources from kernel.org for various reasons and this method always worked out for me before (and I greatly prefer it above Nvidia's installer since apt gets to manage everything). I tried a lot so far, like uninstalling the restricted modules of the Ubuntu kernel I still keep as a 2nd boot option, using the nvidia-kernel-sources from Debian... nothing, it just does not seem to find the nvidia module.

Anyway, I'll include the relevant sections of my xorg.conf, hope someone can point me to the right direction. Thx in advance. 


```
Section "Module"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"dbe"
	Load	"ddc"
#	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"record"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

Section "Device"
  BoardName    "GeForce FX 5700"
  VendorName   "NVidia"
  BusID        "PCI:1:0:0"
  Driver       "nv"
  Identifier   "GeForce FX 5700"
  Screen       0
  Option       "NoLogo" "true"
  Option       "RenderAccel" "true"
  Option       "AllowGLXWithComposite" "true"

#Section "DRI"

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-16
[QUOTE=funkenstein]1. I need the 7676 drivers for Battlefield 2 (8*** ones kill multiplaying with it :( ) method 2 used to be able to do that, but no longer. Can you please add the "Install Any Driver" method again?[/QUOTE]
I don't know if 7676 is compatible with kernels 2.6.14 or higher. There was a patch for 8178 but I don't know if it would work with 7676.

[QUOTE=funkenstein]2. Might be best for backups to append `date +%s` (number of seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) instead of .backup, to avoid overwriting when the n00bs copy and paste from the tutorial *ahem* :rolleyes:[/QUOTE]
Yes, if I'm not wrong my script does it.


It looks like the BusID of your graphic card is missing from your xorg.conf. Try this:
```
lspci | grep nVidia
```

**NOTE: bash is case sensitive, therefore make sure you use a "V" instead of a "v" in the vord "nVidia".**

In this way you should see something like this:
```
[COLOR="Red"]01:00.0[/COLOR] VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5500] (rev a1)
```

The number I highlighted in blue is the BUSId of the card.

Put it in the Section Device of your xorg.conf.
For example in my case it would be:
```
 BusID "PCI:1:0:0"
```

Try the driver then.

---

### Post by joffe on 2006-04-16
[QUOTE=tseliot]Add these options to the Section Device of your xorg.conf and tell me if it works, please:

```
Option "IgnoreEDID" "true"
Option "UseEDID" "false"
Option "ExtactModeTimingsDVI" "true"
```[/QUOTE]
According to the [1.0-8756 README]("http://download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/1.0-8756/README/appendix-d.html"), the "IgnoreEDID" parameter has become deprecated and is ignored by the new driver. UseEDID should be used instead. On a side note, "IgnoreEdidFreqs" has disappeared from the README so I guess it is also no longer recognized by the driver.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-17
[QUOTE=Cyrus XIII]Using the Debian sources was just one of the things I tried after things stopped working with the new Dapper sources.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-27
[QUOTE=Scunizi]What is the "expected" differance and/or benefit between installing the driver manually using version 2 and Synaptic?[/QUOTE]
If a new nvidia driver is released after Dapper is released (as stable) you won't find it in the repos. Method 2 will enable you to install always the latest driver.

Currently there's no need to use Method 2.

### Post by joffe on 2006-04-28
@tseliot:
First things first. Big up to you and everybody else here at the forum helping the nvidia users getting their drivers work with ubuntu.

Apropos the Geforce4 420 mentioned in the problem section of your giude, with the new 1.0-8756 driver none of those options:
```
        Option 		"RenderAccel" 	         "true"
	Option 		"AllowGLXWithComposite"  "true" 
	Option		"IgnoreEDID"	         "true"
	Option		"IgnoreEdidFreqs"        "true"
	Option		"GenerateRTList"         "0"
	Option		"OverridePolarity"       "1"
```
are really needed. The first two are now enabled by default, the following two are deprecated and the last two options don't have anything to do with getting the card to function (as far as I know, and I have spent more time on nvnews.net than is healthy :)). Since there are a couple of different notebooks with this chip and some have various problems (and solutions), may I suggest that you link to these forum threads found over at the nvnews.net as well:

**GeForce4 420 Go**
[ [COLOR="Navy"]PB: Toshiba Satellite 1410 (Geforce4 420Go), TwinView, TV (Composite), Driver 8756[/COLOR]]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=68793")

[[COLOR="Navy"]Go 420 under Ubuntu Dapper - install problems[/COLOR]]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=68245")


**GeForce4 440 Go** (similar workaround as with the GeForce420 Go)
[ [COLOR="Navy"]8756 Works on Toshiba laptops![/COLOR]]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=67896")

### Post by tseliot on 2006-04-29
[QUOTE=cazz]Hi nice thread
After I have read to page 20 my head start to scream so I'm sorry if I repeat if someone have ask.

I have install Dapper Beta 2 and what I can see I dont have any nvidia driver install (No nivida screen on startup)

I have a HP Pavilion zv5000 and that have a Geforce4 440 GO 32 MB
I just like as a easy way install a nvidia driver for my laptop.
Maybe so I dont need to edit some config file.

I just like to install, restart then then it works

How do I do that??

When I was using 5.10 that was very easy but I have forgot the address to that page that I have read about how to do and that is maybe dont work in Dapper??[/QUOTE]
Follow Method 1 and then this before logging out (as described in Method 1):

```
sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

Add the following new option in the "Screen" section of your :

```
Option "ModeValidation" "NoMaxPClkCheck;NoEdidMaxPClkCheck"
```

```
sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.conf
```
and make it look like this:

```
options nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NVreg_Mobile=4
```

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

### Post by Sutekh on 2006-05-01
[quote=enkrypt3d]Yea I went thru all that and it still complains about the gcc version mis-match... sigh... Maybe I should start with a clean install......
[/quote]Which method did you follow?  Method 1 should fix you up fine without the need for compiling and gcc.  

Method 2 is the method that requires the corrct version of gcc (v3.4) to compile the NVIDIA module.  If you follow method 2, make sure you use these steps.
```
cd “directory where you have the nvidia installer”
su
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
exit
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
```

[quote=encrypt3d]
The way I like to install kubuntu is to type server at the boot: prompt so that it does not install KDE....then I can install KDE by doing apt-get install kubuntu-desktop.... at this point, is this where i should start this guide? I don't have to install the nvidia-glx manually right?[/quote]

You can install the NVIDIA drivers whenever you like, you don't even need to wait until you install the kubuntu-dektop.  You only install the nvidia-glx package, when using Method 1 (the Ubuntu repository drivers)

### Post by enkrypt3d on 2006-05-01
[QUOTE=tseliot]Why didn't you use the Uninstall function of my script?

Anyhow, you can use my script for Breezy or follow Method 2

BTW the problem you had with gcc was described in the Problems Section (which sometimes is useful to read)

```
3) If the installer complains in this way:
...
ERROR: Unable to find the development tool `cc` in your path; please make sure
that you have the package 'gcc' installed. If gcc is installed on your
system, then please check that `cc` is in your PATH.

Make sure you have gcc-3.4 installed
Code:

sudo apt-get install gcc-3.4

Then let's make the system use gcc-3.4 instead of gcc-4.0 by default:
Code:

sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/gcc-3.4 /usr/bin/gcc


Run the Nvidia installer again (this time it won't complain)

IF everything went fine you can switch back to your old gcc settings (so as to use gcc-4.0 by default)
Code:

sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/gcc-4.0 /usr/bin/gcc
```

This method is reported to work also when the "Export CC" thing fails



A K8 kernel will work fine.

Regards[/QUOTE]

OH I didnt realize there was an uninstall function on that script......its ok it only took me about 20 mins doing it all from scratch lol 

getting 16K+ FPS in Glxgears and 350-500 FPS in tuxracer is purely amazing...... do you think it has anything to do w/ the SLi? Or is it only running on one card? Thanks for all your help!

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-01
[QUOTE=enkrypt3d]OH I didnt realize there was an uninstall function on that script......its ok it only took me about 20 mins doing it all from scratch lol 

getting 16K+ FPS in Glxgears and 350-500 FPS in tuxracer is purely amazing...... do you think it has anything to do w/ the SLi? Or is it only running on one card? Thanks for all your help![/QUOTE]
If you have 2 graphic cards with SLI you can try this command:
```
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup
```
Then
```
sudo nvidia-xconfig --only-one-x-screen --sli=Auto
```

Log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

If something goes wrong:
```
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

I hope it works

---
