### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-07
[QUOTE=Kirzzy_Boy]Hi tseliot

Here's the info you need to help me...
I used method 2 to install and tried the same to re-install
I followed your instructions and got the following log (attachment)[/QUOTE]
You said you got this error when you tried to install the driver, right?
```
ERROR: Unable to load the kernel module 'nvidia.ko'. This happens most frequently when this kernel module was built against the wrong or improperly configured kernel sources, with a version of gcc that differs from the one used to build the target kernel, or if a driver such as rivafb/nvidiafb is present and prevents the NVIDIA kernel module from obtaining ownership of the NVIDIA graphics device(s).
```

If so, try this:
Press CTRL+ALT+F1 (you'll see ONLY the command line) then type:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (or kdm stop, if you use KDM)
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer
sudo sh name_of_the_installer --uninstall
```

EDIT: I made a mess editing this message but I guess it doesn't matter now since the problem is solved.

Thank you for your help :p 

What happened was that when I ran the script it came up with a fault!
It told me that "sudo: NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8756-pkg1.run not found" and then gdm couldn't start (no driver)
so, what I did was a mix of the older and newer how-to....
I did as follows:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
su
CC=gcc-3.6
export CC
exit
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8756-pkg1.run
```

That worked and it installed perfectly

I realized that it was important to first uninstall the current driver before attempting anything.

Thanks again for the excellent help

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-16
SCRIPT UPDATED to Version 0.033a

*Added a Reinstall function that doesn't download the Nvidia installer every time you need to reinstall it
*Added a control of the integrity of the Nvidia installer (md5sum)
*Added the development package for the Xserver (Dapper's scripts only).
*Fixed a typo in the scripts for Breezy

Please, let me know of any bugs you find.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-16
[QUOTE=ElderLars]1. I guess you have right!

2. I am not sure but i think it said something like "Installation was not sucessful"

3. The content: 

I tried to install Nvidia-glxn throu Synptic. i got no errors but Penguin racer did not work or the most screensavers.
I will trie another method.[/QUOTE]
Please don't mix the methods!

Make your sources.list look exactly like this:

```
##deb cdrom:[Ubuntu 5.10 _Breezy Badger_ - Release i386 (20051012)]/ breezy main restricted


deb [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper main restricted universe multiverse
deb-src [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper main restricted universe multiverse

## Major bug fix updates produced after the final release of the
## distribution.
deb [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-updates main restricted
deb-src [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-updates main restricted

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'universe'
## repository.
## N.B. software from this repository is ENTIRELY UNSUPPORTED by the Ubuntu
## team, and may not be under a free licence. Please satisfy yourself as to
## your rights to use the software. Also, please note that software in
## universe WILL NOT receive any review or updates from the Ubuntu security
## team.
deb [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper universe
deb-src [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper universe

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'backports'
## repository.
## N.B. software from this repository may not have been tested as
## extensively as that contained in the main release, although it includes
## newer versions of some applications which may provide useful features.
## Also, please note that software in backports WILL NOT receive any review
## or updates from the Ubuntu security team.
# deb [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse
# deb-src [http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://se.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse

deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main restricted
deb-src [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main restricted

deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe
deb-src [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe

deb [http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper universe main restricted multiverse
```

  Option 		"RenderAccel" 		"true"
	Option 		"AllowGLXWithComposite" "true"

```

    Option         "ModeValidation"   "DFP-0: NoEdidDFPMaxSizeCheck, NoVesaModes"
```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-16
[QUOTE=OPaul]What were they for?[/QUOTE]
Here is his post (I've lost the file with the updates :(  ):
> @tseliot:
First things first. Big up to you and everybody else here at the forum helping the nvidia users getting their drivers work with ubuntu.

Apropos the Geforce4 420 mentioned in the problem section of your giude, with the new 1.0-8756 driver none of those options:
Code:
        Option          "RenderAccel"            "true"
        Option          "AllowGLXWithComposite"  "true" 
        Option          "IgnoreEDID"             "true"
        Option          "IgnoreEdidFreqs"        "true"
        Option          "GenerateRTList"         "0"
        Option          "OverridePolarity"       "1"
are really needed. The first two are now enabled by default, the following two are deprecated and the last two options don't have anything to do with getting the card to function (as far as I know, and I have spent more time on nvnews.net than is healthy ). Since there are a couple of different notebooks with this chip and some have various problems (and solutions), may I suggest that you link to these forum threads found over at the nvnews.net as well:

GeForce4 420 Go
[PB: Toshiba Satellite 1410 (Geforce4 420Go), TwinView, TV (Composite), Driver 8756]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=68793")

[Go 420 under Ubuntu Dapper - install problems]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=68245")


GeForce4 440 Go (similar workaround as with the GeForce420 Go)
[8756 Works on Toshiba laptops!]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=67896")

### Post by funkenstein on 2006-05-23
hi there,

ran your script, b0rk3d my machine, re ran a couple of times (deleting the redundant .run files and the extracted directory) till it worked - here's my report:

you probably want to purge your current nvidia drivers and reboot, just to be on the safe side. It helped me when I was having problems. you can do it from the script directly as it's the first question.

if you run the script more than once, make sure you delete the package files and the extracted directory before re-running it... tseliot - do you think it's possible to check and make sure the .run file isn't there already, and if it is to delete it and the directory it's extracted to before downloading everything?

I had some sources.list problems, so I cleaned out anything that wasn't official ubuntu. Make sure to "sudo aptitude autoclean" if you have problems as aptitude (or apt-get) won't redownload problematic files if it already has a copy in /var/cache/apt/archives/*.deb 

one of the packages wanted to download from beerorkid (one of the compiz repos)... but that's ok.

tseliot - you might need to modify line 22 in your script from:
```
sudo apt-get --assume-yes install linux-headers-`uname -r` build-essential gcc gcc-3.4 xserver-xorg-dev;
```
to:
```
sudo apt-get --assume-yes [color=red]**--force-yes**[/color] install linux-headers-`uname -r` build-essential gcc gcc-3.4 xserver-xorg-dev;
```

otherwise, it's been a success... now I need to go back home and see if BF2 behaves this time around... ](*,)

---

/usr/lib/xorg/lib/libX

> rinzwind@discworld:~$ sudo nvidia-xconfig

Using X configuration file: "/etc/X11/xorg.conf".
Backed up file '/etc/X11/xorg.conf' as '/etc/X11/xorg.conf.backup'
New X configuration file written to '/etc/X11/xorg.conf'




2. 
and this
**sudo gedit /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop**
gives an empty file.
It looks like it needs to be:
**sudo gedit /usr/share/applications/nvidia-settings.desktop**

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-25
[QUOTE=Rinzwind]Dear TS,

**sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup**

seems to be overkill since

shows this after I started it:[/QUOTE]
"/etc/X11/xorg.conf[COLOR="Red"]_[/COLOR]backup" is different from "/etc/X11/xorg.conf[COLOR="Red"].[/COLOR]backup"


[QUOTE=Rinzwind]2. 
and this
**sudo gedit /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop**
gives an empty file.
It looks like it needs to be:
**sudo gedit /usr/share/applications/nvidia-settings.desktop**

Dapper drake btw :)[/QUOTE]

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-25
[QUOTE=chadk]This script is excellent, worked great on breezy with amd64 (I installed the 32 bit version) but not working on Dapper (tried the 64 bit version).  Just tried over and over to pull up X and it finally failed asking if I wanted to debug (you know, the ugly blue screen with messed up fonts).[/QUOTE]
Ok, let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-05-30
[QUOTE=TakisX]I made a clean install of dapper.I use the 2.6.15-23-686 kernel.I follow your method 2 to install nvidia driver but synaptic download the 2.6.15-23-386 kernel when i type
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-xconfig nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-23-686  as you say
Why? it does not like 386 kernel ?[/QUOTE]
Wrong. The exact command is:
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-xconfig nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
```

Ok, I know it's pretty much the same.

And why does the fact that 2.6.15-23-386 is downloaded bother you?
It should not affect the function of the driver.

### Post by lanhelp on 2006-05-30
Im using the latest nvidia-glx and nvidia-kernel suffix 62. I have a toshiba notebook qwith gforce 4 420 go and all I can get is 969x768 pixels, not 1024. I have played with  many modelines in xorg.conf, EDID's e Mobile options from module but no success... right now I'm seen a vertical black border on my right.

---

### Post by OPaul on 2006-05-30
[QUOTE=lanhelp]Im using the latest nvidia-glx and nvidia-kernel suffix 62. I have a toshiba notebook qwith gforce 4 420 go and all I can get is 969x768 pixels, not 1024. I have played with  many modelines in xorg.conf, EDID's e Mobile options from module but no success... right now I'm seen a vertical black border on my right.[/QUOTE]
In your xorg.conf add the following under the Screen section. ```
    Option         "ExactModeTimingsDVI"   "TRUE"
    Option         "ModeValidation"   "DFP-0: NoEdidDFPMaxSizeCheck, NoVesaModes"
```
and in the /etc/modprobe.d/options file add this.```
options nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NVreg_Mobile=1
```
I'm pretty sure that's all I needed to fix the problem. This information should be in the Notes section of the Howto (#8...eight, stupid smiley faces).

---

Maybe you could change that to read: "echo -e "Do you want to install, reinstall, or uninstall the Nvidia driver 8762?\n(Type "i" to install, "r" to reinstall or "u" to uninstall) (or press CTRL+C to exit)"

### Post by Dice on 2006-06-03
Hi folks.

First post here...  A week ago, I used method 2 with success. Very good work!

After installing the final Dapper, I found and used the script but not without noticing your feedback request.

I am running and AMD machine with a 6800 AGP card; fresh Ubuntu+easyubuntu and after enabling universe, everything ran perfectly, with one tiny issue at the end.  For whatever reason, gdm didn't restart automatically. envydm.log has and had 'gdm' (without quotes) as it's only contents and it should have worked (i think?). I recieved error feedback referencing to lines 93, 95, and 97? Sorry, didn't write them down, but I understand that this is where it looks to see if I'm running G, K, or X and then restart.

```
echo "Do you want to Start the Xserver now? (y/n) \ "Yes" is the default answer";
	read server;
	if [ "$server" = "y" ]
	then 
		if [ cat envydm.log | grep gdm ];
		then sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start;
		elif [ cat envydm.log | grep kdm ];
		then sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start;
		elif [ cat envydm.log | grep xdm ];
		then sudo /etc/init.d/xdm start;
		fi
	elif [ "$server" = "" ]
	then 
		if [ cat envydm.log | grep gdm ];
		then sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start;
		elif [ cat envydm.log | grep kdm ];
		then sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start;
		elif [ cat envydm.log | grep xdm ];
		then sudo /etc/init.d/xdm start;
		fi
```

The only reason I am even giving feedback is because it was requested.  Excellent job with the script. :KS If this has already been mentioned, disregard.  Admittedly, I didn't read all 52 pages.  Thank you for writting this script, as doing method 2 was almost overwhelming because I've only very recently converted to Linux.

Regards,
Dice

### Post by haircut on 2006-06-03

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-03
[QUOTE=galileon]Hello, sorry if this sounds stupid, but I've been trying to install the nvidia-glx drivers on a laptop without internet connection, and its not working...

could anyone tell me if there's a possibility of downloading the required files and if so which ones, to install it?

we're trying to get 3d acceleration on the laptop, but we cannot connect it to the net because of some stupid university rules (only1 computer is allowed, and they check the mac address)

thanks a lot for helping,

galileon.[/QUOTE]
1) Download the following packages:
[http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/linux-restricted-modules-common_2.6.15.11-1_all.deb]("http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/linux-restricted-modules-common_2.6.15.11-1_all.deb")

[http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/n/nvidia-kernel-common/nvidia-kernel-common_20051028+1_all.deb]("http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/n/nvidia-kernel-common/nvidia-kernel-common_20051028+1_all.deb")

[http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-23-386_2.6.15.11-1_i386.deb]("http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-23-386_2.6.15.11-1_i386.deb")

[http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/nvidia-glx_1.0.8762+2.6.15.11-1_i386.deb]("http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/nvidia-glx_1.0.8762+2.6.15.11-1_i386.deb")

2) NOTE: this step is not required if you are using a kernel for 386

Then type: uname -r to know what is the architecture your kernel was built for (e.g. 386, 686, k7, etc.) and download the related restricted modules from the link below:
[http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/]("http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/restricted/l/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15/")

3) Install the packages:
then type:
```
cd directory_with_the_packages
```
```
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```

4) Back up your /etc/X11/xorg.conf: 
```
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup
```

5) Set up your xorg.conf:
```
sudo nvidia-xconfig
```

Log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

### Post by zero_2x on 2006-06-03
[QUOTE=tseliot]Driver 8178 needs a patch.

Can I see your xorg.conf?[/QUOTE]

I used method 1 on a clean installation: This one is with nv driver and with the UseEDID commented, the resolution works fine at 60hz (1680x1050)

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder3)  Mon May 15 13:23:42 PDT 2006

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#

# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#

# again, run the following command:

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
    InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "Files"

	# path to defoma fonts
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "i2c"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "latam"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "eraser"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "SyncMaster"
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV40? [Unknown nVidia Card]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
#   Option         "UseEDID" "False"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV40? [Unknown nVidia Card]"
    Monitor        "SyncMaster"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       15
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1680x1050" "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-03
[QUOTE=crane]OK I am at a loss. I am a long time user of Ubuntu and a long time linux gamer and CANNOT get the nvidia driver to install. I have tried every way I know to make this work and still no good. Now if I run nvidia installer,  or the sctipt here I keep getting the error that the nvidia module connot be found. What kind of issues is dapper having with this? I had this driver running under Breezy but no go here.
 I have installed the K7 kernel for my AMD proc. That should not have issues I wouldn't think.

 Please any advice would be nice.[/QUOTE]
1) Can you post the model of your graphic card?

2) Can you help me to diagnose the problem?
Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by rpesq on 2006-06-03
Hi tseliot,

Your AMD64 script worked great for me.  (Athlon64, 1 GB ram, Geforce 6100 integrated video).  Thanks for the excellent work you have done!

One question -- based upon past experience with the nvidia drivers and Suse, I always had to lower to color depth from 24 bit to 16 bit, or else I get flickering on the monitor.  ...No idea why, but that what would always happen. In sax2, that was easy to do, you could set the resolution, refresh, color depth, and a few other things. So far the flickering has not occurred much, but I would like to know how to drop the color depth, in case this happens with Ubuntu also. ### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-04
[QUOTE=crane]OK sorry for the lack of info, I was a bit frustrated. It has something to do with the K7 kernel. I booted into the stock kernel and nvidia works fine. 
 The error was that the nvidia.ko module could not be found.
 I am running an fx6600 card.
Now if I can figure out how to get that silly module in the K7 working. I would really prefer running the kernel built for my chip.[/QUOTE]
If you're using the driver from method 2 or from my script then you have to boot in your new kernel and install the driver again.

If you're using method 1 you have to install the restricted modules for your new kernel. In other words boot in your new kernel and type: sudo apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-04
[QUOTE=rpesq]Hi tseliot,

Your AMD64 script worked great for me.  (Athlon64, 1 GB ram, Geforce 6100 integrated video).  Thanks for the excellent work you have done!

One question -- based upon past experience with the nvidia drivers and Suse, I always had to lower to color depth from 24 bit to 16 bit, or else I get flickering on the monitor.  ...No idea why, but that what would always happen. In sax2, that was easy to do, you could set the resolution, refresh, color depth, and a few other things.  In Ubuntu, the only options that I see are the options to set screen resolution and refresh rate, but nothing regarding color depth.

So far the flickering has not occurred much, but I would like to know how to drop the color depth, in case this happens with Ubuntu also.  If you know how to do this, please advise.... and thanks again.  I had first tried to install the nvidia driver with EasyUbuntu, and it broke X and I had to reinstall.[/QUOTE]
You have to edit the file manually:
sudo nano -w /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Get to the "Section Screen" and  you will see something like this (just an example):
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier  	"Screen[0]"
    Device      	"Device[0]"
    Monitor     	"Monitor[0]"
    DefaultDepth    [COLOR="Red"]24[/COLOR]
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       15
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

Change the number in red to your desired Depth

### Post by OffHand on 2006-06-04
tseliot, first of all thnx for the great script. It's awesome.

I got 2 questions though.

1) Why does your script remove the restricted modules and can they be restored after installing the drivers with your script?

2) I found this under the Section "Screen":
```
Option         "RenderAccel" "true"
```
Shouldn't this be under Section "Device" ?
Also I don't see the pcibus under Section "Device" in my xorg.conf, but my card still works.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-04
[QUOTE=subsonic_shadow]tseliot, first of all thnx for the great script. It's awesome.

I got 2 questions though.

1) Why does your script remove the restricted modules and can they be restored after installing the drivers with your script?[/QUOTE]
No, because the restricted modules have nvidia-kernel-common as a dependency, which in turn can cause a driver mismatch (unless you're using driver 8762.[/QUOTE]

[QUOTE=subsonic_shadow]2) I found this under the Section "Screen":
```
Option         "RenderAccel" "true"
```
Shouldn't this be under Section "Device" ?
Also I don't see the pcibus under Section "Device" in my xorg.conf, but my card still works.[/QUOTE]
My script uses nvidia-xconfig to set up your xorg.conf. It's unlikely that it put a "RenderAccel" "true" (which is already enabled by default).

And the BUSId seems not to be required for every card.

---

### Post by OffHand on 2006-06-04
Thanks for clearing that up. So if I understand it right, I can use the restricted modules together with the nvidia driver as long as it is the 8762 driver?

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-04
[QUOTE=subsonic_shadow]Thanks for clearing that up. So if I understand it right, I can use the restricted modules together with the nvidia driver as long as it is the 8762 driver?[/QUOTE]
Yes, but only in that case, as the version of the nvidia kernel common (8762) will match the driver of the Nvidia installer (8762). But that's just a coincidence.

---

[QUOTE=slimb]updated to dapper yesterday.   then fought with the nvidia drivers (8762 - or whatever is the most up to date from the Nvidia site).

followed your instructions to a T (multiple times).  each time gdm won't start.

it fails with some module references to glx and nvidia.  this is using the K7 kernel, if i fall back to the reference drivers (nothing more than changing nvidia to nv in xorg.conf) i can load GDM fine.

i'm going to do some more testing this week as i have the time as i saw reference to others with the same issue (problems with the k7 kernel).[/QUOTE]
Ok, let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-06
[QUOTE=mikecalder]Method 1 failed for me.

AMD Athlon 64 3400+, A7V600/K8V-X mobo with 2G memory, Nvidia GeForce 6800 Ultra, 2x 400GB IDEs, etc.  Ubuntu Live CD downloaded yesterday.

After Ctrl-Alt-backspace, xserver fails to restart, with msgs at end:

Failed to load the NVIDIA kernel
Aborting
Unload module nvidia
Unload module randac
Unload module fb
Screens found, but none have a usable configuration
Fatal server error
no screens found.

? 

Why don't my screens have usable configuration? What do I have to do to make sure that they do?[/QUOTE]
Let's try to diagnose the error (make sure the driver is set to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf) :
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-07
[QUOTE=idcard_1]rpesq,

i have the integrated geforce 6100 but i cannot even get the installer to display.  the default nv driver won't work at all.  i eventually switched to fc 5 x86_64, and now since 6.06 has been released and promptly installed it on my toshiba laptop i want to try and get it on my amd 64 desktop.  how did you get the boot cd to load on the 6100?

thanks.[/QUOTE]
Download the Alternate-install instead of the Desktop edition of Ubuntu.

In this way you should be able to install Ubuntu.

After you install it you will have to type (when the xserver crashes)
```
sudo nano -w /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

and set the driver to "vesa" in the Section Device the file.

If you have further problems, don't hesitate to ask.

### Post by georgevn on 2006-06-07
hi tseliot,
for the Dapper 6.0.6 release, i already have gcc-4.0 installed in my box, do i need to install gcc-3.4 as stated in your howto? any differences between them?

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-07
[QUOTE=georgevn]hi tseliot,
for the Dapper 6.0.6 release, i already have gcc-4.0 installed in my box, do i need to install gcc-3.4 as stated in your howto? any differences between them?[/QUOTE]
No, you don't need gcc-3.4.

gcc-3.4 is needed by the users who run Dapper with a Breezy kernel or with a kernel such as 2.6.12 or lower (which is not your case)

### Post by chameleon_789 on 2006-06-07
Has this been mentioned before? 

I'm using the dapper 6.06 RC live cd, which I installed to the hard-drive and updated to the latest release.

When I install the nvidia drivers (8762) using method 2 (using -x--prefix=/usr/lib/xorg) the paths get messed up, and files that are supposed to be installed to the directory **/usr/lib/xorg/modules** get installed into **/usr/lib/xorg/lib**. Once I copy the files to their correct locations, most 3D programs that I've tried work (with the exception of Doom 3, but that might be an error on my part), but nvidia-settings gives me this output :

```
ERROR: NV-CONTROL extension not found on this Display.

ERROR: Unable to determine number of NVIDIA GPUs on ':0.0'.

ERROR: Unable to determine number of NVIDIA Frame Lock Devices on ':0.0'.
```

Is there some other way of installing the driver which won't mess up the directorys, and what exactly is nvidia-settings looking for (I assume some other files have been misplaced also)?

	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "stylus"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "stylus"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "eraser"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "eraser"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "cursor"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "cursor"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV20 [GeForce3 Ti 200]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"GDM-400PST"
	Option		"DPMS"
	HorizSync	30-94
	VertRefresh	50-75
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"NVIDIA Corporation NV20 [GeForce3 Ti 200]"
	Monitor		"GDM-400PST"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		16
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice     "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

### Post by finally on 2006-06-08
[QUOTE=Casey_Switzer]Can someone tell me why I only get 1024x768 as a max resolution?  I don't think the driver installed correctly.[/QUOTE]
Add the desired resolution in front of this lines.
```

        Modes      "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"

```
To get 1280 by 1024 they should look like this:
```

        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"

```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-08
[QUOTE=Raoul Duke]I am using a clean install of Dapper on my Toshiba Satellite 2450, GeForce4 440 Go...[/QUOTE]
Try this:
Boot in Recovery mode (choose it from the GRUB menu)

Type:
```
nano -w /etc/modprobe.d/options
```

Edit this option at the end of the file so as to make it look like:

```
options nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NVreg_Mobile=4
```

CTRL+X to exit

then restart your computer:
```
reboot
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-08
[QUOTE=icedtea]I booted in my previous kernel, and that error is not there. Also, with my kernel before that message there is one that says "serial8250: ttyS1 at I/O 0x2f8 (irq = 3) is a NS16550A". Anyway, I'm not sure if this error is related to the nvidia problem.

My graphics card is Geforce Go 6600 (laptop). By the way, I'm using method 1. When I do updatedb and locate nvidia.ko, I get:

/lib/modules/2.6.15.7-ubuntu1-custom/nvidia/nvidia.ko
/usr/src/modules/nvidia-kernel/debian/nvidia-kernel-2.6.15.7-ubuntu1-custom/lib/modules/2.6.15.7-ubuntu1-custom/nvidia/nvidia.ko

I also tried installing the restricted-modules for 686, it didn't help. Then I tried ln -s /lib/linux-restricted-modules/2.6.15-23-686 /lib/linux-restricted-modules/`uname -r` and that didn't help.[/QUOTE]

You can't use Method 1 with a recompiled kernel (because the restricted modules are available only for the kernels which come with Ubuntu by default.

Boot in your new kernel and do this:
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-kernel-2.6.15.7-ubuntu1-custom

sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-glx nvidia-kernel-common

Then try my script or Method 2

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-08
[QUOTE=Casey_Switzer]Okay, extreme newb warning here (pretty much a  first time linux user).

I used method two.  All appeared to execute successfully, but how do I determine if the install was actually successful?[/QUOTE]
Post the output of the following command:
```
dmesg | grep NVIDIA
```


About the resolution:

Set the refresh rate you would like to use with that resolution instead of the part in red. For example, if you wanted to use a resolution of 75Hz you should write "_75" ( "_60" for 60Hz)

```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV41.1 [GeForce 6800]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       15
        Modes      "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1024x768[COLOR="Red"]_60[/COLOR]" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection


```

  Identifier    "eraser"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "eraser"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "cursor"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "cursor"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV25 [GeForce4 Ti 4600]"
	Driver		"nv"
	BusID		"PCI:3:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"AOC Spectrum"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"NVIDIA Corporation NV25 [GeForce4 Ti 4600]"
	Monitor		"AOC Spectrum"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		16
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice     "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection[CODE]

And this is the modification I made from following the nvidia guide.


```# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
	# path to defoma fonts
	FontPath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"gb"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"		"ExplorerPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"		"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "stylus"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "stylus"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "eraser"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "eraser"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "cursor"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "cursor"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV25 [GeForce4 Ti 4600]"
	Driver		"nv"
	BusID		"PCI:3:0:0"
EndSection



    Section "Device"
        Identifier  "nvidia0"
        Driver      "nv"
        # Edit the BusID with the location of your graphics card
        BusID       "PCI:3:0:0"
        Screen      0
    EndSection

    Section "Device"
        Identifier  "nvidia1"
        Driver      "nv"
        # Edit the BusID with the location of your graphics card
        BusId       "PCI:3:0:0"
        Screen      1
    EndSection


Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Monitor0"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Monitor1"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

    Section "Screen"
        Identifier  "Screen0"
        Device      "nvidia0"
        Monitor     "Monitor0"
        DefaultDepth 24
        Subsection "Display"
            Depth       24
            Modes       "1600x1200" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480" 
        EndSubsection
    EndSection

    Section "Screen"
        Identifier  "Screen1"
        Device      "nvidia1"
        Monitor     "Monitor1"
        DefaultDepth 24
        Subsection "Display"
            Depth       24
            Modes       "1600x1200" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480" 
        EndSubsection
    EndSection


Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		0 "Screen0"
	Screen		1 "Screen1" leftof "Screen0"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice     "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection[/CODE]

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-08
[QUOTE=Blind-Summit]Awesome. OK - that's installed and I changed the setup to LeftOf to get my screens the right way around.

I now have: 
[/QUOTE]
Try setting the refresh rate of t
```
Option         "MetaModes" "1280x1024, 1600x1200[COLOR="Red"]_60[/COLOR]; 1280x1024, 1280x1024; NULL,1600x1200; NULL,1280x1024"
```

Set the refresh rate of that monitor in the part in red. For example if you want to use a 60Hz Refresh rate you have to write "_60" ("_75" for 75Hz and so on).

Then restart the xserver.

---

### Post by mikecalder on 2006-06-09
[QUOTE=tseliot]Can you try my script? (Make sure you don't use a WIFI card for your internet connection)[/QUOTE]

OK. Fix in documentation needed.

I bit the bullet and installed Ubuntu over my Suse partition (I tried to resize to get a clean partition to install in and hosed my Suse installation because resizing a reiserfs partition is a can of worms, but that's another story.

Method 1 now works fine with my config, AMD64 and GeForce 6800 Ultra.

So presumably the reason why it didn't before was something to do with the different environment between a disk install and using the Live CD.

So you need to clearly document that the process may not work under the CD, which is a shame, because one of the main reasons for running from the CD is to check whether or not Ubuntu will work properly with your hardware.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-09
[QUOTE=jdk_]Hi (again) tseliot,

I installed the driver using method 1 and just noticed some strange behaviour when running glxgears:
   1. The benchmarks are not shown in the terminal[/QUOTE]
Glxgears is not a benchmark. Perhaps you meant this command:
```
glxgears -printfps
```

[QUOTE=jdk_]   2. When glxgears is running the computer produces a constant noise at a high tone, I never heard this before, it is definitely not the usual noise made by the GPU.[/QUOTE]
Glxgears stresses your computer a bit. Perhaps it's just the fan which makes that noise. I have no idea.

[QUOTE=jdk_]By the way, I don't like to bring up such silly questions but I have searched my filesystem for the icon of nvidia-settinngs and it isn't there. Just to help you remember[/QUOTE]
If you pm me your email address I'll send you the icon.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-09
[QUOTE=orbadelic]Installed Ubuntu Desktop 6.06TLS today and tried to get the nvidia driver up and running using your method 1, but I ran into some problems: 

did the:

```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
```

then went to do the:

```
sudo nvidia-xconfig
```

and it told me the file could not be found, when trying to install it, ```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-xconfig
``` I recieved the error that it was referenced by other packages but missing. 

I then replaced nv with nvidia, and restarted x, and it says it cannot find the nvidia driver... 

what am I missing?[/QUOTE]
nvidia-glx includes nvidia-xconfig.

Make sure you have all the repositories enabled.

You can check that in the following way
```
sudo nano -w /etc/apt/sources.list
```

### Post by crocd on 2006-06-10
there seems to be an error with the script for method2 for x86_64 for dapper drake. I tried it today and it failed as it was trying to download and install the x86 version of the installer.

This is in the beginning of the script;
"#!/bin/bash
 # "Envy"
 # Written by Alberto Milone (aka "tseliot")
 # Licence GPL
 # Version 0.36
 # For Ubuntu Dapper Drake 32 bit ONLY!!!
 ARCA=x86
 VERA=1.0-8762
 PACA=pkg1
 DRIVER=NVIDIA-Linux-$ARCA-$VERA-$PACA.run
 MD5OLD=86bcf4a3a9d441dff9e25b82ec8a6060
 NVIDIAFTP=Linux-$ARCA/$VERA"

[http://www.albertomilone.eu/ubuntu/nvidia/scripts/envy_8762_64](http://www.albertomilone.eu/ubuntu/nvidia/scripts/envy_8762_64)

Shouldnt the ARCA read x86_64. I changed it on my downloaded script which seemed to have resolved the issue.

### Post by gborzi on 2006-06-10
Hello to everyone,
I'm having the blank screen problem on my amd64 that is equipped with a GeForce 6200 TurboCache on the PCI-E slot. It worked fine with breezy x86_64 with the default drivers (7667), and also works with the 32-bit kororaa 0.2, which uses version 8178 of the nvidia drivers.
I have tried methods 1 and 2, the latter with version 8762, 8178 and 7667 of the drivers to no avail. The nv driver works, but I cannot switch to console with Ctrl+Alt+F[1-6]. Moreover, after the blank screen I'm able to ssh into the system, but after stopping gdm, X is still active. If I kill it, the screen becomes white with some dark strips. After this, no console and no Xwindow even with the nv driver. I have to reboot.
Now I'm trying to compile a kernel without SMP support. I suspect SMP support can cause the problem because under breezy I used a 2.6.15 kernel (because dma didn't worked with 2.6.12 - it's a known issue of the Asrock 939S56-M mobo), and the drivers worked fine. The nvidia-bug-report.log is in attachment. Any help will be appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-10
[QUOTE=gborzi]Hello to everyone,
I'm having the blank screen problem on my amd64 that is equipped with a GeForce 6200 TurboCache on the PCI-E slot. It worked fine with breezy x86_64 with the default drivers (7667), and also works with the 32-bit kororaa 0.2, which uses version 8178 of the nvidia drivers.
I have tried methods 1 and 2, the latter with version 8762, 8178 and 7667 of the drivers to no avail. The nv driver works, but I cannot switch to console with Ctrl+Alt+F[1-6]. Moreover, after the blank screen I'm able to ssh into the system, but after stopping gdm, X is still active. If I kill it, the screen becomes white with some dark strips. After this, no console and no Xwindow even with the nv driver. I have to reboot.
Now I'm trying to compile a kernel without SMP support. I suspect SMP support can cause the problem because under breezy I used a 2.6.15 kernel (because dma didn't worked with 2.6.12 - it's a known issue of the Asrock 939S56-M mobo), and the drivers worked fine. The nvidia-bug-report.log is in attachment. Any help will be appreciated.

Thanks in advance.[/QUOTE]
I'll read the file you attached.

### Post by gborzi on 2006-06-11
A small update about my attempts to get the nvidia drivers working on amd64: the recompiled kernel, without SMP support, doesn't work, like the default one. Searching in the nvidia linux forums I have found someone with a similar problem. In his case the architecture is i386, the video card is different, but it's on PCI-E like mine.
Also, I noticed that when I try to start Xorg with the nvidia drivers it takes almost 100% CPU, and sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop doesn't stop it.
With the nv driver Xorg seems to work.

---

Thanks[/QUOTE]
Try this:

```
sudo cp /usr/src/name_of_your_source/arch/i386/Makefile.cpu /usr/src/kernel-headers-`uname -r`/arch/i386/
```

NOTE: you have to replace "name_of_your_source" with the name of your source, such as linux-2.6.16, etc.)

```
cd /usr/src/kernel-headers-`uname -r`/
```

```
sudo make prepare
```

```
sudo make prepare scripts
```

Then run my script again

### Post by HankB on 2006-06-13
[QUOTE=tseliot]I have no experience of using two cards at the same time (SLI ? ).

Anyhow installing the driver would not be a bad idea. Then, after installing your card you should configure your card manually in your xorg.conf.[/QUOTE]

I don't know what SLI is. (OK, google.. ;) ) No, not SLI. I have two Nvidia cards. An AGP card driving my main monitor and now a PCI card to drive an HDTV via a DVI connection. This is in an AMD 64 system (though I have 32 bit dapper installed to sidestep the headaches associated with running a 64 bit system today.)

I've got the second head working pretty much (except for the desired resolution and some other quirks) and this is the process I followed:

1) install the second card (still using nv drivers.) Note: If you have your BIOS set to initialize PCI video cards first and you can't see the display connected to your PCI video card, you will be pulling the PCI card to change the BIOS before you proceed. :rolleyes: )

2) Modify the xorg.conf to configure the second monitor. This took a couple of iterations, but was relatively straight forward after having looked at all of the other dual head posts.

3) Fiddle with the system a bit just to make sure everything works. It mostly does (*)

4) Follow the procedure #1 to upgrade to the nvidia drivers. This step went without problems (Thanks!)

5) Fiddle with the system a bit more to make sure everything still works. It does, more or less (*)


(*) The more or less part falls into two categories. First, I'm not 100% satisfied that the second head is working optimally. Second, I'm still wrestling a bit with the apps I'm using to view output on the screen, but I think that's a subject for another thread.

The second head is an HDTV driven by a Geforce 5200 FX card. The Specs for the TV are here: [http://support.gateway.com/s/CsmrEltrncs/DigitalTV/2517984/2517984sp10.shtml](http://support.gateway.com/s/CsmrEltrncs/DigitalTV/2517984/2517984sp10.shtml)
And supported video modes are here: [http://support.gateway.com/s/CsmrEltrncs/DigitalTV/Shared/2517984faq50.shtml](http://support.gateway.com/s/CsmrEltrncs/DigitalTV/Shared/2517984faq50.shtml)

Using both the nv and nvidia drivers, the screen runs at 800x600 (it reports M06 when I restart the X server.) The relavent information from my xorg.conf is:

```
Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    Screen         "HDTV Screen" LeftOf "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
    Option         "Clone" "Off"
EndSection
Section "Monitor"

			# for NEC XP21
			#Horizontal: 31 to 89kHz/Vertical: 55 to 160Hz
    Identifier     "NEC XP21"
    HorizSync       31.0 - 89.0
    VertRefresh     55.0 - 160.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "HDTV"
    Option         "DPMS"
#	DisplaySize	852 480
#	HorizSync	31-89
#	VertRefresh	50-85
	# for GTW-P42M403 Plasma TV
	# what we really want is the native resolution:
	#     852x480 (mode 23)
	# Mode 	Horizontal 	Vertical 	Format 		Refresh
	# 23 	31.4 KHz 	60.0 Hz 	852 × 480 (WVGA) 	60
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV18 [GeForce4 MX 4000 AGP 8x]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    BusID          "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    BusID          "PCI:0:10:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "HDTV Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
    Monitor        "HDTV"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "920x520" "852x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```


I've left out sections for the primary monitor since it works fine - as it did before.

My preference is to drive the display at it's native resolution. According to the Gateway specs, this is 852x480 (corresponding to their mode 23) The nv driver probes the monitor and seems to find:

```

(WW) NVIDIA(1):     "nvidia-auto-select".
(WW) NVIDIA(1): 
(II) NVIDIA(1): Validated modes:
(II) NVIDIA(1):     "nvidia-auto-select"
(II) NVIDIA(1): Virtual screen size determined to be 800 x 600
(--) NVIDIA(1): DPI set to (22, 29); computed from "UseEdidDpi" X config option
(--) Depth 24 pixmap format is 32 bpp

```

So... Somewhere I got the impression that the dislay was telling the card it supported 920x520, but now I see that's just the size of the display. 

Anyway, should I settle for 800x600 or should I at least try for something that matches the 16:9 aspect ratio? Is there something I'm missing to be able to do that?

Another questoin involves color saturation. The same DVD played on the screen from a DVD player looks more saturated than when the ripped vob is played using gxine. The difference is subtle and I'm not sure which I prefer. Compared to the gxine dislay, the DVD dislay may actually be oversaturated. But then again, my 'test material' is Shrek and a cartoon may not be the best material for comparison sake. And some of the skin tones do look a bit greenish. :D

<edit>
I just noticed that the nvidia utility reports that the card only has 64MB RAM. That matches with what the driver reports (search for VideoRam parameter.) But lspci seems to report 128 which is what is marked on the box:
```
0000:00:0a.0 VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200] (rev a1) (prog-if 00 [VGA])
        Subsystem: eVga.com. Corp.: Unknown device b309
        Flags: bus master, 66MHz, medium devsel, latency 248, IRQ 201
        Memory at f8000000 (32-bit, non-prefetchable) [size=16M]
**        Memory at f0000000 (32-bit, prefetchable) [size=128M]**
        Expansion ROM at 50020000 [disabled] [size=128K]
        Capabilities: [60] Power Management version 2
```

</edit>


Thanks,
hank

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-14
[QUOTE=HankB])Anyway, should I settle for 800x600 or should I at least try for something that matches the 16:9 aspect ratio? Is there something I'm missing to be able to do that?[/QUOTE]
See the **Problems Section** of my guide. There are 2 points which deal with the screen resolution.

Perhaps you only need to set the right modeline

### Post by frenchtomytoast on 2006-06-14
[QUOTE=tseliot]Try point 5 of the Problems Section then[/QUOTE]

I gave that a whirl and it didn't help either. 

The big problem I have is that when I stop gnome all I get is a black screen without command prompts. Any thoughts?

### Post by xyz321 on 2006-06-14
Hello all.

I am having a problem with starting gdm after using tseliot's script. I use a Geforce4 MX 460. When I start gdm, the screen turns black and nothing happens after "NVIDIA(0): Enabling RENDER acceleration" has been printed to xorg.0.log. However, I can still login through ssh.

Additionally, I have a similar problem to that which frenchtomytoast has. When I stop gdm through ssh, I get a black screen and cannot access any terminals. This only occurs when I had been trying to use the nvidia drivers.

Any ideas?

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-14
[QUOTE=frenchtomytoast]I gave that a whirl and it didn't help either. 

The big problem I have is that when I stop gnome all I get is a black screen without command prompts. Any thoughts?[/QUOTE]
See point 13 of the problems section of my guide. It's a common bug

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-14
[QUOTE=xyz321]Hello all.

I am having a problem with starting gdm after using tseliot's script. I use a Geforce4 MX 460. When I start gdm, the screen turns black and nothing happens after "NVIDIA(0): Enabling RENDER acceleration" has been printed to xorg.0.log. However, I can still login through ssh.

Additionally, I have a similar problem to that which frenchtomytoast has. When I stop gdm through ssh, I get a black screen and cannot access any terminals. This only occurs when I had been trying to use the nvidia drivers.

Any ideas?

### Post by AceRimmer on 2006-06-16
Thanks a lot, your methods finally got my 6600GT running with 3d!  However I have a question.  In your guide you list the solution to the problem of the system locking up while the mouse still moves.  
> 
4) If you have an AGP graphic card and your system freezes but you can still move the mouse pointer you will have to do this:
file.pngCode:

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf


Add the lines which begin with the word "Option" in this section of the file:
file.pngCode:

Section "Device" Identifier "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6200 TurboCache]" 
Driver "nvidia" 
BusID "PCI:1:0:0" 
Option "NvAGP" "0" 
Option "RenderAccel" "Off" 
Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV" 
Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off" 
Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off" EndSection


Then save the file and exit. Press CTRL+ALT+Backspace in order to restart the xserver. 

Ok I did that and BANG!  It works fine and doesn't lock up.  However when I open the Nvidia control panel I see the card is listed as a PCI card.  Does this mean that things like AGP texturing is not going to work?  Will this now just run as an 8x PCI card instead of a full featured AGP card?

Thanks again for the help!

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-16
[QUOTE=AceRimmer]However when I open the Nvidia control panel I see the card is listed as a PCI card.  Does this mean that things like AGP texturing is not going to work?  Will this now just run as an 8x PCI card instead of a full featured AGP card?[/QUOTE]
Usually it runs as an AGP, regardless of what is reported.

BUT the Option "NvAGP" "0" (which you need and already set in your xorg.conf) disables AGP.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-16
[QUOTE=Morce] I awnsered a question if i wanted to instal nvidia drivers, i said "i" and then it asked me if i wanted to change de xorg file, i said y but it said it couldn't.[/QUOTE]
Are you sure of it?

[QUOTE=Morce]Is it or is it not installed, as far as you can understand, if you can understand waht i'm saing.[/QUOTE]
Please open Terminal or Konsole, type the following commands and post the respective outputs:
```
dmesg | grep NVIDIA
```

```
glxinfo | grep direct
```


[QUOTE=Morce]And by de way, where the hell is the nvidia control panel ?!?!?!?!?!?!?! I cant find anything with that name :\
Thank you in advance. (sory for bad english, not main language)[/QUOTE]
If you're using GNOME:
click on the following menus: 
Applications/System tools/NVIDIA Xserver settings

		Modes		"1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice     "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-17
[QUOTE=weasel fierce]This is with the original (working) xorg. Let me know if you need it with the broken one (after reconfigure), though in that case, I'd need the command for dumping the above into a text file.[/QUOTE]
Set the BusID to "PCI:1:5:0" in your xorg.conf:

```
BusID "PCI:1:5:0"
```


And restart the xserver:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart
```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-18
[QUOTE=namnam]- Often I had no idea of what I was doing. E.g. when removing the restricted modules, it would be nice to know what they were and why they had to be removed.[/QUOTE]
I would like to provide as much information as possible but many users made me notice that my guide is too long. Therefore making it even longer might not be a good idea.

BTW the restricted modules are modules (drivers, such as the nvidia driver) compiled for your kernel. You had to remove them in order to avoid driver mismatches with the nvidia driver.

[QUOTE=namnam]- On my box there was no "/usr/src/kernel-headers" but a "/usr/src/linux-headers" and a "/usr/include/linux".[/QUOTE]
Perhaps you refer to the part which says "OTHERWISE if you are using a kernel which you compiled from vanilla sources (i.e. from kernel.org) then you have two options"

That's meant for recompiled kernels. If you didn't recompile one then you didn't have to follow that part.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-19
[B][COLOR="Red"]Envy v 0.38 has been released
[/COLOR][/B]
I warmly recommend you to get the latest version.

**FIXES:**
* support for recompiled kernels (it was broken)
* support for (recompiled) kernel 2.6.14 or higher (it needed some tweaking before because of some changes in the kernel)

**NEW FEATURES:**
* support for Kernel 2.6.17

### Post by ultima on 2006-06-19
I chose method 2 (kind of) and downloaded the newest drivers from the
Nvidia website.

I already had linux-headers, build-essential, and gcc installed so I went
straight into it

```
Ctrl+alt+F1 (command line)
```

then ran
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (or gdm if your running gnome to stop the x server)
```

Then I ran the Nvidia script. It installed the driver then updated and
backed up xorg.conf
```
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8762-pkg1.run
```

then I just had to copy nvidia_drv.so and libglx.so to the xorg modules
directory.

```
sudo cp /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/drivers/nvidia_drv.so
/usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers
```
```
sudo cp /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/extensions/libglx.so
/usr/lib/xorg/modules/extensions/
```

then I ran
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (or gdm if your running gnome to restart x.)
```

Worked for me on kubuntu Dapper, (fresh install) with GeForce4 MX 440,
running on kernel 2.6.17 using Nvidia driver 8762.

---

### Post by HankB on 2006-06-19
Just a heads up. After the most recent upgrade (and reboot) my X server did not start. The error in the log was 
```
(EE) NVIDIA(0): Failed to load the NVIDIA kernel module!
(EE) NVIDIA(0):  *** Aborting ***

```

The upgrade had installed linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-25-**386** but not linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-25-**k7** and I'm running the K7 flavor of the kernel. After manual installation of the package X started normally.

thanks,
hank

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-19
[QUOTE=HankB]Just a heads up. After the most recent upgrade (and reboot) my X server did not start. The error in the log was 
```
(EE) NVIDIA(0): Failed to load the NVIDIA kernel module!
(EE) NVIDIA(0):  *** Aborting ***

```

The upgrade had installed linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-25-**386** but not linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-25-**k7** and I'm running the K7 flavor of the kernel. After manual installation of the package X started normally.

thanks,
hank[/QUOTE]
Just to prevent that error (for future upgrades) type:
```
sudo apt-get install linux-k7
```

In this way the dummy package will update your headers and restricted modules every time your kernel is updated.

And BTW the solution to your problem was posted here:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#WHAT_HAPPENS_IF_YOU_CHANGE_YOUR_KERNEL_OR_IF_YOUR_KERNEL_IS_UPDATED]("http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#WHAT_HAPPENS_IF_YOU_CHANGE_YOUR_KERNEL_OR_IF_YOUR_KERNEL_IS_UPDATED")

---

### Post by HankB on 2006-06-19
[QUOTE=tseliot]Just to prevent that error (for future upgrades) type:
```
sudo apt-get install linux-k7
```

In this way the dummy package will update your headers and restricted modules every time your kernel is updated.
[/quote]

Ahh... I thought I was missing something. Thanks!
> 
And BTW the solution to your problem was posted here:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#WHAT_HAPPENS_IF_YOU_CHANGE_YOUR_KERNEL_OR_IF_YOUR_KERNEL_IS_UPDATED]("http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#WHAT_HAPPENS_IF_YOU_CHANGE_YOUR_KERNEL_OR_IF_YOUR_KERNEL_IS_UPDATED")
And I'm sure I would have found that had I looked, but I thought I knew the immediate problem as soon as I saw the message in the log. And now I've fixed the ultimate problem (missing dummy package.)

Thanks!
hank

BTW, I did finally get two independant X servers working. It wasn't easy. Part of the solution was what is done to handle multiple users and the other part was to figure out how to specify dummy keyboard and mouse for the display only server.

### Post by heroticpaladin on 2006-06-20
Hello,

2 days ago I installed Ubuntu 6.06 ; it was my first Linux experience.
Everything was great! I loved it and even erazed my win xp partition! =D> 

Until I installed my nvidia drivers. #-o 

I've a GeForce4 420Go, I followed the guide.
I went to synapse, and installed the nvidia-glx package.

Then I opened a terminal window and entered: *sudo nvidia-glx-config enable*.
Then I had an error message, and the system told me to enter a command, or to manualy edit the config file to replace nv by nvidia.
I entered the command the system told me to, then entered *sudo vidia-glx-config enable* again, but this time it worked.

The system also gave me a command to cancel this new config and to set back the native config. But as everything had been so clean sor far, I thought it would be useless, and I didn't write it on a memo ... =D> 

Then I reboot, but I could not load the graphic interface ... ](*,) 
And as I had not memo-ed the command to set back the native config, I was fried like a Linux newbee.:-\" 

--

Now, I've a fresh Ubuntu 6.06 installation.

I go to synapse, install the nvidia-glx package, open the terminal window enter *sudo nvidia-glx-config enable*, then I enter the comand the system tells me to enter, and I enter sudo *nvidia-glx-config enable* one more time.

I reboot, and like beforem the graphic interface doesn't work.
(but this time I memo-ed the command to set back the native config).

--

My question is : as I already download the nividia-glx drivers from synapse, can I go directly to the Problem #7 of your HowTo web page?

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-20
> **heroticpaladin]Hello,

2 days ago I installed Ubuntu 6.06  said:**
> (*,) 
And as I had not memo-ed the command to set back the native config, I was fried like a Linux newbee.:-\" 

--

Now, I've a fresh Ubuntu 6.06 installation.

I go to synapse, install the nvidia-glx package, open the terminal window enter *sudo nvidia-glx-config enable*, then I enter the comand the system tells me to enter, and I enter sudo *nvidia-glx-config enable* one more time.

I reboot, and like beforem the graphic interface doesn't work.
(but this time I memo-ed the command to set back the native config).

--

My question is : as I already download the nividia-glx drivers from synapse, can I go directly to the Problem #7 of your HowTo web page?

Or should I cancel the new config, uninstall nvidia-glx, and follow your method 2 from the begining?


Greetings.
Is there any reason for which you're not following my guide?

Try Method 1 (read it carefully, it's not the same as you did) and then follow the point of the Problems section about your card.


A QUICK FIX to access the GNOME or KDE again:
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```
Select the "nv" driver and press ENTER to all the other questions if you don't know the answer.

Then restart the Xserver:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```

---

### Post by princemackenzie on 2006-06-20
First off, thanks so much for help with your guide.

However, whenever I try method 1 from a clean install, it doesn't work.  I just get a black screen - my monitor acts like it isn't receiving a signal at all.  I get the "no signal" message on my monitor and it goes to standby.  I then have to reboot back into single user mode and restore my old xorg.conf.  It's just the "nvidia" driver that does this... once I switch back to "nv" its fine (but without acceleration).

I'm thinking its an out of sync with the monitor and I need to run dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg to fix it... would that do it?  I'm at work and this problem has flummoxed me for 2 days now.  Any input would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.

### Post by heroticpaladin on 2006-06-20
Hi, thanks for your help.
The particular reasons why I did not follow your guide are:

-1- I first followed the Ubuntu 6.06 help file that came with the installation. And it said to download the nvidia-glx file from synapse.

-2- Then I discovered your guide and read this:

*METHOD 1 It is the usual (and easiest) way to install the Nvidia drivers. It allows you to install nvidia driver 8762 OR 7174, i.e. **the Legacy driver** that you need ONLY IF your graphic card belongs to the list in the "NOTES SECTION", which you will find near the end of the guide. [WARMLY RECOMMENDED TO NEWBIES]. It works only with the kernels which you can find in Ubuntu's repository.*

But I could not access the list. I knew that the legacy driver was not for GeForce4 420Go. So I guessed It was not my case.

*METHOD 2 **If you need another driver version (e.g. the latest version if it is not available in the Ubuntu's repositories)** or you have problems with Method 1 or you are using a kernel you have compiled yourself (thus lacking the restricted modules required for method one) or you want to try something different then Method 2 is for you. This Method works ONLY with driver 8756 and 8762! *

I needed the nvidia-glx version, and it was available from the repositories.
So I guessed it was not my case either.


As I thought I was in neather cases, I planned to install the nvidia-glx package from synapse, and to go to problem #7.


Now, I have a fresh new Ubuntu 6.06 installation, and I will follow EXACTLY your guide: method 1, then problem 7, then reboot, and I will let you know.

Thank you.

### Post by heroticpaladin on 2006-06-20
> The particular reasons why I did not follow your guide are:

METHOD 1 It is the usual (and easiest) way to install the Nvidia drivers. **It allows you to install nvidia driver 8762 OR 7174, i.e. the Legacy driver** that you need ONLY IF your graphic card belongs to the list in the "NOTES SECTION", which you will find near the end of the guide. [WARMLY RECOMMENDED TO NEWBIES]. It works only with the kernels which you can find in Ubuntu's repository.

But now I realise it mean't: "It allows you to install the nvidia driver 8762 or the nvidia driver 7174, i.e. the Legacy driver."

-

> The Index in the Wiki is quite clear:
Quote:
* 1 HOWTO: Latest NVIDIA drivers
o 1.1 REQUIREMENTS
o 1.2 EXPLANATION OF THE 3 METHODS
o 1.3 WHAT HAPPENS IF YOU CHANGE YOUR KERNEL OR IF YOUR KERNEL IS UPDATED
o 1.4 WHAT TO DO IF THE METHOD YOU CHOSE DID NOT WORK OR WORKED PARTIALLY
o 1.5 METHOD 1
o 1.6 METHOD 2
o 1.7 HOW TO UNINSTALL THE DRIVER (FROM METHOD 2)
o 1.8 METHOD 3
o 1.9 NOTES SECTION
o 1.10 PROBLEMS SECTION
* 2 Notes

Yes it is very clear. In fact I am able to go to the notes section, but I do not see any list there.
