### Post by FisherPrice on 2006-06-21
[QUOTE=tseliot]You're free not to believe me. I just need you to be as clear as possible when you describe the problem.

1) Did you use my script, method 1 or both and in which did you try first?

2) Try point 4 of the problems section of my guide (even if your problem doesn't match the one described in that point)[/QUOTE]

1) I tried everything and I don't remember which order

2) OK, I've got the card working now, but I thought that I would post up what I had to do.

I did as you mentioned, I looked at the problem solving section of your document and had to add all the options including the "NoAccel"  option. However, the first dozen times I also removed the "GLcore" and "dri" references working off the assumption that you needed to. I have read a lot of posts and they all say that when you install the nvidia driver you need to comment out these lines.

This morning I thought that I would try inserting those lines again and voila I have a fully functioning nvidia driver and am now dling tux racer (or planet penguin as it's called now.)

Thank you for writting the how to. I hope that now that I have the xorg.conf file up and running that I can install the nvidia-glx file from the ubunto repos, that way I won't have to keep going to CLI when a security update is released.   :)

### Post by princemackenzie on 2006-06-22
**Success!**  :-D 

It was, as I had earlier suspected, an issue with the horizontal and vertical refresh rate on my monitor.  I went through the "dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg" path again, going through the advanced monitor dialog, and this time entering the values I could find on the web for my monitor, an Acer AL1715 (17" LCD with DVI).  Once I put those correct values in, voila, it worked perfectly.

This conclusion leads me to believe that if you continue to have "no input" problems then try that fix at least once.

On a side note, I think tseliot, whose guide has been so useful on this topic, may have the same monitor, as evidenced by this post.

[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=98456](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=98456)

---

hedge

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-27
**[COLOR="Red"] Envy v 0.39 has been released[/COLOR]**

FIXES:
* fixed wrong path to xorg in Ubuntu 64bit (thanks to some users of the Italian forum who made me notice)

CHANGES:
* merged "install" and "reinstall" functions. Now there are only "install" and "uninstall" are available. The install function is able to retrieve the installer from your hard disk (if the Envy script is in the same folder of the Nvidia installer) instead of just downloading the Nvidia installer again.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-06-27
I have added the support for the Legacy driver 7178 to my Envy script, please try it (if you have a card which requires a legacy driver)

    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to

Section "InputDevice"

    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

    Identifier     "DELL 1905FP"
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV43 [GeForce 6600/GeForce 6600 GT]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"

    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV43 [GeForce 6600/GeForce 6600 GT]"
    Monitor        "DELL 1905FP"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)

    InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"

EndSection

Section "Files"

	# path to defoma fonts
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "i2c"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "gb"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Synaptics Touchpad"
    Driver         "synaptics"
    Option         "SendCoreEvents" "true"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto-dev"
    Option         "HorizScrollDelta" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "eraser"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 51.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 60.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV17 [GeForce4 440 Go]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV17 [GeForce4 440 Go]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    16
	Option	"ExactModeTimingsDVI" "TRUE" 
	Option	"ModeValidation" "DFP-0: NoEdidDFPMaxSizeCheck, NoVesaModes"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1024x768"

        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
EndSection


```

It is a Dell P991 and is just about capable of 1600x1200, but I preferred it on about 1280ish. I don't know what standard resolutions are... Little help?

        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

Hope someone here can help me. Thanks (and sorry for any english errors)
Do you have an Internet connection in Ubuntu?
It looks like your kernel headers are not installed and that's weird since my script automatically installs them (provided you have an internet connection).

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-01
[QUOTE=ions]Downloaded the driver found here:

[http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_nforce_1.0-0310.html](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_nforce_1.0-0310.html)

It asked for:

```
 ERROR: You do not appear to have libc header files installed on your system.
         Please install your distribution's libc development package.

```

So I tried to get that:

```
$ sudo apt-get install libc
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
Package libc is not available, but is referred to by another package.
This may mean that the package is missing, has been obsoleted, or
is only available from another source
E: Package libc has no installation candidate

```[/QUOTE]
Try this:
```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-`uname -r` build-essential gcc gcc-3.4 xserver-xorg-dev
```

### Post by Kikoolol on 2006-07-01
Yes ! I have managed to get this to work :)

In fact, I gave the kernel source directory as an argument of the nvidia-installer command instead of the kernel headers directory, the module compiled well, but I still had the same error in the xorg log (no screen found).

Then I found on the Internet the reason why it wasn't working (usually I only run the nvidia installer to make it work, and I had the same problem). In fact, the nvidia installer (last version) copy the compiled module in the /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/drivers directory, while xorg is looking for it in the /usr/lib/xorg/modules dir.

So I ran the installer again directly, then copy the modules in the good directory, and YEAH o/

I don't remember that this is mentioned in the howto, I think it could help a lot :)

Thks for you help btw

KKl

---

I also received sometimes this warning message in Synaptic:
```
The following problems were found in your system!
W: Duplicate sources.list entry http://au.archive.ubuntu.com dapper/main Packages (/var/lib/apt/lists/au.archive.ubuntu.com_ubuntu_dists_dapper_main_binary-i386_Packages)

```

And assuming it have someting to do with the sources.list archive, here it goes:
```
deb http://au.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper main restricted universe multiverse
deb http://packages.freecontrib.org/ubuntu/plf/ breezy free non-free
# The above lines were generated automatically by EasyUbuntu 3.0 Beta Release
# The rest of your sources.list follows

# deb cdrom:[Ubuntu 6.06 _Dapper Drake_ - Release i386 (20060531)]/ dapper main restricted

## Major bug fix updates produced after the final release of the

## team.

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'backports'
## N.B. software from this repository may not have been tested as

deb-src http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse
deb http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security main restricted universe multiverse
deb-src http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security main restricted

### Post by Starmartyr on 2006-07-02
Ok, something that has helped was to add in loading for "dri", and "GLcore".

Section "Module"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "dbe"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "dri"  
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "GLcore"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "v4l"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Current glxgears after tweaking:

75505 frames in 5.1 seconds = 14943.314 FPS
76721 frames in 5.0 seconds = 15237.397 FPS

### Post by Starmartyr on 2006-07-03
CULPRIT FOUND!!!

I have installed your envy_8762_32 script and still had the same issue.  So, just as a check, I quit my gDesklets to see if that had anything to do with it, and it did.  gDesklets works great, but there is one gDesklet, **_Nvidia Thermal and Freqs_** ver 0.3, that creates this lag.  Who knew?!

Thanks for the support, and I hope this helps others.  Also, thank you for the great scripts and guides.

Cheers!!!\\:D/

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-04
[QUOTE=dimatrod]Thanks for the reply.  Also, congrats on italy passing to the final on the world cup (if you fancy football).[/QUOTE]
Thanks :D 

[QUOTE=dimatrod]Edit: BLAST!  It didn't work.  It says that X couldn't be started and gives me an error report and all.[/QUOTE]
Ok, let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-05
[QUOTE=D_0ne]It didn't work again...
I still get a black screen...[/QUOTE]
let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-05
[QUOTE=dimatrod]By this I suppose you ask if I typed nvidia-xconfig? (which I did).

Since then I had to reinstall ubuntu since I had to burn some stuff for my aunt's work that I had backed up here, so when I get back from doing my early morning stuff, I'll try once again.

Fake edit:  Is this XGL an option in xorg.conf?[/QUOTE]
Ok, if you don't know what XGL is, it is very likely that you didn't try to install it.

Can you try point 4 of the Problems Section?

Anyhow **if anything goes wrong **you can get back to the "nv" or the vesa driver (so as to be able to enter the Desktop Environment) by typing :
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

select the "nv" driver (or "vesa") and press ENTER whenever you don't know the answer to the other questions.

then restart the Xserver:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (or "kdm restart" if you use KDM)
```

---

---

### Post by joliet on 2006-07-06
I modified this line:
sudo ./nvidia-installer -n -s --x-prefix=/usr/lib/xorg/ --x-module-path=/usr/lib/xorg/modules --kernel-source-path=/usr/src/linux-headers-`uname -r`

Then the X is okey!:D 
Thanks


[QUOTE=tseliot]Try this:
```
sudo apt-get install xserver-xorg-dev pkg-config
```
and try my script again[/QUOTE]

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-06
[QUOTE=joliet]I modified this line:
sudo ./nvidia-installer -n -s --x-prefix=/usr/lib/xorg/ --x-module-path=/usr/lib/xorg/modules --kernel-source-path=/usr/src/linux-headers-`uname -r`

Then the X is okey!:D 
Thanks[/QUOTE]
Thanks for reporting. I'll see if my script needs to be fixed

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-06
**[COLOR="Red"][SIZE="3"]Envy 0.40 has been released[/SIZE][/COLOR]**

FIXES
* sometimes the Desktop Manager didn't stop but Ubuntu's bootsplash showed up and and remained idle.
* there was an error about line 65
* the output of some errors is now redirected to /dev/null

CHANGES
* changed the path to the modules (for the Nvidia installer) because some users had problems with that.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-07
> **nickless said:**
> How can I find out which release my drivers are? Does Automatix install the latest drivers?

```
dmesg | grep NVIDIA
```

Automatix installs the version available in Ubuntu's repositories (just like my Method 1).

Currently the latest driver (8762) matches with the one in the repos. But it's only a temporary thing (the one in the repos won't be updated)

### Post by pravuil on 2006-07-08
I believe I tried method 2 but there were conflicts I had which forced me to simplify the method. I'm not going to go into detail but I will go step by step. This was done on Ubuntu Dapper 6.06.

1 ) # sudo -s
2 ) # apt-get install linux-header-`uname -r` build-essential gcc gcc-3.4 xserver-xorg-dev module-assistant fakeroot dh-make debconf libstdc++5 gcc-3.3-base nvidia-kernel-source
3 ) # wget [http://www.nvidia.com/](http://www.nvidia.com/)(wherever the nvidia driver is located.)
4 ) # wget [http://www.nvidia.com/](http://www.nvidia.com/)(wherever the nforce driver is located.)
5 ) # /etc/init.d/gdm stop
6 ) # cd (to wherever the nvidia driver was downloaded)
7 ) # sh nvidia-installer -n
8 ) # sh nforce-installer -n
9 ) # module-assistant prepare
10 ) # module-assistant update
11 ) # module-assistant build nvidia
12 ) # module-assistant install nvidia
13 ) # depmod -a
14 ) # reboot

Everything installed smoothly but the NForce ethernet driver. Kernel wouldn't recognize the component after it was installed. Any suggestions of how to implement it without recompiling the kernel. Graphics acceleration was already configured which saved me a lot of headaches. Not sure if this will help anyone but it did help me out quite a bit. Just curious, I've also installed XGL for compiz (which works great btw), does this have any effect on how wine will run. It seems like it but I just want to make sure. By the way tseliot, keep up the great work, you saved my rear a couple of times and helped changed my perspective on how to attack certain problems.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-09
> **linkssj6 said:**
> I have the motherboard ASUS A8V Deluxe, and Athlon64 but i dont'n make install my GF 6600GT AGP, but my FX5200 work perfectly. I just change the card with new one.

I try use the options of boot= acpi acpi-off etc etc etc, and dont work.

I try to blacklist the AGP drive and dont work too.

In the windows system work if I intall the VIA 4 all 1
drive.

Tanks in advance.

Boot in RECOVERY MODE from the GRUB Menu almost as soon as you turn on your computer (it will take you to the command line).

Then you need to type:
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

and select the "vesa" driver. Press ENTER whenever you don't know the answer to a question.

4) Type:
```
reboot
```

The Xserver should work fine

### Post by pravuil on 2006-07-09
> Why did you use both the Nvidia installer and module-assistant?

First reason was I had problems with method 2. The nvidia-settings wouldn't display all of the options associated with the nvidia driver such as color-correction, antialiasing, and opengl preferences. I could hack the config file but I want to reduce the time I have to spend on changing  options. For the moment I prefer the manufacturer's driver over Ubuntu's, so method 1 was out of the question.

For using module-assistant with the installer, I had too because x would complain about a mismatch for the driver version in the kernel. Module-assistant changed the  entry from 7XXX within the kernel to the latest 8762 version without too much hassle. I've done this method for the past six system installs without a single error during the installation process. Plus acceleration is already preconfigured after install. Got the idea from a   [howto for installing the ATI driver]("http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Ubuntu_Dapper_Installation_Guide#Installing_the_new_driver").

Just curious why do you prefer to extract the files from the installer instead of running it by itself. Is it because of a conflict with a package required by Ubuntu, to avoid effecting the kernel, or is it to create a Ubuntu compliant package?

---

### Post by monomaniacpat on 2006-07-12
Hello again,

I need help to setup ubuntu to display for my Samsung HDTV through SVGA.

In the instruction manual for the TV it says the best resolution is 1360x768 @ 60hz. So, following the instructions on the Howto problem #10, I added:

```
Option "UseEDID" "False"
```

and changed the 1360 resolution to "1360x768_60".

However, doing this made all my resolutions 60hz, which was painful to the eyes. Furthermore, the number of available resolutions was reduced and my max was now something like 1124x768. So I made sure they all listed _85. Restarted x, and hey presto, all I could select was 800x600! Wow.

So, the question is: how can I get the xorg file to display at 1360x768 @ 60hz and retain my usual monitor's resolution of 1280x1024?

Here's my xorg.conf:

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildd@rothera)  Thu Jan  5 15:32:31 UTC 2006

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#

# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#

# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated

    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
    InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection

Section "Files"

	# path to defoma fonts

    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"

EndSection

    Load           "i2c"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "extmod"

    Load           "glx"

    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "gb"
EndSection

    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"

    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Synaptics Touchpad"
    Driver         "synaptics"
    Option         "SendCoreEvents" "true"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto-dev"
    Option         "HorizScrollDelta" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "eraser"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 51.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 85.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV17 [GeForce4 440 Go]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    Option	   "UseEDID" "False"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV17 [GeForce4 440 Go]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
Option "ExactModeTimingsDVI" 
Option "UseEdidDpi" "FALSE" Option "ModeValidation"
    SubSection     "Display"
   		Depth		1
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1440x900" "1400x1050" "1360x768" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1280x854" "1280x800" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1152x864" "1152x768" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1600x1200" "1440x900" "1400x1050" "1360x768" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1280x854" "1280x800" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1152x864" "1152x768" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8

	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15

```

```

    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "it"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "eraser"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Monitor Generico"
    DisplaySize     270    203
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 MX/MX 400]"
    Driver         "nv"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 MX/MX 400]"
    Monitor        "Monitor Generico"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480" "640x350"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4

0000:00:0d.1 Input device controller: Creative Labs SB Live! MIDI/Game Port (rev 08)
0000:00:0f.0 Ethernet controller: Realtek Semiconductor Co., Ltd. RTL-8139/8139C/8139C+ (rev 10)

```

tnx so much

Ok, let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-`uname -r` build-essential gcc gcc-3.4 xserver-xorg-dev
```

and then reinstall the driver.

```

i saw in Xorg.0.log ```
Build Operating System:Linux 2.6.12 i686
``` but i have an amd athlon... 
do i have to set anything on /usr/src/kernel-headers-2.6.15.7-ubuntu1-fiuman/arch/i386/Kconfig.cpu??? and how???

```
# Put here option for CPU selection and depending optimization
if !X86_ELAN

choice
	prompt "Processor family"
	default M686

config M386
	bool "386"
	---help---
	  This is the processor type of your CPU. This information is used for
	  optimizing purposes. In order to compile a kernel that can run on
	  all x86 CPU types (albeit not optimally fast), you can specify
	  "386" here.

	  The kernel will not necessarily run on earlier architectures than
	  the one you have chosen, e.g. a Pentium optimized kernel will run on
	  a PPro, but not necessarily on a i486.

	  Here are the settings recommended for greatest speed:
	  - "386" for the AMD/Cyrix/Intel 386DX/DXL/SL/SLC/SX, Cyrix/TI
	  486DLC/DLC2, UMC 486SX-S and NexGen Nx586.  Only "386" kernels
	  will run on a 386 class machine.
	  - "486" for the AMD/Cyrix/IBM/Intel 486DX/DX2/DX4 or
	  SL/SLC/SLC2/SLC3/SX/SX2 and UMC U5D or U5S.
	  - "586" for generic Pentium CPUs lacking the TSC
	  (time stamp counter) register.
	  - "Pentium-Classic" for the Intel Pentium.
	  - "Pentium-MMX" for the Intel Pentium MMX.
	  - "Pentium-Pro" for the Intel Pentium Pro.
	  - "Pentium-II" for the Intel Pentium II or pre-Coppermine Celeron.
	  - "Pentium-III" for the Intel Pentium III or Coppermine Celeron.
	  - "Pentium-4" for the Intel Pentium 4 or P4-based Celeron.
	  - "K6" for the AMD K6, K6-II and K6-III (aka K6-3D).
	  - "Athlon" for the AMD K7 family (Athlon/Duron/Thunderbird).
	  - "Crusoe" for the Transmeta Crusoe series.
	  - "Efficeon" for the Transmeta Efficeon series.
	  - "Winchip-C6" for original IDT Winchip.
	  - "Winchip-2" for IDT Winchip 2.
	  - "Winchip-2A" for IDT Winchips with 3dNow! capabilities.
	  - "GeodeGX1" for Geode GX1 (Cyrix MediaGX).
	  - "CyrixIII/VIA C3" for VIA Cyrix III or VIA C3.
	  - "VIA C3-2 for VIA C3-2 "Nehemiah" (model 9 and above).

	  If you don't know what to do, choose "386".

config M486
	bool "486"
	help
	  Select this for a 486 series processor, either Intel or one of the
	  compatible processors from AMD, Cyrix, IBM, or Intel.  Includes DX,
	  DX2, and DX4 variants; also SL/SLC/SLC2/SLC3/SX/SX2 and UMC U5D or
	  U5S.

config M586
	bool "586/K5/5x86/6x86/6x86MX"
	help
	  Select this for an 586 or 686 series processor such as the AMD K5,
	  the Cyrix 5x86, 6x86 and 6x86MX.  This choice does not
	  assume the RDTSC (Read Time Stamp Counter) instruction.

config M586TSC
	bool "Pentium-Classic"
	help
	  Select this for a Pentium Classic processor with the RDTSC (Read
	  Time Stamp Counter) instruction for benchmarking.

config M586MMX
	bool "Pentium-MMX"
	help
	  Select this for a Pentium with the MMX graphics/multimedia
	  extended instructions.

config M686
	bool "Pentium-Pro"
	help
	  Select this for Intel Pentium Pro chips.  This enables the use of
	  Pentium Pro extended instructions, and disables the init-time guard
	  against the f00f bug found in earlier Pentiums.

config MPENTIUMII
	bool "Pentium-II/Celeron(pre-Coppermine)"
	help
	  Select this for Intel chips based on the Pentium-II and
	  pre-Coppermine Celeron core.  This option enables an unaligned
	  copy optimization, compiles the kernel with optimization flags
	  tailored for the chip, and applies any applicable Pentium Pro
	  optimizations.

config MPENTIUMIII
	bool "Pentium-III/Celeron(Coppermine)/Pentium-III Xeon"
	help
	  Select this for Intel chips based on the Pentium-III and
	  Celeron-Coppermine core.  This option enables use of some
	  extended prefetch instructions in addition to the Pentium II
	  extensions.

config MPENTIUMM
	bool "Pentium M"
	help
	  Select this for Intel Pentium M (not Pentium-4 M)
	  notebook chips.

config MPENTIUM4
	bool "Pentium-4/Celeron(P4-based)/Pentium-4 M/Xeon"
	help
	  Select this for Intel Pentium 4 chips.  This includes the
	  Pentium 4, P4-based Celeron and Xeon, and Pentium-4 M
	  (not Pentium M) chips.  This option enables compile flags
	  optimized for the chip, uses the correct cache shift, and
	  applies any applicable Pentium III optimizations.

config MK6
	bool "K6/K6-II/K6-III"
	help
	  Select this for an AMD K6-family processor.  Enables use of
	  some extended instructions, and passes appropriate optimization
	  flags to GCC.

config MK7
	bool "Athlon/Duron/K7"
	help
	  Select this for an AMD Athlon K7-family processor.  Enables use of
	  some extended instructions, and passes appropriate optimization
	  flags to GCC.

config MK8
	bool "Opteron/Athlon64/Hammer/K8"
	help
	  Select this for an AMD Opteron or Athlon64 Hammer-family processor.  Enables
	  use of some extended instructions, and passes appropriate optimization
	  flags to GCC.

config MCRUSOE
	bool "Crusoe"
	help
	  Select this for a Transmeta Crusoe processor.  Treats the processor
	  like a 586 with TSC, and sets some GCC optimization flags (like a
	  Pentium Pro with no alignment requirements).

config MEFFICEON
	bool "Efficeon"
	help
	  Select this for a Transmeta Efficeon processor.

config MWINCHIPC6
	bool "Winchip-C6"
	help
	  Select this for an IDT Winchip C6 chip.  Linux and GCC
	  treat this chip as a 586TSC with some extended instructions
	  and alignment requirements.

config MWINCHIP2
	bool "Winchip-2"
	help
	  Select this for an IDT Winchip-2.  Linux and GCC
	  treat this chip as a 586TSC with some extended instructions
	  and alignment requirements.

config MWINCHIP3D
	bool "Winchip-2A/Winchip-3"
	help
	  Select this for an IDT Winchip-2A or 3.  Linux and GCC
	  treat this chip as a 586TSC with some extended instructions
	  and alignment reqirements.  Also enable out of order memory
	  stores for this CPU, which can increase performance of some
	  operations.

config MGEODEGX1
	bool "GeodeGX1"
	help
	  Select this for a Geode GX1 (Cyrix MediaGX) chip.

config MCYRIXIII
	bool "CyrixIII/VIA-C3"
	help
	  Select this for a Cyrix III or C3 chip.  Presently Linux and GCC
	  treat this chip as a generic 586. Whilst the CPU is 686 class,
	  it lacks the cmov extension which gcc assumes is present when
	  generating 686 code.
	  Note that Nehemiah (Model 9) and above will not boot with this
	  kernel due to them lacking the 3DNow! instructions used in earlier
	  incarnations of the CPU.

config MVIAC3_2
	bool "VIA C3-2 (Nehemiah)"
	help
	  Select this for a VIA C3 "Nehemiah". Selecting this enables usage
	  of SSE and tells gcc to treat the CPU as a 686.
	  Note, this kernel will not boot on older (pre model 9) C3s.

endchoice

config X86_GENERIC
       bool "Generic x86 support"
       help
	  Instead of just including optimizations for the selected
	  x86 variant (e.g. PII, Crusoe or Athlon), include some more
	  generic optimizations as well. This will make the kernel
	  perform better on x86 CPUs other than that selected.

	  This is really intended for distributors who need more
	  generic optimizations.

endif

#
# Define implied options from the CPU selection here
#
config X86_CMPXCHG
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config X86_XADD
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config X86_L1_CACHE_SHIFT
	int
	default "7" if MPENTIUM4 || X86_GENERIC
	default "4" if X86_ELAN || M486 || M386
	default "5" if MWINCHIP3D || MWINCHIP2 || MWINCHIPC6 || MCRUSOE || MEFFICEON || MCYRIXIII || MK6 || MPENTIUMIII || MPENTIUMII || M686 || M586MMX || M586TSC || M586 || MVIAC3_2 || MGEODEGX1
	default "6" if MK7 || MK8 || MPENTIUMM

config RWSEM_GENERIC_SPINLOCK
	bool
	depends on M386
	default y

config RWSEM_XCHGADD_ALGORITHM
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config GENERIC_CALIBRATE_DELAY
	bool
	default y

config X86_PPRO_FENCE
	bool
	depends on M686 || M586MMX || M586TSC || M586 || M486 || M386 || MGEODEGX1
	default y

config X86_F00F_BUG
	bool
	depends on M586MMX || M586TSC || M586 || M486 || M386
	default y

config X86_WP_WORKS_OK
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config X86_INVLPG
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config X86_BSWAP
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config X86_POPAD_OK
	bool
	depends on !M386
	default y

config X86_CMPXCHG64
	bool
	depends on !M386 && !M486
	default y

config X86_ALIGNMENT_16
	bool
	depends on MWINCHIP3D || MWINCHIP2 || MWINCHIPC6 || MCYRIXIII || X86_ELAN || MK6 || M586MMX || M586TSC || M586 || M486 || MVIAC3_2 || MGEODEGX1
	default y

config X86_GOOD_APIC
	bool
	depends on MK7 || MPENTIUM4 || MPENTIUMM || MPENTIUMIII || MPENTIUMII || M686 || M586MMX || MK8 || MEFFICEON
	default y

config X86_INTEL_USERCOPY
	bool
	depends on MPENTIUM4 || MPENTIUMM || MPENTIUMIII || MPENTIUMII || M586MMX || X86_GENERIC || MK8 || MK7 || MEFFICEON
	default y

config X86_USE_PPRO_CHECKSUM
	bool
	depends on MWINCHIP3D || MWINCHIP2 || MWINCHIPC6 || MCYRIXIII || MK7 || MK6 || MPENTIUM4 || MPENTIUMM || MPENTIUMIII || MPENTIUMII || M686 || MK8 || MVIAC3_2 || MEFFICEON
	default y

config X86_USE_3DNOW
	bool
	depends on MCYRIXIII || MK7
	default y

config X86_OOSTORE
	bool
	depends on (MWINCHIP3D || MWINCHIP2 || MWINCHIPC6) && MTRR
	default y

config X86_TSC
	bool
	depends on (MWINCHIP3D || MWINCHIP2 || MCRUSOE || MEFFICEON || MCYRIXIII || MK7 || MK6 || MPENTIUM4 || MPENTIUMM || MPENTIUMIII || MPENTIUMII || M686 || M586MMX || M586TSC || MK8 || MVIAC3_2 || MGEODEGX1) && !X86_NUMAQ
	default y

```

> i saw in Xorg.0.log ```
Build Operating System:Linux 2.6.12 i686
``` but i have an amd athlon...

### Post by fiuman on 2006-07-14
> **tseliot said:**
> What do you mean? Did you download gcc-4.0 or what?

i did 
```
su [invio]
CC=gcc-4.0 [invio]
export CC [invio]
exit [invio]
CC=gcc-4.0 [invio]
export CC[invio]
```

```
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run
```

it have worked.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-21
> **sebz2005 said:**
> It isn't working!!!
I have a GeForce 4 Ti with AGP 8x, I follow method 1 and when I restart X won't load.
I've updated to the 2.6.17 kernel.

I guess you didn't read the description of the 3 Methods, did you?

> EXPLANATION OF THE 3 METHODS

METHOD 1 It is the usual (and easiest) way to install the Nvidia drivers. It allows you to install nvidia driver 8762 OR 7174, i.e. the Legacy driver that you need ONLY IF your graphic card belongs to the list in the "NOTES SECTION", which you will find near the end of the guide. [WARMLY RECOMMENDED TO NEWBIES]. [COLOR="Red"]It works only with the kernels which you can find in Ubuntu's repository[/COLOR]. 

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-21
> **anton_most_muscular said:**
> Hi,

I've tried method1,2, the quickstart guide method, nVidia instructions and the add packages method -> I think all of them *should* work but I get this error when I reboot into X (in that blue error screen):

"no symbols found" "failed to allocate rankine object" "Addscreen/ScreenInit failed for driver 0".

-not very useful output to me! Maybe somebody in the forums knows...

Any idea where I could possibly be going wrong? I've spent approx 1 week trying to get this to work (a little bit obsessive).

I havn't been able to search for any help (although I've found solutions for the million other things that have/could go wrong).
I would GREATLY appreciate any help - I really want to use linux to build an openGl robotics simulation.

I'm using an nVidia 7300LE. It works well under windows. I havn't been able to get it to work in Fedora or Kubuntu either (similar errors).

Cheers

Anton from New Zealand
1) First of all I understand your frustration but trying all the methods can make a mess on your system. Uninstall the driver from Method 2 and install it with Method 1 (read my guide for both things).

2) Let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

3) post your xorg.conf

---

# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig

# package.
#

    Identifier     "Default Layout"

EndSection

Section "Files"

        # paths to defoma fonts

Section "Module"

    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc104"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "DELL E773s"
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NVIDIA Default Card"
    Driver         "nv"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NVIDIA Default Card"
    Monitor        "DELL E773s"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"

EndSection

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-21
> **larytet said:**
> my setup NVIDIA GeForce 6200 Go Graphics with 128MB, Sony Vaio, VGN-S460
2.6.15-23-386 #1 PREEMPT Tue May 23 13:49:40 UTC 2006 i686 GNU/Linux
i struggle with dual monitor support
should i replace nv driver ?
i do not need fast graphic, just dual monitor  with reasonable resolution
and two screens - primary and secondary

Install the Nvidia driver and follow a guide about Twinview:

### Post by ÄlveKatt on 2006-07-21
Thank you. The thing with being new at something is that you need extra clarity, not because you can't figure it out if you put some effort into, but rather because it feels safer. Call it a stress reduction feature. 

Other stress reduction features could include a link to a list of what things needs the restricted modules. And if possible an assurance that there are alternative ways of getting those things running without them.

> If you wish to install the NVIDIA Linux graphics driver on a Debian GNU/Linux or Ubuntu system that ships with Xorg 7.x, please ensure that your system meets the following requirements:

    * development tools like make and gcc are installed
    * the linux-headers package matching the installed Linux kernel is installed
    * the pkg-config and xserver-xorg-dev packages are installed
    * the nvidia-glx package has been uninstalled with the --purge option and the file /etc/init.d/nvidia-glx does not exist.

If you use Ubuntu, please also ensure that the linux-restricted-modules packages have been uninstalled.

### Post by anton_most_muscular on 2006-07-22
Final Comments - For anyone that's installed with method 2 and realised they've just messed up their wireless internet

I just installed drivers using method 2, which removes 'restricted modules' including wireless internet module.
*afterwards* I decided I wanted wireless internet (I know what an idiot).
Anyway -> I got it working really quickly using this sneaky trick I came up with:

(involves copying the needed files using the LIVE CD to avoid uninstalling and reinstalling everything)

1. insert ubuntu LIVE CD and restart
2. once booted, Places->Computer, open filesystem and navigate to the
/lib/modules/VERSION_NUMBER/volatile directory
3. COPY the ath_hal.ko file (could perhaps paste to desktop for now)
4. go to System->Admin->Disks and mount your main ubuntu partition on the desktop.
5. in the partition you just mounted, navigate to the
/lib/module/VERSION_NUMBER/kernel directory
6. paste in the ath_hal.ko file that we copied earlier.
7. remove Live CD and reboot.

Hope that helps someone else

Anton

---

### Post by mlind on 2006-07-25
I just had the most painless nvidia driver upgrage ever, I think you should add module-assistant way as alternative. I needed nvidia-kernel module for my custom kernel

```

sudo aptitude install kernel-headers module-assistant nvidia-glx

#user is on **src** group
m-a prepare && m-a build nvidia
sudo dpkg -i /usr/src/modass/nvidia-kernel*.deb
sudo depmod && sudo modprobe nvidia

```

---

### Post by srikat on 2006-07-27
> **jclmusic said:**
> i tried method 1, and now x won't start!

what's the command to restore the backup i made?

```
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

### Post by srikat on 2006-07-27
Works for me. I have nVIDIA GeForce 6100. Downloaded nvidia-glx driver via Add/Remove Apps. Then did:

sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx

followed by 

sudo nvidia-xconfig

and pressed CTRL+ALT+BACKSPACE.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-07-27
> **jclmusic said:**
> please say this doesn't require a whole new install!:(

Of course you don't have to reinstall. Try this:

Boot in RECOVERY MODE from the GRUB Menu almost as soon as you turn on your computer (it will take you to the command line).

Then you will need to type:
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

and select the "nv" driver. Press ENTER whenever you don't know the answer to a question.

Type:
```
reboot
```

The Xserver should work fine

---

deb [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-updates main restricted
deb-src [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-updates main restricted

deb [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper universe
deb-src [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper universe

deb [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse
deb-src [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse

deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main
deb-src [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main
deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe
deb-src [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe

Add these lines:
```
deb http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security main restricted
deb-src http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security main restricted
```


Then type:
sudo apt-get update

and follow Method 1 (use the command line as suggested in my guide)

---

Try disabling RenderAccel. In other words add this option to the Section Device of your /etc/X11/xorg.conf:


```
Option "RenderAccel" "False"
```

Set the driver to "nvidia".

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

### Post by dizm on 2006-08-09
I'm about to try one of your methods on my ASUS N6200 (nvidia 6200).

Can you please explain (or point me to FAQ) about the restricted modules?

- why must they be removed in one case and not the other?
- can they be re-installed afterwards?

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-09
> **dizm said:**
> I'm about to try one of your methods on my ASUS N6200 (nvidia 6200).

Can you please explain (or point me to FAQ) about the restricted modules?

- why must they be removed in one case and not the other?
- can they be re-installed afterwards?

The restricted modules contain modules of some drivers. For example they contain the module for driver 8762.

Let's suppose you want to install a newer version (when it's released) e.g. 9172 (it's an invented number) you will have to remove the restricted modules (which contains the module for driver 8762) and compile a new module for 9172.

And yes, you can reinstall them once you remove them.

BUT if you use method 2 and reinstall the restricted modules you risk causing a bit of a trouble i.e. e driver mismatch.

I suggest you to use Method 1 since (currently) the driver from method 1 and the one from method 2 are the same version.

---

### Post by dizm on 2006-08-10
Method one worked for me, thanks (albeit with the usual ancialiary weirdnesses that we seem always to have to muddle through).

I'd still like to understand:
 - why the nvidia installer doesn't work with dapper
 - why you have to remove _all_ the restricted drivers, not just nv

Cheers for the help.  Now on to TwinView...

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-10
> **dizm said:**
> I'd still like to understand:
 - why the nvidia installer doesn't work with dapper
It's not that it doesn't work. There is a problem since the version of the Xserver (7.0) in Dapper stores his libraries, etc. in paths which are not the same as the ones the Xserver 6.8.x uses.

Version 6.8.x is still used by some distros therefore (I guess) Nvidia preferred not to break the compatibility with older distros since a parameter could solve the problem with newer distros.

> **dizm said:**
>  - why you have to remove _all_ the restricted drivers, not just nv
"nv" is the opensource driver and you don't really remove it: you just don't use it.

Removing the restricted modules is required because the Nvidia installer compiles its own modules for your kernel.


In other words if you had used Method 1 you wouldn't have had to remove the restricted modules (and you would have used them).

---

	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
	# path to defoma fonts
	FontPath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc104"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"		"ExplorerPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"		"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "stylus"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "stylus"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "eraser"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "eraser"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "cursor"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "cursor"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"VIA Technologies, Inc. S3 Unichrome Pro VGA Adapter"
	Driver		"vesa"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Philips 170S"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"VIA Technologies, Inc. S3 Unichrome Pro VGA Adapter"
	Monitor		"Philips 170S"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		16
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice     "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection
```

Thanks for any help

### Post by Avalokiteshvara on 2006-08-13
Hi,

I am having trouble setting up my NVIDIA graphics card. I have a Compaq Presario V6000 laptop with NVIDIA GeForce Go 6150. It comes with 15.4" widescreen LCD. 

I first tried installing the drivers with Automatix, but on reboot I would get a blank screen (although hear the drums and then be able to log in) - it wouldn't even display the text mode (so I reinstalled Ubuntu).

After reinstalling, I tried Envy (envy_8762_64). Now when I reboot my computer, only the text mode works. This is what I get:

"Failed to start the X server (your graphical interface). It is likely that it is not set up correctly. Would you like to view the X server output to diagnose the problem?"

I click yes, this is the error it gives:

"(WW) NVIDIA: No matching Device section for instance (BusID PCI:0:10:3) found
FATAL: Module nvidia not found
(EE) NVIDIA(0): Failed to load the NVIDIA kernel module!
(EE) NVIDIA(0): *** Aborting ***
(EE) Screen(s) found, but none have a usable configuration.

Fatal server error:
no screens found"

Any idea how I could fix this? Any help greatly appreciated :-)

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-13
> **Avalokiteshvara said:**
> I tried what you said. I keep getting the same error message.

can you post the output of:
```
lspci
```

and of:
```
lspci -n | grep 0300
```

P.S. if you want to get to the Desktop Environment again (so as not to use text mode):
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

and select the "nv" driver. Press Enter whenever you don't know the answer to a question

### Post by Avalokiteshvara on 2006-08-13
I did "sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg" as you suggested, and then when I rebooted, it loaded the graphical environment. Before I could only display "1024x768" (which looked very weird in widescreen), but now it loaded 1280x800 without a problem!

Problem solved?

I will try to play some videos to make sure.

:)

### Post by mdrileynyc on 2006-08-13
I was having some video problems when using the "nv" driver (namely (a) when blank xscreensaver activates often I get 'no signal' on my monitor after a while (b) sometimes I get random flashing colors - both stopped by e.g. moving mouse) so I decided to try the "nvidia" driver. 

Using Nvidia method 1 I had serious problems. I followed directions completely. X will start successfully on reboot, however when I log out things freeze up (process Xorg running). If instead I restart X (ctl-alt-bksp), then it sometimes is OK but more often various Gnome apps die (clock, nautilus, panel). 

Here are my relevant system details:

1)Linux nero 2.6.15-26-386 #1 PREEMPT Thu Aug 3 02:52:00 UTC 2006 i686 GNU/Linux
2)Ubuntu 6.06.1 LTS
3)amd32
4)integrated nvidia nforce (South Bridge)
5)42" panasonic plasma display
6) using own modeline (works)

Note that both "nv" and "nvidia" drivers worked fine
(using modeline aboe) on this system when running fedora core 3 with none of the problems mentioned.

My real problem is the "no signal/flashing colors" issue
under "nv". I don't really need "nvidia" unless it happens to fix these problems.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-13
> **mdrileynyc said:**
> ... but skipped #13 since it suggests it's only for some specific cards (if you don't look very carefully).

It's not specific to those card ONLY. I'm only sure that those cards are affected by that problem.

> **mdrileynyc said:**
> Before I execute #13 I have to ask a question. You say:

[INDENT]look for this line

# defoptions=quiet splash

Remove the word "splash" from it. 
[/INDENT]

But the '#' appears to be a comment so isn't this a nop?
Not exactly.

If you modify that line (leaving the #) (as I suggest) and do "sudo update-grub" (as the instructions say) you will notice the the word splash will disappear from all your kernels.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-13
> **Avalokiteshvara said:**
> This is for "lspci -n | grep 0300":

0000:00:05.0 0300: 10de:0244 (rev a2)

Thanks!
Unfortunately your card is not supported by the Nvidia driver yet.

That's why you can't use the driver right now.

You should use the "nv" driver and if you have problems with the "nv" driver you can use "vesa".

The next version of the driver will possibly support your card.

### Post by mdrileynyc on 2006-08-13
> **tseliot said:**
> Did you reboot?

If not, please do it.


If you did, post your menu.lst

Here is my menu.lst:


# menu.lst - See: grub(8), info grub, update-grub(8)
#            grub-install(8), grub-floppy(8),
#            grub-md5-crypt, /usr/share/doc/grub
#            and /usr/share/doc/grub-doc/.

## default num
# Set the default entry to the entry number NUM. Numbering starts from 0, and
# the entry number 0 is the default if the command is not used.
#
# You can specify 'saved' instead of a number. In this case, the default entry
# is the entry saved with the command 'savedefault'.
# WARNING: If you are using dmraid do not change this entry to 'saved' or your
# array will desync and will not let you boot your system.
default         0

## timeout sec
# Set a timeout, in SEC seconds, before automatically booting the default entry
# (normally the first entry defined).
timeout         3

## hiddenmenu
# Hides the menu by default (press ESC to see the menu)
hiddenmenu

# Pretty colours
#color cyan/blue white/blue

## password ['--md5'] passwd
# If used in the first section of a menu file, disable all interactive editing
# control (menu entry editor and command-line)  and entries protected by the
# command 'lock'
# e.g. password topsecret
#      password --md5 $1$gLhU0/$aW78kHK1QfV3P2b2znUoe/
# password topsecret

#
# examples
#
# title         Windows 95/98/NT/2000
# root          (hd0,0)
# makeactive
# chainloader   +1
#
# title         Linux
# root          (hd0,1)
# kernel        /vmlinuz root=/dev/hda2 ro
#

#
# Put static boot stanzas before and/or after AUTOMAGIC KERNEL LIST

### BEGIN AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST
## lines between the AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST markers will be modified
## by the debian update-grub script except for the default options below

## DO NOT UNCOMMENT THEM, Just edit them to your needs

## ## Start Default Options ##
## default kernel options
## default kernel options for automagic boot options
## If you want special options for specific kernels use kopt_x_y_z
## where x.y.z is kernel version. Minor versions can be omitted.
## e.g. kopt=root=/dev/hda1 ro
##      kopt_2_6_8=root=/dev/hdc1 ro
##      kopt_2_6_8_2_686=root=/dev/hdc2 ro
# kopt=root=/dev/hda1 ro

## default grub root device
## e.g. groot=(hd0,0)
# groot=(hd0,0)

## should update-grub create alternative automagic boot options
## e.g. alternative=true
##      alternative=false
# alternative=true

## should update-grub lock alternative automagic boot options
## e.g. lockalternative=true
##      lockalternative=false
# lockalternative=false

## additional options to use with the default boot option, but not with the
## alternatives
## e.g. defoptions=vga=791 resume=/dev/hda5
# defoptions=quiet

## altoption boot targets option
## multiple altoptions lines are allowed
## e.g. altoptions=(extra menu suffix) extra boot options
##      altoptions=(recovery mode) single
# altoptions=(recovery mode) single

## controls how many kernels should be put into the menu.lst
## only counts the first occurence of a kernel, not the
## alternative kernel options
## e.g. howmany=all
##      howmany=7
# howmany=all

## should update-grub create memtest86 boot option
## e.g. memtest86=true
##      memtest86=false
# memtest86=true

## should update-grub adjust the value of the default booted system
## can be true or false
# updatedefaultentry=false

## ## End Default Options ##

title           Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.15-26-386
root            (hd0,0)
kernel          /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.15-26-386 root=/dev/hda1 ro quiet
initrd          /boot/initrd.img-2.6.15-26-386
savedefault
boot

title           Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.15-26-386 (recovery mode)
root            (hd0,0)
kernel          /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.15-26-386 root=/dev/hda1 ro single
initrd          /boot/initrd.img-2.6.15-26-386
boot

title           Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.15-23-386
root            (hd0,0)
kernel          /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.15-23-386 root=/dev/hda1 ro quiet
initrd          /boot/initrd.img-2.6.15-23-386
savedefault
boot

title           Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.15-23-386 (recovery mode)
root            (hd0,0)
kernel          /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.15-23-386 root=/dev/hda1 ro single
initrd          /boot/initrd.img-2.6.15-23-386
boot

title           Ubuntu, memtest86+
root            (hd0,0)
kernel          /boot/memtest86+.bin
boot

### END DEBIAN AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST

### Post by pcfreak on 2006-08-15
Hi, I am new to Ubuntu...
I tried to install the NVIDIA driver as described on this page [http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper), however, after i pressed Ctrl + Alt + Back Space, the screen was dark for a while, until it became completely white. I tried to reset the machine, but when it tries to run X again, it happens again. I have a GeForce 4 Ti4800SE AGP8X card. I cannot see the mouse cursor or anything.

Anotherthing, is it possible for me to prevent Ubuntu from starting X during startup, which could have been convinient now.

Thank's for all replies.

EDIT: And oh, I chose method one...

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-15
> **pcfreak said:**
> Hi, I am new to Ubuntu...
I tried to install the NVIDIA driver as described on this page [http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper), however, after i pressed Ctrl + Alt + Back Space, the screen was dark for a while, until it became completely white. I tried to reset the machine, but when it tries to run X again, it happens again. I have a GeForce 4 Ti4800SE AGP8X card. I cannot see the mouse cursor or anything.

Anotherthing, is it possible for me to prevent Ubuntu from starting X during startup, which could have been convinient now.

Thank's for all replies.

EDIT: And oh, I chose method one...

boot in RECOVERY MODE from the GRUB Menu almost as soon as you turn on your computer (it will take you to the command line).

Then you will need to type:
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

and select the "vesa" driver. Press ENTER whenever you don't know the answer to a question.

Type:
```
reboot
```

The Xserver should work fine but you won't use the nvidia driver.

After you do that I would like you to answer some questions:

1) what is the model of your card?

2) can you post the output of these commands:
```
lspci
```

and

```
lspci | grep 300
```

---

### Post by pcfreak on 2006-08-15
well, I did what you said, I did go through the configurator for X, but when I was finished, and Ubuntu tried to enter X, the screen turned off.. And when I try to go into recovery mode again to give it another try, then the root password does not work... I have tried many times... Then tried Control-D wich is my option, but then X starts and the same happens... :sad:
Luck is not with me today...

I chose the VESA driver... And for the most part just pressed enter as you told me to...

EDIT: I managed to come around it when X started by pressing ctrl alt f1, or esc, something like that, then I could log on as root.
I'll give it another try...

### Post by geckomind on 2006-08-16
Everything worked fine for me (I used the script). Just one thing: Can anyone tell me how I can get rid of that annoying nVidea splash screen at startup?

Help greatly appreciated.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-16
> **geckomind said:**
> Everything worked fine for me (I used the script). Just one thing: Can anyone tell me how I can get rid of that annoying nVidea splash screen at startup?

Help greatly appreciated.
Ad this to the Section Device of your xorg.conf:

```
Option "NoLogo" "0"
```

P.S. I might add an option on Envy to disable it

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-16
> **JeanNiBee said:**
> Success with Method 1 worked with kernel 2.6.15-26-386

At least I think it did.. when I rebooted X I saw the NVidia logo briefly... but I can't find that old glfrsgears or whatever it's called to test the fps it can handle in 3d.

Thanks.

type:
```
glxgears
```

    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"

    InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"

EndSection

Section "Files"

	# path to defoma fonts

    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"

EndSection

    Option         "CoreKeyboard"

    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "de"
    Option         "XkbVariant" "nodeadkeys"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Synaptics Touchpad"
    Driver         "synaptics"
    Option         "SendCoreEvents" "true"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto-dev"
    Option         "HorizScrollDelta" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to

    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 96.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 60.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV41.9 [GeForce Go 6800 Ultra]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    Option         "NoLogo" "0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV41.9 [GeForce Go 6800 Ultra]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    16
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1920x1200"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1920x1200"
    EndSubSection

        Depth       16
        Modes      "1920x1200"
    EndSubSection

        Modes      "1920x1200"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-16
**[COLOR="Red"][SIZE="3"]Envy 0.4.2-1ubuntu1 has been released![/SIZE][/COLOR]**


**** Fixed a BUG in “instun/main.py” which caused Envy to abort the operation on 64bit OSes***

Remove the previous version of envy:

```
sudo apt-get remove envy
```

and install the current version.

Ubuntu 64bit users: I apologise for the inconvenience.

---

here is my xorg.conf backup, maybe there is a hint to my problem. I dont have the one which envy create but driver wath set to nvidia etc.

```
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
	# path to defoma fonts
	FontPath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"de"
	Option		"XkbVariant"	"nodeadkeys"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"		"ExplorerPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"		"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "stylus"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "stylus"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "eraser"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "eraser"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "cursor"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to 
                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "cursor"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
	Driver		"nv"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Generic Monitor"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
	Monitor		"Generic Monitor"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		16
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice     "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice     "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection
```

I would be thankful for every hint to get my card work.

peetpan

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-18
[SIZE="3"][COLOR="Red"]**Envy 0.4.2-1ubuntu2 has been released!**[/COLOR][/SIZE]

 * Fixed the path to the Xserver for Breezy

> **autocrosser said:**

0000:01:00.0 0300: 10de:0221 (rev a1)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-20

I have tried the solution at point 16 in the problems but the same happens with the screen going on standby and hearing the drums.
Try point 4 of the Problems Section

### Post by aos101 on 2006-08-20

Just tried that and its the same with the monitor going on standby, but pressing Ctrl-Alt-F3 (I think that's it) to get a command line when the monitor went off didn't work whereas it did before (I normally use that to then restore to a working xorg.conf).

### Post by aos101 on 2006-08-21
> **tseliot said:**
> I see...

Perhaps point 13 can help you:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper)
Tried that and didn't work, but then I had an excellent idea - why not turn on my other monitor.  I run a 17" LCD as my main monitor and a 15" one on the side, but I've had the 15" turned off when using Ubuntu because it just showed a messed up image.  I just tried running the xorg.conf that was setup after running "sudo nvidia-xconfig" with my other monitor on and the desktop loads fine, but on the other monitor (the 15" one) with my main 17" one just sitting on standby.  

So I've got it kind of working now (with the nvidia slpash image), but need the image on the other monitor (then I can play with getting my desktop on both monitors).

Edit: Got the image on my main monitor now by adding "Option "UseDisplayDevice" "DFP"" to my device section.  Thanks very much for your help.
