### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-21
> **TakisX said:**
> 1. if it works why i get the wrong resolution
The driver is not perfect. And it depends also on your card.

That's why my guide has a Problems Section which perhaps you didn't read.

> **TakisX said:**
> 2. i followed method 2 skiping the line
sudo ./nvidia-installer -n -s --x-prefix=/usr/lib/xorg/ --kernel-source-path=/usr/src/linux-headers-`uname -r`

and it works
It worked in your case. It didn't work for many users without that line.
I have to foresee all the other cases too.


> **TakisX said:**
> 3. what is your opinion about this 

takisx@nisaki:~$ dmesg | grep NVIDIA
[17180209.824000] nvidia: module license 'NVIDIA' taints kernel.
[17180209.832000] NVRM: loading NVIDIA Linux x86 NVIDIA Kernel Module  1.0-7174  Tue Mar 22 06:44:39 PST 2005
[17183564.620000] NVRM: loading NVIDIA Linux x86 Kernel Module  1.0-7182  Wed Ap r 19 12:56:13 PDT 2006
[17183724.240000] NVRM: loading NVIDIA Linux x86 Kernel Module  1.0-7182  Wed Ap r 19 12:56:13 PDT 2006
takisx@nisaki:~$ glxinfo | grep direct
direct rendering: Yes
takisx@nisaki:~$
The driver is working just fine

### Post by anindya_m on 2006-08-21

I have a nvidia 5700 LE (256MB) on AGP and this is the only graphics card. After the install the nv driver worked on first boot. Then I updated the kernel to 2.6.15-26-amd64-generic. ```

### Post by mlind on 2006-08-22
> **ITSNev said:**

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-22
> **anindya_m said:**
> Hi

I just upgraded my AMD64 system to Dapper and immediately landed up with the nvidia problem :( My CPU is AMD Athlon 64 3400+ on an Asus K8V motherboard with 1 GB RAM. I have a nvidia 5700 LE (256MB) on AGP and this is the only graphics card. After the install the nv driver worked on first boot. Then I updated the kernel to 2.6.15-26-amd64-generic. The nv driver has stopped working since (even on the old kernel). The log says

```
(EE) No devices detected.
```

for nv. Next I followed Method 1 in tseliot's howto and installed the nvidia-glx package (I have the restricted-modules package). However even the nvidia driver cannot find the card. Here is my xorg.conf:

```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-22
> **ITSNev said:**
> Same problem here too, worked fine on first install, did an update now no screens Found....

Any Solutions out there?

I need to know exactly what you did.

1) Did the "nv" driver work?
2) Did the "nvidia£ driver work?
3) Which card is yours?
4) post the output of this command:
```
lspci
```
5) post your /var/log/Xorg.0.log

### Post by jadfw007 on 2006-08-23
Method 1 didn't work for me. I can't finish method 2 because I don't know how to browse the files on a CDROM from the command line (explanation of why I have to use the command line follows).

I ran update yesterday. I don't remeber the exact module, but I know it was an xorg module - probably the server. My card (NVidia GeForce 6200) was working fine up until that point with dual monitors (a Princeton and a Samsung 913v). I rebooted after the upgrade and Xserver just would not load. "No device found." "No screen found." Those were the only errors.

I've spent the last several hours combing through this forum and several others. I can't post my xorg.config file b/c I'm posting this from another computer entirely. My Ubuntu machine is totally GUI-hosed and I'm not command-line savvy enough to write this post from the command line. I can outline the various things I've tried.

[LIST]
[*]Ran sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg several times, trying every combination I could think of for every setting that some post recommended changing
[LIST]
[*]driver: nv -> nvidia -> generic driver and back again
[*]modifying refresh rates
[*]framebuffer: with and without
[/LIST]
[*]Unplugged one of the monitors and did it all again
[*]Commented out everything in the module file and did it all again
[*]modprobed nvidia driver and startx
[/LIST]

Always the same error. I reboot and the X server fails to start, kicking me to the command line. I'm rapidly approaching the point where a full re-install would be less trouble than what I've gone through so far. Any help at all would be greatly appreciated! Note that I'll accept desperation measures like "how to manually uninstall Xorg and re-install an old version" if it means keeping my applications and data.

---

### Post by jadfw007 on 2006-08-23
> **tseliot said:**
> Read this thread:
[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=241254](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=241254)
Now how could I have done all that forum surfing and missed this? Obviously, the upgrade was my issue and your instructions for rolling back the upgrade fixed most of the problem. Thanks! Of course, I had to re-install restricted modules and the nvidia driver. Those instructions can be found here:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/Nvidia]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/Nvidia")

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-23
> **diff_sky said:**
> Firstly, you do some great work for the ubuntu community - thanks for all your efforts

I'm having a problem with envy_0.4.2-1ubuntu3_all.deb

I installed the package, but when I run envy on the command line and select install I get an error saying my card is not supported by the nvidia drivers. I have an Nvidia Geforce 6200.

I previously used you envy_8762_32 without any problems, so I am not sure what is going wrong.

Any suggestions? You probably need further info from me, let me know what that is and I will supply it.

try with:
```
sudo apt-get --purge remove envy
```
```
sudo rm -R /usr/share/envy
```
then install it again

---

### Post by ConteMoka on 2006-08-24
I am completely new to ubuntu and linux.

I installed ubuntu a few days ago on my Toshiba Satellite 5000-204 laptop and, since I was disappointed with the performance of the video, I tried to install the "latest" nVidia drivers.

To be honest, after having searched for hint in almost every forum I could find and after having spent more than 4 days trying different procedures, I am running out of ideas.

I tried Method 1, Method 1 (offline), Method 2 and your script. Everytime I had my Ubuntu re-istalled before (just to be sure that I did not make some mistake).
I read the nVidia official guide and I run the following:

sudo modprobe nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NVreg_Mobile=4

(I also tried with NVreg_Mobile=2 and =0xFFFFFFFF)

NO WAY!

The graphic card is a GeForce2 Go
(nVidia Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 Go] rev b2)

Could anyone please help me? I can also re-install Ubuntu again, follow any procedure you prefer, and post the logs.

Thank you.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-24
> **ConteMoka said:**
> I am completely new to ubuntu and linux.

I installed ubuntu a few days ago on my Toshiba Satellite 5000-204 laptop and, since I was disappointed with the performance of the video, I tried to install the "latest" nVidia drivers.

To be honest, after having searched for hint in almost every forum I could find and after having spent more than 4 days trying different procedures, I am running out of ideas.

I tried Method 1, Method 1 (offline), Method 2 and your script. Everytime I had my Ubuntu re-istalled before (just to be sure that I did not make some mistake).
I read the nVidia official guide and I run the following:

sudo modprobe nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NVreg_Mobile=4

(I also tried with NVreg_Mobile=2 and =0xFFFFFFFF)

NO WAY!

The graphic card is a GeForce2 Go
(nVidia Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 Go] rev b2)

Could anyone please help me? I can also re-install Ubuntu again, follow any procedure you prefer, and post the logs.

Thank you.

I always suggest not to use more than 1 method.

I need to know the following things about **your current installation of Ubuntu**:

1) Which is the last method you tried?
2) how many method did you try?

I need to know those things so as to know what you need to uninstall.

3) Did you try point 7 of the Problems Section of this guide?
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper)

4)In point 7 I suggest one thing and then, in case of failure, another. Did you try them both?

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-24
> **ConteMoka said:**
> Ok. Here I am.

1. I re-installed Ubuntu.
2. I followed Method 1. Step-by-step.
3. Applied changes to xorg.config as follows:
 Option         "ExactModeTimingsDVI"   "TRUE"
 Option         "ModeValidation"   "DFP-0: NoEdidDFPMaxSizeCheck, NoVesaModes"

4. ... and to the option file (options nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NV_regMobile=1)
Result. The screen blinks three times (the bottom part is white the top part is black). Then remains black.
You can find the xorg.conf and the Xo.0.log file attached as Xorg_Config.first and Xorg_log.first, respectively.

5. Changed the xorg.conf to include:
Option "ExactModeTimingsDVI"
Option "UseEdidDpi" "FALSE"
Option "ModeValidation" "NoEdidDFPMaxSizeCheck, NoVesaModes"

Result: As above

6. Changed the option file to include NVreg_Mobile=4 (instead of 1)
Same result

7. Changed back the xorg.conf file to the first version:
  Option         "ExactModeTimingsDVI"   "TRUE"
  Option         "ModeValidation"   "DFP-0: 
Same result. xorg.conf and Xorg.0.log attached as Xorg_Config.second and Xorg_log.second, respectively.

8. Tried NVreg_Mobile=2
Same result

I already updated the BIOS to the latest version.

9. HELP! :D

Remove all the things I suggested you to add (there's no need to reinstall anything).

then let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-26
**[COLOR="Red"]Envy 0.4.2-1ubuntu7 has been released[/COLOR]**
> 
   * Changed: disabled the Composite extension in Edgy
   * Changed: blacklisted "nv" in /etc/default/linux-restricted-modules-common (to prevent API mismatch) in Edgy
   * BUG: Envy causes warning when redirecting the errot to /dev/null in Edgy
   * Changed: /etc/default/linux-restricted-modules-common is now required in Edgy
   * Changed: the support for Geforce FX 6200 (ID = 0221) is in the list again
   * Removed: the support for Debian Etch (temporarily)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-27
> **ginzberg said:**
> Hrm, any way to force the script to use http?  I keep timing out to ftp, but I can easily wget the nvidia install package.

Download the installer with wget separately.

then type:
```
cd folder_with_the_installer_which_you_downloaded
```
```

sudo cp name_of_the_installer_which_you_downloaded /usr/share/envy/
```

then run envy again

### Post by ginzberg on 2006-08-27

However, I seem to have another problem.  I hope that you can help.  I have seen some indications to this same issue, but have not found a solution.  I may not be looking deep enough, but the issue seems to be dropped, or someone 'reformats and reinstalls'.  Anyways, I am receiving the elusive "direct rendering: No" in glxinfo.

I did install XGL/Compiz at one point, and hopefully this will help clarify where I think the problem is.

> ~$ glxinfo |grep rendering && glxinfo |grep vendor
direct rendering: No
server glx vendor string: SGI
client glx vendor string: NVIDIA Corporation
OpenGL vendor string: NVIDIA Corporation

As you can see, the server glx vendor is SGI, rather than NVIDIA.  
A little bit more information regarding that server glx extension:

> server glx vendor string: SGI
server glx version string: 1.2
server glx extensions:
    GLX_ARB_multisample, GLX_EXT_visual_info, GLX_EXT_visual_rating, 
    GLX_EXT_import_context, GLX_EXT_texture_from_pixmap, GLX_OML_swap_method, 
    GLX_SGI_make_current_read, GLX_SGIS_multisample, GLX_SGIX_hyperpipe, 
    GLX_SGIX_swap_barrier, GLX_SGIX_fbconfig

I am assuming this has something to do with mesa, however I am not sure how I force xserver to use nvidia.  Installing the driver using method1, method2, and the envy script all did not provide a solution.

> ~$ ls -l /usr/lib/libGL*
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root      21 2006-08-27 19:41 /usr/lib/libGLcore.so.1 -> libGLcore.so.1.0.8762
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 8113728 2006-07-10 10:10 /usr/lib/libGLcore.so.1.0.8762
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root      16 2006-07-31 20:16 /usr/lib/libGLEW.so.1.3 -> libGLEW.so.1.3.1
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  205204 2005-11-30 15:47 /usr/lib/libGLEW.so.1.3.1
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root      10 2006-08-27 19:39 /usr/lib/libGL.so -> libGL.so.1
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root      17 2006-08-27 19:41 /usr/lib/libGL.so.1 -> libGL.so.1.0.8762
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  540136 2006-07-10 10:10 /usr/lib/libGL.so.1.0.8762
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  671844 2006-08-24 01:29 /usr/lib/libGLU.a
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root      11 2006-08-24 08:30 /usr/lib/libGLU.so -> libGLU.so.1
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root      20 2006-08-24 08:25 /usr/lib/libGLU.so.1 -> libGLU.so.1.3.060501
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  483308 2006-08-24 01:29 /usr/lib/libGLU.so.1.3.060501

Here is a dump of my GL libraries.  The only mesa one that I use is in /opt/mesa/libGL.so.1.2, and I use LD_PRELOAD in order to launch compiz with that.  I have no desire to cling on to compiz/xgl, I would rather have my graphics card working as it should, and allow for those projects (or most likely, aixgl) to mature.  

I tested the waters of apt-get remove libgl1-mesa* and my machine told me it was going to delete the world.  I wanted to get an second opinion here before I do anything drastic.

Incidentally, I have also not been able to force nvagp.  I have blacklisted, set agp=off in kernel strings, set option nvagp=1 in xorg, everything... I wonder if it is related, or, is this just due to the fact that it is a pci-e card (6800)?  I thought I read that nvagp still does provide some performance changes on the pci-e cards. (though I assume that agp fastwrites, etc are not applicable).

Thank you in advance for any information you may be able to provide!

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-29
> **Causinc said:**
> Um, er, I *know* you have in BIG RED LETTERS on your website not to use envy if you need the restricted modules for your wifi card, but I was just *wondering*.... what would one do if (hypothetically) you used envy, loved it, but now your wifi card doesn't work? Is there a recommended course of action? 

Of course, if I (for example, hypothetically) had done this, I could still download stuff by booting into windows and downloading to my common fat32 area. Any thoughts on where I should look? (if I had been stupid enough to ignore big red letters because, to be honest, I wouldn't have a clue as to whether my wifi card needed restricted modules or not - 'cos I have no idea what they are or what they do!)

Still think it's an awesome script though ;^)

Try in this way:
1) Dowload this file:
[www.webalice.it/albertomilone/ubuntu/ath_hal.ko](www.webalice.it/albertomilone/ubuntu/ath_hal.ko)

2) open Terminal or Konsole (and type the commands EXACTLY as I suggest):
```
cd path_to_the_folder_containing_that_file
```
NOTE: you need to replace path_to_the_folder_containing_that_file with the path to the folder containing the file you downloaded

```
sudo cp ath_hal.ko /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/kernel/
```

then:
```
sudo depmod -a $(uname -r)
```

Reboot and tell me if it works

---

Ok, I know that's not sufficient info, so here's what I did:

First I saved the kernel module to my D: drive from windows (that's the fat32 drive that's common). Then I booted into Linux. 

Became root (sorry, I can't be doin' with all this sudo stuff. I've been running Linux systems, mosrtly redhat based, for a long time and virtually the first thing I did was a "sudo passwd" to allow me to log in as root - am I bad?)

I copied the kernel module to /lib/modules/2.6.15-26-386/kernel and typed depmod -a $(uname -r)

Note: I've not used this syntax before. If I include something like "uname -r" within a command I use backquotes to effect the substitution - I'll have to look into what this does (I assume this assumes bash as a shell).

Rebooted - no Wifi card (although you are correct in assuming it is an Ath based card - did I tell you that?)

Then tried the following:

root@kaftan:~# mv /lib/modules/2.6.15-26-386/kernel/ath_hal.ko /lib/modules/2.6.15-26-386/kernel/drivers/net/
root@kaftan:~# insmod
Usage: insmod filename [args]
root@kaftan:~# insmod /lib/modules/2.6.15-26-386/kernel/drivers/net/ath_hal.ko
insmod: error inserting '/lib/modules/2.6.15-26-386/kernel/drivers/net/ath_hal.ko': -1 Invalid module format
root@kaftan:~# uname -r
2.6.15-26-386

As you probably guessed, I'm new to Ubuntu/Debian but not to Linux. These "restricted" modules - are they a package of drivers that contain non-open source code? I don't understand (a) why they are called "restricted"; (b) why they have to be unloaded to get the nvidia drivers to work; and (c) why they are bundled *together*. It seems to me that, assuming that they are a ragtag mob of kernel drivers that somehow contravene purists sensibilities about some principle or other, that bundling them together just means there is a huge liklihood of breaking package dependancies. But I'm probably missing a point or ten ;^)



> **tseliot said:**
> Try in this way:
1) Dowload this file:
[www.webalice.it/albertomilone/ubuntu/ath_hal.ko](www.webalice.it/albertomilone/ubuntu/ath_hal.ko)

2) open Terminal or Konsole (and type the commands EXACTLY as I suggest):
```
cd path_to_the_folder_containing_that_file
```
NOTE: you need to replace path_to_the_folder_containing_that_file with the path to the folder containing the file you downloaded

```
sudo cp ath_hal.ko /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/kernel/
```

then:
```
sudo depmod -a $(uname -r)
```

Reboot and tell me if it works

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-08-30
> **Causinc said:**
> As you probably guessed, I'm new to Ubuntu/Debian but not to Linux. These "restricted" modules - are they a package of drivers that contain non-open source code? I don't understand (a) why they are called "restricted";
The restricted modules include drivers which are not opensource.

> **Causinc said:**
> (b) why they have to be unloaded to get the nvidia drivers to work; and (c) why they are bundled *together*. It seems to me that, assuming that they are a ragtag mob of kernel drivers that somehow contravene purists sensibilities about some principle or other, that bundling them together just means there is a huge liklihood of breaking package dependancies. But I'm probably missing a point or ten ;^)
Actually the nvidia driver is included in the restricted modules but it's not updated to the latest version. If you had used Method 1 you wouldn't have had any problem.

BTW I have got the solution for you:
1) Remove the file I gave you.
2) Download the **alternate installer** of Ubuntu Dapper LTS **6.06.1** and burn it
3) Boot in Ubuntu
4) Use the uninstallation function from envy
5) Then type:
```
sudo apt-cdrom add
```

it will ask you to insert your CD

then:
```
sudo apt-get install linux-386
```

Reboot and everything should work.


P.S. There's nothing bad in using root (instead of sudo). It's your choice.

### Post by Causinc on 2006-09-02
Ok, I'm back online again ;)

Sort of followed your advice but took a few liberties. This is roughly what I did:

Found the ISO file for the cd which was on the dvd that I got ubuntu on (the live distro). Copied that to my fat32 disk then booted into ubunbtu and mounted it via the loopback device. Of course this meant that it wasn't in the cd drive so your commands for getting the packages from the cd probably wouldn't work. So I just used dpkg (recursively) to install the restricted modules again from their deb files. Anyway, got all that done and rebooted.

Of course the restricted modules were all built for the kernel version on the cd (2.6.15-23) so I judiciously booted into that kernel version rather than the latest. Sure enough, my display still worked and now my WiFi card was working again. Ther was also a little blinking "Update Me" light which showed me that the restricted modules and related items were out of date and needed upgrading - sweet!

However, a little troubling was that the "latest" linux-386 package was listed as version 2.6.15-24, whereas the latest kernel is 2.6.15-26. Some of the other modules, such as nvidia-glx, were telling me 2.6.15-26 - it was just the linux-386 which seemed to be out.

Anyway, I upgraded the ones it told me to and rebooted. Sure enough, if I boot into kernel 2.6.15-26 I have nvidia drivers but no WiFi card, whereas if I boot back into 2.6.15-23 everything is working (or seems to be!).

Maybe my repositories are out of date? Anyway, I'll just stick with the original kernel for the moment and see how it goes :)

Thanks again for your help. And I'll be intrigued to see what you mean by not needing Envy soon. That'll be a fresh sticky thread I presume?

Cheers

Ron

---

### Post by kornelix on 2006-09-02
TSEliot, thanks for helping us out.

I did "method 1" after trashing my x11 system with Easy Ubuntu (and taking a crash course in vim to fix the xorg.conf file).

As long as such complex and non-intuitive methods are needed to make Ubuntu work (and Linux in general), the market share will remain at 2%. I hope someone is working on the Big Picture to make installations and upgrades more reasonable (transparent, automatic).

I stupidly tried to file a bug report as follows: when you update the kernel version using synaptic, you can easily trash x11 and make the whole system unusable. I was politely informed that this is not a bug, but my mistake for not knowing (or remembering) that the corresponding restricted module package must be simultaneously installed. What a bunch of xxxxx.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-09-03
> **SBFC said:**
> Are the madwifi drivers for an Atheros wireless card considered restricted?

You can't use Envy therefore we need to find another way.

Try with:
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-glx
```
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-glx-legacy
```
```
sudo rm /etc/init.d/nvidia
```
```
sudo depmod -a
```

then:
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx
```
```
sudo depmod -a
```

and restart the Xserver:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (or "kdm restart" if you use kdm)
```

---

/etc/apt/sources.list:

## Major bug fix updates produced after the final release of the
## distribution.
deb [http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-updates main restricted universe multiverse
deb-src [http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-updates main restricted

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'universe'
## repository.
## N.B. software from this repository is ENTIRELY UNSUPPORTED by the Ubuntu
## team, and may not be under a free licence. Please satisfy yourself as to
## your rights to use the software. Also, please note that software in
## universe WILL NOT receive any review or updates from the Ubuntu security
## team.
# deb [http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper universe
# deb-src [http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper universe

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'backports'
## repository.

## extensively as that contained in the main release, although it includes
## newer versions of some applications which may provide useful features.
## Also, please note that software in backports WILL NOT receive any review
## or updates from the Ubuntu security team.

# deb-src [http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse

deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main restricted

# deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe

Make it look exactly like this:

```
deb http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper main restricted
deb-src http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper main restricted

## Major bug fix updates produced after the final release of the
## distribution.
deb http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper-updates main restricted
deb-src http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper-updates main restricted

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'universe'
## repository.
## N.B. software from this repository is ENTIRELY UNSUPPORTED by the Ubuntu
## team, and may not be under a free licence. Please satisfy yourself as to
## your rights to use the software. Also, please note that software in
## universe WILL NOT receive any review or updates from the Ubuntu security
## team.
deb http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper universe
deb-src http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper universe

## Uncomment the following two lines to add software from the 'backports'
## repository.
## N.B. software from this repository may not have been tested as
## extensively as that contained in the main release, although it includes
## newer versions of some applications which may provide useful features.
## Also, please note that software in backports WILL NOT receive any review
## or updates from the Ubuntu security team.
# deb http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse
# deb-src http://gb.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper-backports main restricted universe multiverse

deb http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security main restricted
deb-src http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security main restricted
deb http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security universe
deb-src http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu dapper-security universe

deb http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ dapper universe main restricted multiverse
```

### Post by shanonf on 2006-09-04
Hi,

I installed Kubuntu dapper 64 bit version a couple of months ago. In order to go to Linux full time I really needed to get my 2nd monitor working. I have the NVIDIA 6200 video card. I have 2 Dell 1905FP Digital monitors. 

I found your guide and installed the nvidia driver using step one. It worked great except for one thing. On my 2nd monitor, running analog, the screen resolution isn't right. It is hard to explain, but the desktop is too big for my screen. If I move my mouse to the far right of the monitor, the desktop scrolls to the right. It does it in all four directions. If I put a window onto the second monitor and make it full screen, I have to scroll to the right, find the edge, and resize it so that it fits without scrolling. Sometimes, when I open a program, it starts way to the right on my second monitor and I can just barely see the edge of it. I can scroll over and see the whole thing, then move it back over to the left. 

I set the resolution for both monitors in System Settings. The resolutions are both set to 1280x1024. My main screen works fine, the secondary one isn't.

When the new driver came out, I gave it a try, using Step 2 from your guide, since I don't need the restricted modules. No problems with the install, but same problem with screen 2. Is it because I am running it analog?

These montiors worked with this card and in this resolution in Windows XP.

Thanks for your help!

Shanon

### Post by jixun on 2006-09-06
tseliot, great effort! :cool: 

FYI, I ran into a little problem when using Method 2. After running the nvidia-installer, it reported not being able to find the correct X11 path because pkg-config wasn't installed. After which, kdm wouldn't start.

So, I uninstalled it (nvidia-installer --uninstall), then apt-get pkg-config, then re-ran the installer. It worked perfectly this time.

I am using Kubuntu (dapper) 64-bit

p.s. I wasn't using your Envy script. If pkg-config is already included there, then just ignore this post :D

### Post by tseliot on 2006-09-06
> **loki74 said:**
> It only works upon first installation. After rebooting, I think the drivers are not loaded properly.  The nvidia-settings program no longer shows options (such as gamma correction, etc), and the nvidia splash screen does not come up.  I tried point 15 to add 'nvidia-settings --load-config-only' because the problem only occurs on reboot. If I uninstall, and reinstall, it works, but does not survice a reboot.

Thanks!

Ok, let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

### Post by tseliot on 2006-09-10
> **BLTicklemonster said:**
> My desktop keeps giving me flashbacks. I see trails when I drag anything. Is this gnome, or my computer, or my card?

amd athlon 2600+

512 ram

80 7200 rpm hd

Try using this option in the Section Device of your xorg.conf:
```
Option "RenderAccel" "False"
```


Or try setting the default colour depth to 16

A little help please? :)

use Envy "uninstall" function and then install the driver again

then type (to restore the default xorg.conf):
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
```

and then:
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

Thanks for your work, and please let me know if any other information would be useful.

Dr. Dweebis
Try point 10 of the Problems Section of this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION)

### Post by EyeBun2 on 2006-09-12
I have a GeForce4 MX 440. My screen resolution was stuck at 640 X 480 and I was unable to install the proper NVidia drivers. I tried a multitude of methods that I found during my search enquiries but nothing worked. I was almost gave up and was considering a reinstall of M$. 

One more kick at the can and I landed here at this forum where I found [Tseliot's Guide]("http://www.albertomilone.eu/europeo/latest_nvidia_udsf.html"). By following his exact instructions I was able to install the 8762 driver and change my resolution to 1280 X 1024. Before I dived into the guide I first read all of the instructions as well as the troubleshooting section just to be sure that I understood every step. I checked in the Notes to see if my chip was supported or if I needed a Legacy driver. The chip was supported so I moved on to Method #1 and I also did Step #7. 

It worked like a charm. This is the best Nvidia Guide I have found. Thank you very much Tseliot. You are a great asset to Ubuntu.

---

### Post by gregbazar on 2006-09-14
I just did an upgrade to the 2.16.26-686 kernel for SMP support.  I also installed the 686 restricted modules so that I could keep the "nvidia" driver matched up correctly. Everything was working dandy with "nvidia" and my 386 kernel on my Sony Viao SZ160P.

When I rebooted, I got this in dmesg:

```
[17179655.524000] nvidia: disagrees about version of symbol boot_cpu_data
[17179655.524000] nvidia: Unknown symbol boot_cpu_data

```

Basically the nvidia kernel module never loads.  I have un-installed and re-installed to no avail.

If I switch to the "nv" driver, everything comes up fine.  Is anyone aware of an icompatibility in the nvidia linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-26-686 driver with the linux-image-2.6.15-26-686 kernel?

Thanks,

Greg

---

### Post by rdilliker on 2006-09-17
I have an nvidia 6600 video card and a Dell P780 17" CRT. I installed the nvidia drivers per your walkthrough method 1, but I have black bars on the left and right sides of my screen. These black bars aren't there in WinXP.I tried explicity putting the monitor screen height and width under the monitor section but it made no difference. My xorg.conf file is below:
```

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
	# path to defoma fonts
	FontPath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"

	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc104"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"

	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"

	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"

	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
  Driver        "wacom"
  Identifier    "stylus"
  Option        "Device"        "/dev/wacom"          # Change to

EndSection

  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

                                                      # /dev/input/event
                                                      # for USB
  Option        "Type"          "cursor"
  Option        "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"               # Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA GeForce 6600"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	BusID		"PCI:5:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"DELL P780"
	Option		"DPMS"
	HorizSync	30-85
	VertRefresh	48-120
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"NVIDIA GeForce 6600"
	Monitor		"DELL P780"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1920x1440" "1920x1200" "1856x1392" "1792x1344" "1680x1050" "1600x1200" "1440x900" "1400x1050" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1280x854" "1280x800" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1152x864" "1152x768" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"

### Post by Hyuuga on 2006-09-18

returns this message:

### Post by tseliot on 2006-09-18
> **Hyuuga said:**
> this part:
gksudo gedit /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop

returns this message:
(gksudo:8789): Gtk-WARNING **: cannot open display:

please tell me what on earth i'm not getting here. I followed all the other steps to the dot and this one too.
Are you typing that while you're using GNOME?

btw you could also type:
```
sudo gedit /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop
```

or if you have shut down the Xserver you can use nano:
```
sudo nano -w /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop
```

> **Hyuuga said:**
> also, will the driver let me choose the resolution 1290 x 960 60hz? currently it won't go above 1024 768 85hz

Of course.

But if it doesn't, try point 10 of the Problems Section of this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION)

Then if that didn't solve the problem:

Try this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution)


OR this one (read where it says "Getting the Right Mode for an LCD monitor"):
[https://wiki.caosity.org/tiki-index.php?page=X%20Server%20Configuration](https://wiki.caosity.org/tiki-index.php?page=X%20Server%20Configuration)

---

### Post by Hyuuga on 2006-09-18
> **tseliot said:**
> Are you typing that while you're using GNOME?

btw you could also type:
```
sudo gedit /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop
```


that gives this message: "cannot open display: (null)"
but the nano command worked, but it doesn't tell me how to save the file. Sorry to be such a big trouble.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-09-18
> **Hyuuga said:**
> that gives this message: "cannot open display: (null)"
but the nano command worked, but it doesn't tell me how to save the file. Sorry to be such a big trouble.

Press "CTRL+O" to save the file
"CTRL+X" to exit

---

### Post by Hyuuga on 2006-09-18
alright! thanks a lot.

### Post by onlyoneskwalla on 2006-09-18
Ran into KoRnholio's problem also when using Method 2 install(couldn't use envy 6800gt sli says it's not supported), anyways I found the cause of the error to be the way I installed xgl. Specifically part 5 of this [HowTo:Amd64+ Nvidia + xgl/compiz]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=133427") 

Probably has to do with the installer moving things around or deleting them. Just undid the "sudo gedit /etc/gdm/gdm.conf-custom" part and restarted gdm with no problems. Note haven't gotten around to trying a reinstall of xgl, just been too busy.

Update: just reinstalled xgl via the guide and no problems smooth sailing.

Try point 10 of the Problems Section of this guide:

[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution)

[n](n)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-09-21
> **thepanu said:**
> Thank you for your response.

I did complete re-installation and formatting of partitions between trying methods 1 & 2 and envy. So I think there shouldn't be any problems left by previous attempts.

I tried adding those lines to xorg.conf. And it now it went a little further. It displayed Kubuntu splash screen and load bar but hanged there. After pushing power button it started system shutdown. Same thing after reboot.

I have now done uninstallation via Envy and going to try method 1 again.

EDIT:

Followed Method 1 again. No change in end result. Added all lines recommended in problem#4 with no help. Again blank screen when restarting X.

Ok, let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

Try point 10 of the Problems Section of this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION)

Then if that didn't solve the problem:

Try this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution)


OR this one (read where it says "Getting the Right Mode for an LCD monitor"):
[https://wiki.caosity.org/tiki-index.php?page=X%20Server%20Configuration](https://wiki.caosity.org/tiki-index.php?page=X%20Server%20Configuration)

### Post by catanzag on 2006-09-22
Hi Tseliot,

yesterday I installed Xgl/Compiz following what is written   in [http://ubuntuguide.org](http://ubuntuguide.org). The only trouble I got was ```
 $ thefuture
 $ compiz.real: GLX_EXT_texture_from_pixmap is missing
 compiz.real: Failed to manage screen: 0
 compiz.real: No managable screens found on display :0.0

```
Differently like suggested in point 12 of Problem Section of your wonderful guide [Latest Nvidia Dapper]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper") (many thanks for it!!!), the only way I got to get around the problem is to add the following code at the end of /etc/X11/xorg.conf file
```
Section "Extensions"
          Option  "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection
```

I have a GeForce4 420 Go nvidia-glx installed

Regards
catanzag

### Post by icantdothatdave on 2006-09-23
Hello!

I've been trying to solve this problem for months. First off, I have a Dell Latitude C840 with an Nvidia GeForce4 440 Go running Dapper 6.06 LTS. I can install the normal nvidia-glx driver from the repos, and Ubuntu will boot with 3D acceleration and all. The only problem is that the desktop isn't filling the whole screen (it's forcing 1400x1050 on a 1600x1200 monitor). There is an inch of the screen on the bottom that is a repeat of the top inch of the screen, and an inch on the right side of the screen that is just garbage. Ubuntu won't let me select 1600x1200 for the resolution even though it's in my xorg.conf, and anyway 1400x1050 should be expanding to fill the whole screen but isn't. I have already added the following lines to my xorg.conf screen section:

    Option "ExactModeTimingsDVI"
    Option "UseEdidDpi" "FALSE"
    Option "ModeValidation" "NoEdidDFPMaxSizeCheck, NoVesaModes"

and the following lines have been added to /etc/modprobe.d/options:

    # Enable double-buffering so gstreamer et. al. work
    options quickcam compatible=2

    # Default hostap to managed mode
    options hostap_pci iw_mode=2
    options hostap_cs iw_mode=2
    options nvidia NVreg_SoftEDIDs=0 NVreg_Mobile=4

I have tried the script, but it doesn't seem to be compatible with the latest kernel (2.6.15-27-686). PLEASE HELP! :confused:


EDIT:

Ha! I finally decided to write in the forums after months of searching, and immediately afterward, I find the answer! :-P  For anyone else having the same problem, the answer is in number 10 of the problems section. Basically, add:

Option "UseEDID" "False"

and then in the modelines, add the refresh rate you want to the resolution you plan to use:

Modes "1600x1200_**60**"

Well, thank you whoever added that to the wiki, it wasn't there the last time I read the through the whole darn thing ;)

---

### Post by pseudonym on 2006-09-25
Here's my experience to throw into the mix.

Installed 8774 driver using method 2 with a custom 2.6.17 kernel from kernel.org. Didn't use the script, but added the '-x-prefix' option to my command line, figuring it might be needed for Ubuntu. Didn't specify the kernel-source path and it just defaulted to /lib/modules/uname-r/source (I built a headers package along with my kernel, which I don't usually bother with, but both nvidia graphics and sound drivers compiled flawlessly nonetheless).

Can report that everything video related is working fine.

Might I offer a suggestion for your howto (it's probably already been thought of, but I can't be bothered checking :) )? It's a little unwieldy to read, so it would be a good idea to split it up into 3 different howtos in separate documents - 1 for non-legacy cards, one for legacy cards, and one for 64-bit. There would necessarily be some overall duplication involved, but for the sake of conciseness and clarity it would be worth it.

Another thing would be to make each howto as readable as possible from start to finish, without having to refer readers back to previous or subsequent steps based on what they've done or will do. Splitting them up would again help with this.

But you probably already thought of all that :)

Thanks for all your tips anyway!

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-02
> **Murwiz said:**
> Yes, I can toggle the "*" that appears. No matter what options are on or off (all on, all off, some of each), the screen reappears.

I think you should file a bug on launchpad about this.

In the meantime you can type:
```
sudo nano -w /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

get to the Section Device of the file and set the driver to "nvidia"

CTRL+X to exit (save the file)

---

### Post by Murwiz on 2006-10-02
Actually, that's already done. At present, xorg.conf looks like this:

```
Section "Device"
        Identifier      "NVIDIA Corporation NV37GL [Quadro FX 330]"
        Driver          "nvidia"
#       Driver          "nv"
        BusID           "PCI:5:0:0"
        BoardName       "NVIDIA GeForce 2 MX (generic)"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
        Identifier      "Generic Monitor"
        Option          "DPMS"
        HorizSync       28-51
        VertRefresh     43-60
EndSection

Section "Screen"
        Identifier      "Default Screen"
        Device          "NVIDIA Corporation NV37GL [Quadro FX 330]"
        Monitor         "Generic Monitor"
        DefaultDepth    24
        SubSection "Display"
                Depth           1
                Modes           "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
        EndSubSection
```

with "Display" repeated for Depth 4, 8, 15, 16, and 24.

### Post by NDN on 2006-10-05
Thank you so much.

I spent days shitting around with my Nvidia Geforce 4 Ti 4200, my Xerox XA 19i monitor und those nvidia drivers in Ubunto 6.06... and it finally worked out. 

It was always the same: I installed the driver and when I entered Ubuntu, there was only a black screen.

This was also the case when I used your Method 2 Install Package. But then I altered my backup of thexorg.conf and added "_60" to "1240*1024" and "1024*768" and it worked out (I also altered "vesa" to "nvidia".

Thank you very much for your work! (especially because I am a Linux newbie and was quite frustrated because of these graphics issues)

Regards
Andi

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-10
> **Timothy Butwinick said:**
> Hello. I am using an old Nvidia Riva TNT2 card, and have tried method 1. At the reboot, X gave me some error messages (I 
have attached the log file to this message), saying that the Nvidia drivers can't be loaded, and that I should restart 
GDM when the 
problem is fixed. 

I do like the command line, but it would be nice to have a GUI as well...

Try these commands when you get that error:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (or "kdm stop" if you use KDM)
```

```
sudo rmmod nvidia
```
```

sudo modprobe nvidia
```

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (or "kdm restart" if you use KDM)
```


Please, tell me if it works

### Post by Markkreuzz on 2006-10-12
Hello... 
This method worked for me, i got it from the 2nd page posted by **r4ik** but slightly modified. 8-[ , i don't know which one of tseliots guide this one belongs to but here goes....

Method:
added "http://www.albertomilone.com/drivers/dapper/nonlegacy/32bit"  to repository

sudo apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-27-k7 
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx
sudo nvidia-xconfig 
reboot 
stared at nvidia logo during startup and smiled

Details:
using ubuntu Dappper 6.06.1
kernel 2.6.15-27-k7 
Hardware biostar geForce 6100-m9
Nvidia Integrated Video 6100
CPU: socket 939 Athlon64 3000+
Nvidia drivers 1.0-8774

Note: 
somehow after i installed this drivers the mouse moves smoothly, before it was stuttering and freezing 2-3 seconds then works again, which is very annoying...  it works properly now. Thanks Very much...

```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-20
> **yellow beard said:**
> Im follwing method 2 of the tut but when i get to: 

```
sudo cp /usr/src/name_of_your_source/arch/i386/Makefile.cpu /usr/src/kernel-headers-`uname -r`/arch/i386/
```

Theres no such file or dir as kernel-headers. Theres only the dirs linux-headers-2.6.15-27 and linux-headers-2.6.15-27-386.
There's no need to use that command if you use a kernel from Ubuntu's repositories.

let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

```
sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-21
> **DraeLee said:**
> I am stuck at the point where it says to edit the xorg.conf file only when gedit opens it it is completely blank there is nothing there.  

sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx nvidia-kernel-common
sudo nvidia-glx-config enable

In my opinion "sudo nvidia-glx-config enable" shouldn't be used as it can mess up your xorg.conf.

You should use this command instead:
```
sudo nvidia-xconfig
```

That command will set up your xorg.conf properly

Ok, you got the driver to work.

Boot in Recovery Mode (select it from the GRUB menu almost as soon as you turn on your computer) and try point 10 of the Problems Section of this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION)

Then if that didn't solve the problem:

Try this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution)


OR this one (read where it says "Getting the Right Mode for an LCD monitor"):

### Post by DraeLee on 2006-10-21

I am glad that i didnt give up on ubuntu and kept at it.  now i got my video card installed and it seems to be running i dont know how to test it but it looks good

Try point 10 of the Problems Section of this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Dapper#PROBLEMS_SECTION)

Then if that didn't solve the problem:

Try this guide:
[http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution](http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/ChangeResolution)


OR this one (read where it says "Getting the Right Mode for an LCD monitor"):
[https://wiki.caosity.org/tiki-index.php?page=X%20Server%20Configuration](https://wiki.caosity.org/tiki-index.php?page=X%20Server%20Configuration)


If none of the above solves the problem you can try point 4 of the Problems Section

and I have changed several things as well.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-24
> **yellow beard said:**
> I uninstalled all the drivers, and tried a fresh install with method one. Upon restart of x this is the error i received:

```
FATAL:could not open
/lib/module/2.6.15-27-386/kernel/drivers/video/nvidia.ko
```

I installed the restricted modules like the tut said, although they seem to be linux-restricted-modules-2.6.15-23-386 where my kernel version is 27 not 23. I don't know if that matters or not I just thought id point it out.
Try with:
```
sudo aptitude linux-386
```

and
```

sudo aptitude linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-24
> **BLTicklemonster said:**
> Is that the blob vulnerability I read about over on /. ?
I guess so
> **BLTicklemonster said:**
> And if I do, I won't have any problems, will I? I suppose going to kde from gnome, I'll have to reinstall, prolly, but that's it, right?
You shouldn't have any problem.

You have to reinstall the driver (so as to update to 8776).

Changing from KDE to GNOME won't change your kernel therefore it won't break the driver.

### Post by Henry Hollenberg on 2006-10-25
Nice HowTo.

I installed ubuntu dapper (6.06.1)(gnome version) last night 
on an AMD64 platform running nvidia video card: 
NVIDIA GPU GeForce 6800 GT. Sound, and networking 
worked right out of the box.

I also have a wide screen sony lcd Sony SDM-P234 1920x1200 which
led to all sorts of problems.

Video defaulted to about 800x600 which was kind of cramped.

So I tried all sorts of tricks that I found around the internet including turning off autodetection of video settings ie:

Option "UseEDID" "FALSE" in Screen/Device section of /etc/X11/xorg.conf

None of which worked.

So I decided that perhaps the problem was the opensource "nv" driver
and maybe I needed the (closed source?) "nvidia" driver for the Xorg graphics system.

So, I tried method 1 of this HowTo and basically did:

Since I saw some apt-get stuff in these directions I
first went to /etc/apt/sources.list and uncommented some
lines that seemed to make sense:

# Line commented out by installer because it failed to verify:
deb [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper main restricted
# Line commented out by installer because it failed to verify:
deb-src [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper main restricted

## Major bug fix updates produced after the final release of the
## distribution.
# Line commented out by installer because it failed to verify:
deb [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-updates main restricted
# Line commented out by installer because it failed to verify:
deb-src [http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/](http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/) dapper-updates main restricted

# Line commented out by installer because it failed to verify:
deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main
# Line commented out by installer because it failed to verify:
deb-src [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security main
# deb [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe
# deb-src [http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu](http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu) dapper-security universe

I would suggest that the installer request you to set up networking
prior to moving on with the installation so that these lines might
be uncommented automatically.....which it looked like the system
attempted to do during the install but as I hadn't set up networking
on that boot/install iteration (tried kubuntu first) it seemed to skip
over this step.


hgh@andy:~$ uname -r
2.6.15-26-amd64-generic

root@andy:/home/hgh# apt-get install linux-amd64-generic
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
linux-amd64-generic is already the newest version.
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 38 not upgraded.

apt-get install nvidia-glx

nvidia-xconfig

vi /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop
i (to begin text insertion mode in vi)

drag-and-plop this stuff:

[Desktop Entry]
Name=NVIDIA Settings
Comment=NVIDIA X Server Settings
Exec=nvidia-settings
Icon=
StartupNotify=true
Terminal=false
Type=Application
Categories=Application;System;

:wq (to save and close the file)

Unfortunately this didn't work.

However, when I removed this entry from the config file /etc/X11/xorg.conf:
Option "UseEDID" "FALSE"

It magically worked and I had a beautiful 1920x1200 display with acres
of room to work in.



What didn't work was the other resolutions that I carefully built modelines for using the 'gtf' utility (see 'man gtf'):

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "SDM-P234"
    HorizSync       28-92
    VertRefresh     48-85
    Option         "DPMS"

# 1920x1200 @ 48.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 59.23 kHz; pclk: 151.63 MHz
    Modeline "1920x1200_48.00"  151.63  1920 2032 2240 2560  1200 1201 1204 1234  -HSync +Vsync

# 1600x1000 @ 65.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 67.47 kHz; pclk: 144.66 MHz
    Modeline "1600x1000_65.00"  144.66  1600 1704 1872 2144  1000 1001 1004 1038  -HSync +Vsync

# 1392x870 @ 80.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 72.88 kHz; pclk: 136.43 MHz
    Modeline "1392x870_80.00"  136.43  1392 1480 1632 1872  870 871 874 911  -HSync +Vsync

# 1280x800 @ 85.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 71.40 kHz; pclk: 123.38 MHz
    Modeline "1280x800_85.00"  123.38  1280 1368 1504 1728  800 801 804 840  -HSync +Vsync

# 1024x640 @ 85.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 57.12 kHz; pclk: 77.68 MHz
    Modeline "1024x640_85.00"  77.68  1024 1080 1192 1360  640 641 644 672  -HSync +Vsync

# 800x500 @ 85.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 44.71 kHz; pclk: 46.50 MHz
    Modeline "800x500_85.00"  46.50  800 840 920 1040  500 501 504 526  -HSync +Vsync

# 640x400 @ 85.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 35.78 kHz; pclk: 29.20 MHz
    Modeline "640x400_85.00"  29.20  640 664 728 816  400 401 404 421  -HSync +Vsync
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Monitor        "SDM-P234"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1920x1200_48.00" "1600x1000_65.00" "1392x870_80.00" "1280x800_85.00" "1024x640_85.00" "800x500_85.00" "640x400_85.00"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

These all threw errors in /var/log/Xorg.0.log

(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1920x1200_48.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1600x1000_65.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1392x870_80.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1280x800_85.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "1024x640_85.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "800x500_85.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0): No valid modes for "640x400_85.00"; removing.
(WW) NVIDIA(0):
(WW) NVIDIA(0): Unable to validate any modes; falling back to the default mode
(WW) NVIDIA(0):     "nvidia-auto-select".
(WW) NVIDIA(0):
(II) NVIDIA(0): Validated modes:
(II) NVIDIA(0):     "nvidia-auto-select"
(II) NVIDIA(0): Virtual screen size determined to be 1920 x 1200


I took this kind of personally since I had gone to the trouble to calculate
up all these maintaining a 16:10 aspect ratio for all the lines ie
1600x1000 pixels (H x V).  I was trying to keep it under 155MHz MaxPixClck
but wasn't sure if this was neccesary, kind of guessing from this line:

(--) NVIDIA(0): Sony SDM-P234 (DFP-1): 155.0 MHz maximum pixel clock

in /var/log/Xorg.0.log

So that's my saga.  Hope it helps someone else.  And by the way
thanks for keeping clean package mirrors so the apt-get steps
could go smoothly.  If anyone knows why my modelines won't work
would appreciate a pointer.

hgh.

---

### Post by Henry Hollenberg on 2006-10-25
Ooops!,

Might have spoken to soon.  The cool little update icon
was popping up a balloon saying I had some new packages so
I went ahead an upgraded 30 or so, including a couple
of kernels.

Bammm!, graphics are gone.

Repeated the install steps and Bammmm!, No graphics.

uname -r was 2.6.15-26-amd64-generic

and is now: 2.6.15-27-amd64-generic

What to do now?

hgh.

### Post by Henry Hollenberg on 2006-10-25
Well, that was a few hours of fun!

After breaking my method 1 install with an apt-get kernel upgrade...

I uninstalled/reinstalled according to method 1 a bunch 
of times before and after uncommenting all available sites in my:

/etc/apt/sources.list file.

Several hours later: No joy.

So, I was about to try method 2 when I noticed that reference
to envy, hmmmm what's that about....looks like an
automated/scripted version of method 2.

Let's give that a whirl.....

downloaded it on my laptop.
sftp'ed it over to this $4K no-gui piece of junk desktop

installed it:
root@andy:/home/hgh# dpkg -i envy_0.4.3-1ubuntu3_all.deb

ran it:
root@andy:/home/hgh# envy
1
<ENTER>

WOW!, worked like a charm built me a new 
driver off the nvidia site to match my
running kernel.  Now ain't that slick.

Man, this desktop system sure is charm'in all the sudden.

Thanks again Alberto, you da man!

hgh.

---

### Post by BLTicklemonster on 2006-10-26
Okay, I had to boot to XP for the first time since Oct 2 this evening. We had our clan meeting and were going into the server to play some Unreal Tournament, and the game wouldn't start. I didn't have time to mess with anything, so I went to XP. I just came back and looked at my log to see what happened, and it says 

> Critical: appError called:
Critical: Could not load OpenGL library

Well, everything is where it ought to be, so all I can figure is that the new drivers are messing me up. Perhaps I ought to edit xorg? I did go to the nvidia control panel, and noticed this under opengl/glx settings

> The OpenGL extension 'GLX' is not supported by
the X server or there was a problem retrieving
GLX information from the X server.

So I guess that's it.

I'll dick around and see what I come up with and edit this post.

*edit:

Okay, here's from xorg.conf

> Section "Module"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

so glx is set to load. I wonder what's going on?

*edit again

Okay, booted to recovery, uninstalled the nvidia drivers, then installed them again, but didn't start x because HAL doesn't like that. Just typed reboot and everything works now. I have the glx info in my nvidia panel, and UT is starting up again.

Whew.

---

No luck at all with envy--it told me my vid cards weren't supported (I have an AGP geforce 5200 and a PCI geforce 5500. It's old hardware, but it still runs.) With 8776 the module built but I can't get modelines or metamodes or even display subsections to work. ### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-29
> **Zaggy said:**
> Hi, I'm using Envy 0.4.2-1ubuntu9 , and it seems to work fine, but in everything 3D (glxgears, Warcraft3(with wine), UT2k4(native), etc)
I see a sudden freeze every second/ 2 seconds.

I'm pretty clueless on what to do, should I get the latest envy?

### Post by tseliot on 2006-10-31
> **riddlebox said:**
> Hello,
I am not using the beta version I am using the dapper version but it is the second one, the one that stops the md5sum errors. It installed correctly but shortly after boot my system will lock up.  The tv-out is working until the system locks up.

Thanks

boot in RECOVERY MODE from the GRUB Menu (select it using your keyboard) almost as soon as you turn on your computer (it will take you to the command line).

then try point 4 of the Problems Section (from the command line).

**If that doesn't work**:
boot in RECOVERY MODE from the GRUB Menu (select it using your keyboard) almost as soon as you turn on your computer (it will take you to the command line).

Then you will need to type:
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

and select the "vesa" driver. Press ENTER whenever you don't know the answer to a question.

Type:
```
reboot
```

On next reboot the Xserver should work fine

### Post by yellow beard on 2006-10-31
Woap finally got the drivers going! It seems that "Option "NvAGP" "0" was the problem". If I dont have that in my xorg.conf file, the drivers will not work. Everything seems to work fine with out these options:

	Option "RenderAccel" "Off"
	Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
	Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off"
	Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off"
	Option "UseEDID" "False"

so yeah. there was a problem with our proxy server that stopped me getting the proper restricted modules i needed as well. But thats all fixed now.

But... when i try to run glxgears i get this message:

```
Error: Could not open /dev/nvidiactl because the permissions
are too resticitive.  Please see the FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
section of /usr/share/doc/NVIDIA_GLX-1.0/README for steps
to correct.
Segmentation fault
```

But i can run it fine as root. ](*,)

---

### Post by yabbadabbadont on 2006-10-31
> **yellow beard said:**
> Woap finally got the drivers going! It seems that "Option "NvAGP" "0" was the problem". If I dont have that in my xorg.conf file, the drivers will not work. Everything seems to work fine with out these options:

	Option "RenderAccel" "Off"
	Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
	Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off"
	Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off"
	Option "UseEDID" "False"

so yeah. there was a problem with our proxy server that stopped me getting the proper restricted modules i need as well. But thats all fixed now.

But... when i try to run glxgears i get this message:

```
Error: Could not open /dev/nvidiactl because the permissions
are too resticitive.  Please see the FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
section of /usr/share/doc/NVIDIA_GLX-1.0/README for steps
to correct.
Segmentation fault
```

But i can run it fine as root. ](*,)

The permissions should look like this:
```
/home/bubba $ ls -l /dev/nv*
crw-rw-rw- 1 root root 195,   0 2006-10-31 14:48 /dev/nvidia0
crw-rw-rw- 1 root root 195, 255 2006-10-31 14:48 /dev/nvidiactl

```

---

### Post by yabbadabbadont on 2006-10-31
```
/usr/share/doc/NVIDIA_GLX-1.0 $ grep -i nvagp README.txt 
Option "NvAGP" "integer"
AGP: you can choose to either use NVIDIA's AGP module (NVAGP), or the AGP
the "NvAGP" option in your X config file:
    Option "NvAgp" "0"  ... disables AGP support
    Option "NvAgp" "1"  ... use NVAGP, if possible
    Option "NvAgp" "2"  ... use AGPGART, if possible
    Option "NvAGP" "3"  ... try AGPGART; if that fails, try NVAGP
    hangs on your ASUS KT400 system with either Linux AGPGART or NvAGP enabled
    workaround is to use NVAGP or update to a newer kernel. Known problematic
    To configure NVAGP, please see Appendix F.
restore relevant register state on such systems; NVIDIA's NvAGP is notified of
NVIDIA's NvAGP driver.

```
Hope that helps.  ;)

### Post by yabbadabbadont on 2006-11-01
> **yellow beard said:**
> Im having problems again.. everytime i restart the permissions are being reset to this:

aaron@erv:~$ ls -l /dev/nv*
crw-rw---- 1 root video 195,   0 2006-11-02 08:52 /dev/nvidia0

### Post by oswallt on 2006-11-05
I tried using method 1, but I guess the kernel in edgy has been updated.  So, I've tried method 2 several times, but after the usplash the screen just stays blank and x never starts.  (I think. At least my screen says "no signal")  I've been trying to get this working for over 6 hours now and tried a plethora of guides, too many to list.  I think I have undone everything I did, though.  I'm afraid I need help.

I have a BFG 6600oc AGP card and a 20" wide screen display.  I'm a linux newbie, so I don't know what other info would be useful.  Any help is much appreciated.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-11-05
> **oswallt said:**
> I tried using method 1, but I guess the kernel in edgy has been updated.  So, I've tried method 2 several times, but after the usplash the screen just stays blank and x never starts.  (I think. At least my screen says "no signal")  I've been trying to get this working for over 6 hours now and tried a plethora of guides, too many to list.  I think I have undone everything I did, though.  I'm afraid I need help.

I have a BFG 6600oc AGP card and a 20" wide screen display.  I'm a linux newbie, so I don't know what other info would be useful.  Any help is much appreciated.

let's try to diagnose the error:

Boot with the "nv" driver in your xorg.conf

Then (after Ubuntu has booted) Set the driver to "nvidia" in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Log out and Press CTRL+ALT+F1

You will get to the command line (no GUI)
Type:

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then:
```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will see several lines with the same error.

Then if you can't get to the command line press CTRL+ALT+F1 or CTRL+C (select NO if it asks you if you want to see the output of the error or something like that)

Then type this in order to copy the output of the error to your home folder:
```
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
```
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

Access the GNOME or KDE again in the following way:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm restart (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm restart (if you use KDM)
```


Post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by yabbadabbadont on 2006-11-05
The file you posted shows that you are using the opensource, nv driver and not the official nvidia driver.  Did you just change it so that you could post the file?  (I'll assume so)  When you have 'Driver "nvidia"' in your xorg.conf, try adding to that same section: Option "NvAGP" "1"

If that doesn't work, try changing the "1" to "3" or "2".

### Post by Wim Sturkenboom on 2006-11-13
If the following info was already posted, apologies (I'm not going to read all 140 pages of comments).

My comments:
1)
Main reason to install the NVidia driver is that the NV driver does not work properly on a Biostar CRU51-M9 motherboard (as far as I know the same as the Biostart Geforce 6100-M9).
Symptoms:
- video/computer* regularly freezes for up to 20 seconds
- artefacts (i.e while using backspace in firefox)

2)
Method 1 works like a dream on the above motherboard; I've used the 386 version.

Note:
I'm not 100% sure what freezes, but the system does not repond at all in that period.

### Post by benyau on 2006-12-16
Tseliot,
  Excellent instructions.  

Method #2 almost worked for me.  The only modification I needed was to reboot before running the installer.  It seems the nvidia installer needs the target kernel running for it to install successfully.

If step 5 is a reboot instead of ctrl-alt-F1, it will have worked perfectly for my case. 

FYI.  I built a vanilla kernel, 2.6.19.1, on Edgy.

---

### Post by Shiva_T on 2006-12-21
Hi,

Thanks for the great guide, tseliot, I got the drivers installed fine on Edgy. However, the highest resolution option it gives me is 1280x720. I'm using a 1080p Samsung HDTV via DVI-HDMI, and it overscans quite a bit. So I have two problems:

1. I can't set the resolution higher than 1280x720, either through the NVIDIA Control Panel, or through the Screen Resolution setting.
2. At 1280x720 or even 800x600, the TV is overscanning like crazy.

What I'd really like to do is to set the resolution to 1768x992, which works perfectly in Windows and gives me a full, non-overscanned desktop.

I've made the following change to my xorg.conf file:

```
Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Samsung"
    HorizSync       32.0 - 79.0
    VertRefresh     59.0 - 79.0
    Option         "DPMS"
    Modeline "1768x992" 145.42  1768 1872 2064 2360   992  993  996 1027 +hsync +vsync
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "7600GT"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "7600GT"
    Monitor        "Samsung"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1768x992" "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

Still no luck; I don't see the 1768x992 mode under screen resolution. Any idea what might be wrong?

I really need to get this resolution working before I can start using my new Linux installation.

Thanks!

---

Thanks again
Eric

### Post by Inglor on 2007-01-04
First of all thanks for script :)
Secondly sorry for my long post

Now, I used to have an ATI graphic card nad 3D was working basically, but I changed to Nvidia (6800 Ultra).
I followed the Ubuntu guide with no results cause I was getting an error when trying to install nvidia-glx package cause of some conflict with ATI packages, seems i had to remove them first (logically) which I couldn't :confused: 
I tried removing the packages manually but didn't worked well since I had to recompile the ATI drivers to function properly (or at least that is what I think).
After an evening searching forums and sites I thought to use official drivers (why didn't I thought of that earlier.. I wonder)
After running the 15Mb script I get a message nvidia drivers are installed :o 
I log in X server to find a server running almost like a failed 3D enviroment.. Mouse was stacking every half second for some milliseconds, annoying (note: i don't use XGL).
So I run envy
I thought I should do a "clean" install (as clean as t could be) so I first choose to Uninstall nvidia driver.
Uninstallation done.
And now install, and voila, installation completed :)
But XServer had a different opinion.

Failed to start XServer
/var/log/Xorg.0.log

Try this:
```
cp  /usr/lib/libGL.so.1 $HOME/
```
```
cp -R /usr/lib/fglrx/ $HOME/
```

then:
```
sudo rm -R /usr/lib/fglrx/
```

and try to install nvidia-glx again

### Post by tseliot on 2007-01-14
> **venik212 said:**
> Could someone please tell me how to get Ubuntu working again, after I replaced an old, lousy monitor with a higher resolution one?  I ran the ENVY script again from teh Recovery Mode (after Ubuntu refused to start the X11 thingie), but now it complains of an API mismatch.  I guess my NVIDIA driver is still not 1.0-9631 but the pre-Xmas 1.0-9667, which ENVY had installed a few weeks ago.
I need help!
Thanks,

Use the uninstall function of Envy and then the install function.

NOTE: make sure that you run Envy outside of the desktop environment, as described in the instructions:
[http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html](http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html)

### Post by venik212 on 2007-01-14
If people considering switching to Linux from Windows knew that they will have to recompile the Linux kernel simply because they plugged their computer into a larger monitor, it will DRASTICALLY reduce the number of such conversions.  This is simply unacceptable.  
I do not know much about the Linux world, so I am not sure who should fix/change this situation, but you simply cannot expect *users* to do this sort of thing.  Drivers for ALL hardware components should be easily and readily available, or else Linux shall no longer be a serious contender.

### Post by chinocracy on 2007-01-21
Hi, I had posted this problem in other parts of the forum when I should have posted here in the first place. 
I keep failing my various attempts to install Nvidia's driver on my MX400. I did sudo sh installs, and then modify the xorg.conf, sometimes using nvidia-xconfig. I've tried several drivers, 9629, 9631, 8776 and even 7174 (legacy). I install by stopping X, install, then restart X. But if I restart the computer, X fails. If I use other drivers aside from 7174, and I restart, I always get the error that I have this driver version, but the kernel module is for 7174, so it won't start. So I install 7174 via sh, and I tried synaptic too. But it also fails, and I get the message that xorg cannot load glx and module. I just do the dpkg-reconfig thing afterwards to start over. I wonder what's wrong?

Some notes:
I use the 2.6.15-27-686 kernel on Dapper.
I believe Load "glcore" is absent the unaltered config file.
System is a Celeron 1.2 S370.

Thanks.

EDIT: Will try installing the Linux-686 package to see if that'll help supply headers/kernel modules for the driver. Maybe that's what's missing. Now that I've read in depth Tseliot's postings on how to install drivers, I'm sure I'm not doing it right, and will find the right way later on.

### Post by davidvasta on 2007-01-21
Worked for me like a charm. Nice work. I am all about "EASY".

ubuntu 6.10

I have a Athlon 2600 with an Nvidia 6800 EFX 256MB Card AGP. It installed and came back to life and everything seems like it's working . Thanks!

### Post by chinocracy on 2007-01-23
As of this time, I think I got the 7174 driver to work. 

What I did is to manually copy /lib/modules/2.6.15-27-686/volatile/nvidia.ko
to:
/lib/modules/2.6.15-27-686/kernel/drivers/video/nvidia.ko

That did it.
